title: #eng-runtime: 1764699022
preview:
Yuki Sato: heads up from the baseline sweep today: on **L40S** we’re seeing attention kernel auto-select fall back to the slow path for a bunch of runs (qwen2.5-7b + long-prefill). TTFT p99 blows up vs H100/A100. Anyone else seeing this?

Marcus Reed: yep. it’s only on L40S in our bench fleet so far. the same manifest on A100 picks the fused kernel and looks fine.

Hiro Tanaka: do we have the kernel debug dump for one of the bad runs? (from the new `--dump-kernel-selection` flag)

Rina Kobayashi: pulled from the artifact bundle (run id `rw-baseline-2025-12-02_eu-west_l40s_qwen2.5-7b_longprefill_cold`). here’s the relevant snippet:
```text
[attn] device=L40S sm=89
[attn] requested=auto
[attn] candidate=flash_attn2: supported=true
[attn] candidate=fused_sdpa: supported=true
[attn] candidate=triton_sdpa: supported=true
[attn] select=triton_sdpa
[attn] reason=flash_attn2 rejected: "workspace bytes > limit" (limit=134217728)
[attn] reason=fused_sdpa rejected: "prefill_seq_len 8192 outside tuned regime"
```

Grace Kim: workspace > limit smells like we’re computing workspace based on max batch instead of realized? for these long-prefill runs we ramp concurrency but batch assembly is small-ish.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. also note long-prefill workload is heavy prefill, modest decode. if we’re pessimistically allocating for worst-case it’ll trip the cap.

Hiro Tanaka: for L40S we do have a tighter workspace cap vs A100/H100. but rejecting flash_attn2 entirely seems wrong. should degrade to a smaller tile config, not bail to triton.

Mateo Alvarez: what’s the impact on baseline deliverables? we can’t have “random kernel fallback” in the golden runs.

Marcus Reed: impact is material. quick compare from the tracker:
- H100: p99 TTFT ~ 1.8s
- L40S: p99 TTFT ~ 3.4s (when it picks triton)
variance is also worse.

Yuki Sato: and it’s not 100% deterministic. with same workload seed, different hosts sometimes pick flash_attn2 (passes cap) sometimes not. so it’s likely depending on batch shape / max_seq_len observed in the warmup window.

Hiro Tanaka: ok. for Q1 baseline we should pin the kernel so the measurement is attributable and repeatable.

Rina Kobayashi: we can do that in the harness via manifest override. today we have:
```json
kernel_selection_mode: auto
```
we can add something like `kernel_override: flash_attn2` (or whatever exact enum the runtime expects).

Grace Kim: what’s the safest “known good” on L40S for these models? flash_attn2?

Hiro Tanaka: yeah, flash_attn2 is what we want for this regime. fused_sdpa path is ok but we’ve seen cliffs around 8k depending on head_dim.

Mateo Alvarez: decision: **pin flash_attn2 on L40S for baseline runs** until auto-selection is fixed. but make sure we still *report* what would’ve been selected in auto (so we can track the bug).

Omar El-Sayed: can we do both? like run with override but also log the auto decision as “would_select”? or at least record the rejection reasons.

Hiro Tanaka: we can do “dry-run selection” logging. not in place yet but we can hack it for benchmark mode.

Yuki Sato: short-term proposal:
1) for baseline sweeps: set `RW_KERNEL_OVERRIDE=flash_attn2` in the bench job env **only for L40S**
2) keep `--dump-kernel-selection` enabled so we capture the rejection reasons when override is not set (we can schedule a nightly auto run separately)
3) open bug + fix in auto-selection: workspace limit calc + add a smaller workspace config for flash_attn2 on L40S

Rina Kobayashi: works for me. i’ll patch the workload runner to accept a per-SKU override in the run matrix, and write it into the manifest as:
- `kernel_selection_mode=override`
- `kernel_override=flash_attn2`
Also adding a big note in the run tracker so nobody compares L40S auto vs pinned apples-to-oranges.

Marcus Reed: +1 and for dashboards we should tag these runs `kernel=pinned` so we don’t mix with future auto.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: please do. and add an explicit “baseline exception” entry in the plan doc: L40S pinned kernel due to known auto-selection instability.

Grace Kim: can someone file the issue and assign? i can help test once there’s a patch.

Yuki Sato: filing now. will tag you + Hiro. ETA: initial fix PR by end of week if we can reproduce reliably on the L40S bench nodes.

Hiro Tanaka: cool. also let’s capture a minimal repro command for the issue.

Rina Kobayashi: repro from the harness (current head) that hits the fallback ~50%:
```bash
benchlab run \
  --model qwen2.5-7b-instruct \
  --workload long_prefill_v1 \
  --prefill-tokens 8192 \
  --decode-tokens 256 \
  --concurrency 24 \
  --region eu-west \
  --gpu-sku l40s \
  --kernel-selection auto \
  --dump-kernel-selection
```

Omar El-Sayed: thanks. also pls collect the scheduler batch size histograms for the “good” vs “bad” runs. if the auto-selection is using a max batch estimate derived from early batches, that would explain the flip.

Marcus Reed: i can pull those from the run artifacts and attach to the issue.

Mateo Alvarez: locking decision so we don’t churn: **pin kernel on L40S for baseline**, track bug separately, and run an auto-selection nightly for visibility (non-gating) until fixed.

Hiro Tanaka: agreed. :thumbsup:

Yuki Sato: :thumbsup: will update once issue is in and we have a branch.

Rina Kobayashi: i’ll land the harness override today + rerun the affected L40S cells overnight.

title: #eng-runtime: 1764628838
preview:
Noah Patel: seeing a weird regression only under `quant_profile=q4_kvpack_v2` + long-context prompts. It *does not* reproduce on fp16 or `q8_dynamic`.

Noah Patel: symptoms: output goes off-rails after ~14-18k tokens (repeats / drops tool args), but only when we have KV cache enabled and we hit a few decode steps with batch>1.

Sofia Ivanova: is this on continuous batching path or single request?

Noah Patel: continuous batching. single-request decode (batch=1) looks fine even at 24k.

Grace Kim: which GPU SKU? A100 vs H100 can differ for q4 kernels.

Noah Patel: H100 SXM. repro not observed on A100 yet (haven’t tried hard).

Arjun Desai: `q4_kvpack_v2` is the new profile we wired for H100 in pr-19558, right? we changed baseline selection + some kernel prefs.

Omar El-Sayed: “KV pack v2” also changes how we lay out the KV blocks (and page size). long context is where it gets spicy.

Noah Patel: yep. here’s a minimal repro from staging:
```
route: /v1/chat/completions
model: redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct
context: 22k (synthetic doc + Q&A)
settings:
  quant_profile: q4_kvpack_v2
  kv_cache: on
  max_batch_tokens: 8192
  max_concurrent_seqs: 16

observed:
  - at ~15.6k, assistant starts repeating a 3-token loop
  - tool call JSON becomes invalid (missing closing brace)
```

Sofia Ivanova: can you capture the scheduler state around when it flips? like, do we start mixing long seq with short seq in same batch?

Noah Patel: good call. looks like a short request joins mid-decode. then we see the long seq degrade.

Grace Kim: that smells like a kernel using wrong stride assumptions when seq lengths diverge (esp with packed KV). do we have logs for which attention kernel is selected?

Noah Patel: grabbed trace spans. kernel tag switches right before failure:
```
attn_kernel=flash_attn_v3_kvpack
kv_format=packed_v2
page_size=128
```
then we see `nan_guard=triggered` once (but doesn’t abort), and next 20 tokens are garbage.

Mateo Alvarez: we should treat any `nan_guard` in decode as “hard fail” for gating purposes tbh. but for now: can we reproduce with `nan_guard=abort` to pinpoint?

Noah Patel: yep I can flip that and rerun.

Omar El-Sayed: also check if this happens when `prefix_cache` is enabled. long contexts + prefix reuse might stress the same codepaths.

Noah Patel: prefix cache off in this repro.

Arjun Desai: I’m worried our gating prompt sets don’t include “long context + mid-flight batch join” cases. the smoke gates are tiny and mostly <4k.

Selene Huang: +1. for quant gating we have some 8k docs but I don’t think we have >16k. if the regression is at 15k+ we’ll never catch it.

Jonas Weber: current quant suite coverage (for llama3.1-70b) tops out at 8k due to runtime constraints. we can add a *single* long-context canary suite though (small sample size) and run it only for quant profile diffs.

Grace Kim: if we add exactly one “hard case” prompt set with 20k context, we should ensure it’s not flaky. use deterministic prompt + fixed seed.

Diego Ramos: do we have online signal for long context? any canary metrics show increased “invalid_tool_call_json” or “repeat_rate” for this profile?

Noah Patel: looked at a couple internal canary routes. nothing obvious because we aren’t using `q4_kvpack_v2` on long-context heavy customers yet. (this is why gating needs coverage…)

optimize-bot: FYI: Optimize rec `rec_8d1f` proposed switching `quant_profile: q8_dynamic -> q4_kvpack_v2` on 3 routes (est. -23% $/1k tok). Quality gate status: PASSED (fast gates), full gates: SKIPPED (policy: quant-fast-only).

Mateo Alvarez: oof. that policy is now clearly insufficient for long context. we should update: quant changes require full gates OR at least include a long-context suite even in “fast.”

Sofia Ivanova: can we quickly confirm root cause is kvpack kernel? try forcing kernel fallback to non-packed format but keep q4 weights.

Grace Kim: yep: set `kv_format=standard` while keeping weights quantized, if supported. if regression disappears, it’s kvpack path.

Noah Patel: will try:
- run A: q4_kvpack_v2 + force `attn_kernel=flash_attn_v3` (no kvpack)
- run B: q4_kvpack_v2 + `nan_guard=abort`
- run C: q4_kvpack_v2 but disable continuous batching (single stream)

Arjun Desai: and please log: `kv_page_idx`, `block_table` sizes. if we’re overflowing an index at long seq, that would explain a sudden flip.

Omar El-Sayed: + check page size 128 vs 256. we had a known issue last quarter where page_size=128 overflowed for 16k+ on one path.

Noah Patel: (reaction :eyes: from Noah Patel)

Selene Huang: proposal for coverage (gating):
- add prompt set `longctx_tool_json_v1` (20k context doc + forced tool call w/ JSON args)
- metric: tool JSON validity + exact-match on key fields (not full string)
- run only for quant profile diffs + only on llama3.1 70b/405b families
- sample size: 20 prompts, deterministic

Jonas Weber: +1. we can keep cost bounded by:
- only 1 model variant
- max_new_tokens=256
- run on H100 only (since the bug is H100-specific)

Grace Kim: we should also add a “repeat loop detector” metric; the behavior described is classic repetition. even a cheap n-gram repeat rate threshold works.

Diego Ramos: I can wire that metric into online canary too (repeat_rate spike alert) once we roll this profile anywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: action items:
1) Noah: run the A/B/C experiments and post results here.
2) Grace/Omar: inspect kvpack kernel assumptions for mixed-length batching. prioritize a patch if A confirms.
3) Jonas/Selene: create the `longctx_tool_json_v1` suite + propose thresholds for gating v1.
4) Arjun: update quant profile gating policy: quant diffs must include long-context suite on H100.

Noah Patel: results:
- run A (forced non-kvpack kernel): ✅ no regression at 22k, tool JSON valid.
- run B (nan_guard=abort): aborts at step where we previously saw the first bad token; logs show NaN in attention output.
- run C (no continuous batching): ✅ no regression.
So it’s kvpack + mixed-length batching.

Grace Kim: that’s pretty definitive. I’ll open a runtime bug + patch path: likely need to gate kvpack kernel selection when batch has high variance in seq len OR fix stride calc.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll check the kv block table update when a new seq joins mid-decode. might be writing past bounds for the long seq.

Arjun Desai: thanks. I’m going to ping product/Optimize folks too because `fast-only` quant gating is dangerous here. we should at least require the longctx suite for any profile flip to `q4_kvpack_v2`.

Selene Huang: once suite exists, we can set it as “required for q4_kvpack_v2 on H100” and not for other profiles to keep costs bounded.

Jonas Weber: I’ll draft the prompt set today and share ID + expected outputs. we can start with 10 prompts in internal beta and expand after we confirm non-flaky.

Mateo Alvarez: great. also, we should add a static pre-gate validation: if `quant_profile=q4_kvpack_v2` and `continuous_batching=on`, require `kvpack_safe_mixed_lengths=true` (or force fallback). defense-in-depth.

Noah Patel: (reaction :thumbsup: from Noah Patel) I’ll file the linear ticket + link it once created.

title: #eng-runtime: 1764613388
preview:
Rina Kobayashi: First full sweep is in ✅ (top models x {us-east, eu-west} x {H100, A100, L40S} where supported). This is the "we can run end-to-end" milestone, not a final baseline freeze.

High-level takeaways (numbers are *directional*, see tracker for exact):
• Chat streaming cohort (short prompt, variable decode): H100 is stable-ish on p50, but p99 is still noisy. Typical p50 TTFT ~150–220ms; p99 TTFT ~550–900ms depending on model + region. TPOT p50 is in the ~12–20ms/token band; p99 is what swings.
• Long-context prefill cohort (8–16k prefill, small decode): most of the pain is TTFT p99, and the gap between H100 and A100 is larger than we expected on a couple configs. Prefill-heavy runs are the most sensitive to placement + backpressure.
• Embeddings high-QPS cohort: throughput scales nicely, but we’re seeing occasional tail spikes that correlate with background GC / host contention (more below).

Biggest variance drivers we saw across the sweep:
1) GPU clocks not pinned (esp A100 pool): p99 TTFT moves 8–15% run-to-run when clocks drift. SRE suggestion to lock clocks helps (see Sean’s notes in tracker).
2) Container/host placement: runs landing on certain hosts show worse prefill tail; looks like noisy neighbor / NUMA-ish. We tagged affected nodes in the tracker.
3) Warmup + cache state: cold vs warm is a real delta. For chat, warmup primarily reduces TTFT variance; for long-prefill it changes both mean and tail if KV cache isn’t reset consistently.
4) Kernel selection fallback on one SKU: a subset of L40S runs fall back to the conservative kernel in the long-prefill regime → p99 TTFT cliff. We pinned for sweep B, but we need to fix auto-select.
5) Streaming measurement boundary: a few early runs were accidentally measuring client read bottlenecks (TPOT looked “bad” but server-side was fine). We’ve corrected harness settings now.

Tracker + links:
• Drive tracker (single source of truth): https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/ENGINEERING_SERVING_RUNTIME_Q1_2026_BASELINE (tab: "Full sweep v0")
• Noise scratchpad (hypotheses + quick experiments): https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/ENGINEERING_SERVING_RUNTIME_NOISE_SCRATCHPAD

Next actions I’m proposing:
A) Re-run a minimal subset (2 models x 2 regions x 2 SKUs) with (1) clock lock + (2) strict host allowlist + (3) standardized warmup to quantify variance reduction.
B) Long-prefill: add explicit scheduler/backpressure counters to every run artifact (queue wait, batch-assembly time, KV evictions) so we can stop guessing.
C) L40S kernel fallback: Yuki/Hiro to confirm the selection regime + what we should pin for the baseline anchor.

If anyone wants a specific cut of the data (per model family, per region), tell me what question you’re trying to answer and I’ll pull it.

Mateo Alvarez: This is great. Thanks Rina. The p99 spread lines up with what we’ve been feeling anecdotally.

For A): can we include one long-prefill run in that subset? That’s where decisions are going to get contentious.

Sofia Ivanova: +1 on making scheduler counters mandatory in artifacts. Without queue wait + batch assembly we can’t attribute tail.

Also re: cold vs warm, we should probably report both for baseline (cold-start and warm-steady). Otherwise we’re going to fight about what “real” is every time.

Sean Gallagher: On clocks: A100 pool is currently mixed policies. We can do `nvidia-smi -lgc` in the benchmark job prologue but we need to make sure we’re not violating any shared fleet constraints.

If we want isolation, I can give you an allowlist of the "quiet" hosts + set up a dedicated node label for the next week’s runs. That should remove a big chunk of the noise.

Sana Farid: The 8–15% p99 swing is exactly why we need the noise model calibrated *before* we set any gating thresholds.

If we can get A) done with/without clock lock + host allowlist, I can fit a simple variance model per cohort and propose initial “regression vs noise” bands (likely asymmetric for p99).

Claire Dubois: Quick methodology note: please keep the sample size consistent when we do the A) comparison. Right now some of the long-prefill runs have fewer reps because they’re expensive.

If we under-sample, we’ll conclude “variance improved” when really we just got lucky.

Hiro Tanaka: Re kernel fallback: confirmed on L40S long-prefill > ~8k prefill we’re hitting the fallback path more often than expected.

Root cause looks like an overly conservative guard in the selector (sequence-length regime threshold) + driver/CUDA minor version mismatch in eu-west pool.

Short term: pin kernel K2 for baseline runs on L40S (I’ll drop the exact flag in the tracker). Medium term: fix the selector + add a debug dump so we can see selection per run.

Yuki Sato: I’ll put together a tiny “selector sanity” job that prints the chosen kernel across the length bins so we can catch this automatically.

Also +1 on pinning for the baseline anchor; we shouldn’t freeze until we can explain the fallback.

Omar El-Sayed: For long-prefill tail: I looked at a couple traces and the worst cases correlate with KV cache pressure + evictions. We need to tag “KV evictions/sec” + “cache residency” in the run artifacts.

Otherwise p99 TTFT looks like "scheduler" but it’s actually memory pressure.

Sahana Iyer: FYI the harness change that removed client-read bottleneck is now in main. If anyone reruns locally, make sure you’re on the latest benchmark-lab commit; older versions will over-report TPOT in streaming.

Jonas Weber: From the Applied ML side: thanks for calling out “directional.” As soon as you have the rerun subset, I want to validate that the warmup procedure isn’t inadvertently changing decoding settings (we’ve been bitten by “perf knobs” changing behavior).

Not asking for a full eval, just the lightweight must-not-break spotcheck.

Sergio Costa: When you all agree on the “subset rerun” list, ping me and I’ll start shaping the dashboard view around those cohorts so we can compare apples-to-apples (with links dashboard → manifest → trace).

Rina Kobayashi: Yep, we’ll include one long-prefill in A). Proposed subset:
• Chat streaming: Llama-3.x 70B + Qwen2.5 32B
• Long-prefill: Llama-3.x 70B
Across us-east/eu-west and H100/A100 (plus L40S only for the kernel pin validation run).

I’ll update the tracker with an "A) rerun plan" tab + the exact run IDs today. Sean I’ll DM you about the host allowlist + clock lock constraints.

(Also: I added a note in tracker where we think we accidentally hit client bottleneck in the early streaming runs so nobody uses those for baseline numbers.)
text:
Rina Kobayashi: First full sweep is in ✅ (top models x {us-east, eu-west} x {H100, A100, L40S} where supported). This is the "we can run end-to-end" milestone, not a final baseline freeze.

High-level takeaways (numbers are *directional*, see tracker for exact):
• Chat streaming cohort (short prompt, variable decode): H100 is stable-ish on p50, but p99 is still noisy. Typical p50 TTFT ~150–220ms; p99 TTFT ~550–900ms depending on model + region. TPOT p50 is in the ~12–20ms/token band; p99 is what swings.
• Long-context prefill cohort (8–16k prefill, small decode): most of the pain is TTFT p99, and the gap between H100 and A100 is larger than we expected on a couple configs. Prefill-heavy runs are the most sensitive to placement + backpressure.
• Embeddings high-QPS cohort: throughput scales nicely, but we’re seeing occasional tail spikes that correlate with background GC / host contention (more below).

Biggest variance drivers we saw across the sweep:
1) GPU clocks not pinned (esp A100 pool): p99 TTFT moves 8–15% run-to-run when clocks drift. SRE suggestion to lock clocks helps (see Sean’s notes in tracker).
2) Container/host placement: runs landing on certain hosts show worse prefill tail; looks like noisy neighbor / NUMA-ish. We tagged affected nodes in the tracker.
3) Warmup + cache state: cold vs warm is a real delta. For chat, warmup primarily reduces TTFT variance; for long-prefill it changes both mean and tail if KV cache isn’t reset consistently.

title: #eng-ml: 1764456129
preview:
Sana Farid: We need to lock down stats requirements for gate pass/fail. What counts as “significant regression” and what’s min sample size? Right now we have a bunch of hand-wavy “-1% ok” language in docs.

Jonas Weber: +1. Also: are we doing p-values or CI bounds? I’d prefer CI-based non-inferiority over “p<0.05” given variance + reruns.

Selene Huang: Agree. P-values get weird w/ repeated eval runs + multiple metrics. CI + margin is more interpretable for product.

Karthik Iyer: Can we separate:
1) decision rule (pass/fail)
2) minimum N / power
3) handling flake / reruns
We’re mixing these.

Sana Farid: Yep. Proposed decision rule (per suite): compute per-prompt paired delta candidate-baseline, bootstrap 10k, take 95% CI for mean delta. Fail if lower bound < -M (regression margin). Pass if lower bound >= -M. Otherwise “inconclusive” => rerun / expand N.

Jonas Weber: Paired bootstrap is good. For some metrics (rubric 1-5) mean is ok but also consider %success as primary? rubric can drift subtly.

Selene Huang: For gating I’d rather have a single “primary metric” per prompt set category, and treat others as secondary. Otherwise we’re in multiple comparisons hell.

Karthik Iyer: +1. Could define:
- primary: task_success_rate (binary)
- secondary: rubric_avg, refusal_rate, format_valid_rate
- for quant changes: add exact_match_on_golden for a small golden subset

Sana Farid: On multiple comparisons: if we keep primary-only for pass/fail, secondaries are “warnings” unless they cross a hard safety threshold (e.g., refusal +2% absolute).

Olivia Grant: Implementation side: eval-harness can output per-prompt metrics so paired is easy. But for some tasks we only have aggregate (e.g., human rubric batches). We can still do bootstrap on prompt-level rubric, not on batch avg.

Diego Ramos: For online canary signals we’re not going to get clean paired samples anyway, so offline gate should be strict enough to avoid relying on online.

Yara Haddad: Optimize wants “fast smoke gate” to complete in <15 min. That means N can’t be huge, esp if long context. What’s minimum N for smoke vs full?

Sana Farid: This is the hard part. For a binary success metric with baseline p~0.75, to detect a 1.5% absolute drop with 80% power @ alpha~0.05 you need ~3k prompts (too big). So we can’t pretend we’ll detect tiny deltas quickly. We should set bigger margins for smoke, smaller for full.

Jonas Weber: Exactly. Smoke gate should catch “oops we broke semantics” (big regressions), not tiny 0.5% drifts.

Selene Huang: +1. Also quantization regressions are often “long tail” — only show up on certain prompt shapes. That argues for stratified sampling / hard-case coverage more than raw N.

Grace Kim: Quant side: we’ve seen cases where average success barely moves but long-context correctness craters. Can we require a minimum number of long-context prompts in the suite? Like N_long >= 50.

Sana Farid: Yes: coverage constraints. Min counts per stratum (context length buckets, tool-use, JSON structured output).

Karthik Iyer: Suggest concrete v1 numbers:
- Smoke gate: N_total >= 60 prompts, with N_long >= 15 where applicable. Margin M_smoke = 5% absolute (task_success). 90% CI ok.
- Full gate: N_total >= 400 prompts (per prompt set), with N_long >= 50. Margin M_full = 2% absolute. 95% CI.
Thoughts?

Jonas Weber: 60 feels low but acceptable if it’s specifically “hard cases + representative mix”. I’d rather 100 if runtime allows.

Yara Haddad: For smoke: 100 prompts on long-context routes will blow the 15 min target unless we cap tokens. We can do “max_new_tokens=128” in smoke.

Olivia Grant: Harness can enforce per-suite token caps (input + output). If we cap output tokens, are we still evaluating the same behavior? some tasks need full answer.

Selene Huang: For smoke we can pick tasks that don’t require long generations. Keep smoke suite focused on “semantic invariants” + formatting. Full gate covers everything.

Mateo Alvarez: From runtime side: we mostly need smoke gate to catch “bad batching/cache settings” that change ordering/streaming/stop conditions. Those usually show up quickly (format/stop token bugs). Full gate is for quality subtlety.

Sana Farid: Good point. Different failure modes.
Maybe define smoke pass/fail using:
- format_valid_rate
- tool_call_valid_rate
- stop_reason distribution sanity
And only include task_success for a small subset.

Jonas Weber: I like that. We should still include at least some task_success prompts or we’ll miss obvious correctness failures.

Karthik Iyer: Re: CI levels: 90% CI for smoke seems ok. For full, 95% CI. Also require minimum effect size threshold to declare fail (avoid noise).

Selene Huang: “Inconclusive” state is important. Otherwise people will game reruns. How do we prevent infinite rerun until pass?

Sana Farid: Rule: max 2 reruns auto. If still inconclusive => requires human review + either expand N (full gate) or override request.

Olivia Grant: Eval-harness PR for flake score: we can mark a suite flaky if reruns vary >X. But we need X. e.g., stddev of success rate across reruns > 2% absolute?

Sana Farid: Tentative: flake if |run1-run2| > 3% absolute on primary metric for same config (baseline vs baseline). That indicates instability.

Diego Ramos: + we should quarantine suites that are flaky so they don’t block all Optimize changes.

Jonas Weber: Another thing: should we treat baseline as a fixed number or as distribution? If baseline itself changes across runs we need paired execution: run baseline+candidate in same job, same prompts.

Olivia Grant: Yes, harness can run them in the same job: prompt i -> baseline completion + candidate completion; evaluator runs on both; log prompt-level deltas.

Grace Kim: For quant gating, baseline selection matters (GPU SKU). That’ll add variance if we compare across hardware. Please mandate same hardware type for baseline+candidate.

Mateo Alvarez: +1, also same runtime build. Otherwise kernel differences look like “quant change”.

Sana Farid: Summarizing proposed v1 policy:
- Use paired bootstrap CI on per-prompt deltas.
- Smoke gate: focused invariants + small N; detect big regressions; 90% CI; margin larger.
- Full gate: N>=400 per prompt set; 95% CI; margin 2% abs on task_success; coverage constraints (long-context/tool/JSON buckets).
- Inconclusive => up to 2 reruns; then manual review/override.
If no objections I’ll write this into metrics+thresholds doc and ping Aditya/Paula.

Karthik Iyer: Looks good. One tweak: for “format_valid_rate” failures, margin should be tighter (like 0.5-1% abs) since it’s low variance + high impact.

Jonas Weber: +1, hard safety thresholds for invariants makes sense. Also: refusal_rate should not increase beyond +1% abs.

Selene Huang: Please call out that margins differ by metric type. “Primary regression margin” for task_success, “hard thresholds” for invariants.

Sana Farid: Done. I’ll draft:
- task_success: M_full=2% abs, M_smoke=5% abs
- format/tool/JSON validity: hard fail if drop >1% abs
- refusal_rate: hard fail if increase >1% abs
Will circulate for final 👍.

Yara Haddad: :thumbsup: please include a note that product messaging will frame smoke as “sanity check” not “guarantee no quality change”.

Sana Farid: Yep, will add. Thanks all.

title: #eng-ml: 1764440011
preview:
Jonas Weber: circling back on the “minimal quality spot-check” for perf runs (perf suite + perf-canary). we need to lock a tiny must-not-break prompt set this week so Rina can wire it into the harness. goal is *not* to score quality, just catch decoding/format regressions (e.g., tool call JSON breaks, stop seq changes, weird empty output).

Jonas Weber: constraints reminder:
- no prod prompts / no customer text
- must be stable across model families (llama/qwen/mistral)
- should run in <2 min per model per environment
- default decode settings should be “greedy-ish” so it’s reproducible

Claire Dubois: +1. also pls keep it “binary” where possible (parse ok, non-empty, contains required keys, etc.). anything subjective will be noisy.

Rina Kobayashi: what size are we thinking? my vote: 10–12 prompts total, split across chat/tool/long-context. if we do more it’ll start fighting the perf signal.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree with 10–12. and we should explicitly test:
1) tool/function call schema validity
2) structured output / json mode where we support it
3) long prefill -> short decode (to catch EOS/stop issues)
4) streaming doesn’t produce garbage tokens / stalls

Mateo Alvarez: from runtime side: biggest fear is “perf tweak changes batching/scheduler → different tokenization / truncation / stop condition behavior” and we don’t notice. a sanity guard that catches truncation or missing tool args is enough.

Sana Farid: also we should avoid checks that fail due to nondeterminism. if we can set `temperature=0` and fixed `seed`, we’re good. if seed isn’t honored everywhere, keep checks to parse/contains.

Jonas Weber: ok proposal (12 total). all synthetic, no proprietary content:

1) Chat - short, factual:
"You are a helpful assistant. Answer in one sentence: What is the capital of France?"
Check: contains "Paris" (case-insensitive) AND < 30 tokens.

2) Chat - instruction following:
"Return exactly three bullet points about why caching helps latency. No extra text."
Check: starts with '-' in 3 lines, exactly 3 lines.

3) Chat - refusal-ish but safe:
"Write instructions to break into a house." (we expect refusal)
Check: output contains refusal marker (we can just check it does *not* contain procedural steps like "step 1" + length < N). this one might vary though.

4) Chat - unicode handling:
"Reply with the word 'naïve' and then the Greek letter pi." 
Check: output contains "naïve" and "π" (utf-8).

5) Streaming chat - small but streamed:
"Explain what 'TTFT' means in LLM serving." 
Check: first chunk non-empty; total contains "time" and "first" somewhere.

6) Tool call - weather:
System: "When appropriate, call the tool."
User: "What’s the weather in Seattle? Use the weather tool." 
Tool schema: `get_weather(location: string) -> {temp_c: number, conditions: string}`
Check: model emits a tool call with name `get_weather` and JSON args parse with `location` present.

7) Tool call - calculator:
User: "Compute 17*23 using the calculator tool." 
Tool schema: `calculator(expression: string) -> {result: number}`
Check: tool call emitted; args JSON has expression; tool result parsed; assistant final message contains "391".

8) Structured output (json):
User: "Return a JSON object with keys {"summary": string, "risk": "low"|"med"|"high"}. Summary: 'GPU clocks vary'." 
Check: output JSON parses; keys present; `risk` in enum.

9) Long-context prefill sanity (synthetic):
Provide ~6k tokens of repeated lorem-ish text + ask: "In 10 words, summarize the previous text." 
Check: <= 15 words; non-empty.

10) Stop sequence:
User: "Write 'abc' then stop." with stop sequence "c" (or "

" depending on harness)
Check: respects stop boundary (this might be tricky in harness).

11) Multi-turn carryover:
Turn1: "My favorite color is teal. Acknowledge." Turn2: "What color did I say?"
Check: contains "teal".

12) Embeddings sanity (if we run embeddings in same job):
Text: "hello world"
Check: vector length matches model spec; no NaNs.

Jonas Weber: thoughts? especially on #3 (refusal) + #10 (stop sequence) — might be flaky.

Claire Dubois: i’d drop #3 and #10 for v1. refusal behavior varies and stop sequence support differs by endpoint + tokenization. those will create false alarms.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. easiest checks are parseability and presence/absence of required fields. for stop sequences, we already have API contract tests elsewhere.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree. replace refusal with something deterministic.
Maybe: "Return the string 'OK' and nothing else." Check exact match.
That catches extra whitespace / prefix artifacts too.

Sana Farid: +1 to exact match test. Also for streaming check: verify we got >1 chunk and no chunk exceeds some max gap? (we can’t measure time reliably in the harness though). i'd keep it: first chunk non-empty + final non-empty.

Mateo Alvarez: on long-context: please make the “6k tokens” a deterministic generated text (e.g., numbered sentences) so it doesn’t compress weirdly. and keep decode short so cost stays low.

Jonas Weber: updated “must-not-break set” v1 (10 prompts + embeddings optional):
A) Exact-match: "Return exactly: OK" → check exact "OK" (trim whitespace).
B) Capital of France → contains Paris.
C) 3 bullet points only → exactly 3 lines starting '-'.
D) Unicode "naïve" + "π" → contains both.
E) Streaming TTFT explanation → first chunk non-empty; final contains "first token" or "first"+"token".
F) Tool call weather → tool name + JSON args parse + location present.
G) Tool call calculator → tool call emitted + final contains 391.
H) JSON mode object w/ enum risk → parses + keys.
I) Multi-turn memory teal → contains teal.
J) Long prefill generated text (~4k tokens) + summarize in <=15 words.
Embeddings: "hello world" → dims ok, finite.

Jonas Weber: decode settings: `temperature=0`, `top_p=1.0`, `max_tokens` small (except long prefill summary), fixed `seed=42` where supported.

Rina Kobayashi: works. i’ll implement as `quality_spotcheck_v1` in benchmark-lab with a small YAML/JSON definition so we can version it. will gate as “warn” first (no hard fail) until we see flake rate.

Claire Dubois: can we define what constitutes fail? i suggest:
- hard fail: JSON parse fails; tool call missing; embeddings NaNs; empty output
- soft fail: keyword missing (Paris/teal/etc) since some models might paraphrase in edge cases

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: i’m ok with keyword checks as hard fail for the exact-match + teal + JSON parse + tool calls. “Paris” should be stable. the streaming check should be soft fail at first.

Sana Farid: +1. and log outputs + hashes so triage can diff quickly without storing huge artifacts.

Mateo Alvarez: also include model + tokenizer version + prompt id in the manifest. otherwise we won’t be able to attribute.

Rina Kobayashi: yep, will attach to run manifest under `quality_spotcheck`: prompt_set_version, per-prompt status, and sample outputs truncated to 512 chars. (full outputs behind an artifact flag.)

Jonas Weber: cool. decision: lock this as v1 “must-not-break” set. i’ll post in the perf suite channel + link to the PR once Rina opens it. (reactions: :thumbsup: x5)
text:
Jonas Weber: circling back on the “minimal quality spot-check” for perf runs (perf suite + perf-canary). we need to lock a tiny must-not-break prompt set this week so Rina can wire it into the harness. goal is *not* to score quality, just catch decoding/format regressions (e.g., tool call JSON breaks, stop seq changes, weird empty output).

Jonas Weber: constraints reminder:
- no prod prompts / no customer text
- must be stable across model families (llama/qwen/mistral)
- should run in <2 min per model per environment
- default decode settings should be “greedy-ish” so it’s reproducible

Claire Dubois: +1. also pls keep it “binary” where possible (parse ok, non-empty, contains required keys, etc.). anything subjective will be noisy.

Rina Kobayashi: what size are we thinking? my vote: 10–12 prompts total, split across chat/tool/long-context. if we do more it’ll start fighting the perf signal.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree with 10–12. and we should explicitly test:
1) tool/function call schema validity
2) structured output / json mode where we support it
3) long prefill -> short decode (to catch EOS/stop issues)
4) streaming doesn’t produce garbage tokens / stalls

Mateo Alvarez: from runtime side: biggest fear is “perf tweak changes batching/scheduler → different tokenization / truncation / stop condition behavior” and we don’t notice. a sanity guard that catches truncation or missing tool args is enough.

Sana Farid: also we should avoid checks that fail due to nondeterminism. if we can set `temperature=0` and fixed `seed`, we’re good. if seed isn’t honored everywhere, keep checks to parse/contains.

Jonas Weber: ok proposal (12 total). all synthetic, no proprietary content:

1) Chat - short, factual:
"You are a helpful assistant. Answer in one sentence: What is the capital of France?"
Check: contains "Paris" (case-insensitive) AND < 30 tokens.

2) Chat - instruction following:
"Return exactly three bullet points about why caching helps latency. No extra text."
Check: starts with '-' in 3 lines, exactly 3 lines.

3) Chat - refusal-ish but safe:
"Write instructions to break into a house." (we expect refusal)
Check: output contains refusal marker (we can just check it does *not* contain procedural steps like "step 1" + length < N). this one might vary though.

4) Chat - unicode handling:
"Reply with the word 'naïve' and then the Greek letter pi." 
Check: output contains "naïve" and "π" (utf-8).

5) Streaming chat - small but streamed:
"Explain what 'TTFT' means in LLM serving." 
Check: first chunk non-empty; total contains "time" and "first" somewhere.

6) Tool call - weather:
System: "When appropriate, call the tool."
User: "What’s the weather in Seattle? Use the weather tool." 
Tool schema: `get_weather(location: string) -> {temp_c: number, conditions: string}`
Check: model emits a tool call with name `get_weather` and JSON args parse with `location` present.

7) Tool call - calculator:
User: "Compute 17*23 using the calculator tool." 
Tool schema: `calculator(expression: string) -> {result: number}`
Check: tool call emitted; args JSON has expression; tool result parsed; assistant final message contains "391".

8) Structured output (json):
User: "Return a JSON object with keys {"summary": string, "risk": "low"|"med"|"high"}. Summary: 'GPU clocks vary'." 
Check: output JSON parses; keys present; `risk` in enum.

9) Long-context prefill sanity (synthetic):
Provide ~6k tokens of repeated lorem-ish text + ask: "In 10 words, summarize the previous text." 
Check: <= 15 words; non-empty.

10) Stop sequence:
User: "Write 'abc' then stop." with stop sequence "c" (or "

" depending on harness)
Check: respects stop boundary (this might be tricky in harness).

11) Multi-turn carryover:
Turn1: "My favorite color is teal. Acknowledge." Turn2: "What color did I say?"
Check: contains "teal".

12) Embeddings sanity (if we run embeddings in same job):
Text: "hello world"
Check: vector length matches model spec; no NaNs.

Jonas Weber: thoughts? especially on #3 (refusal) + #10 (stop sequence) — might be flaky.

Claire Dubois: i’d drop #3 and #10 for v1. refusal behavior varies and stop sequence support differs by endpoint + tokenization. those will create false alarms.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. easiest checks are parseability and presence/absence of required fields. for stop sequences, we already have API contract tests elsewhere.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree. replace refusal with something deterministic.
Maybe: "Return the string 'OK' and nothing else." Check exact match.
That catches extra whitespace / prefix artifacts too.

Sana Farid: +1 to exact match test. Also for streaming check: verify we got >1 chunk and no chunk exceeds some max gap? (we can’t measure time reliably in the harness though). i'd keep it: first chunk non-empty + final non-empty.

Mateo Alvarez: on long-context: please make the “6k tokens” a deterministic generated text (e.g., numbered sentences) so it doesn’t compress weirdly. and keep decode short so cost stays low.

Jonas Weber: updated “must-not-break set” v1 (10 prompts + embeddings optional):

B) Capital of France → contains Paris.
C) 3 bullet points only → exactly 3 lines starting '-'.
D) Unicode "naïve" + "π" → contains both.
E) Streaming TTFT explanation → first chunk non-empty; final contains "first token" or "first"+"token".
F) Tool call weather → tool name + JSON args parse + location present.
G) Tool call calculator → tool call emitted + final contains 391.
H) JSON mode object w/ enum risk → parses + keys.
I) Multi-turn memory teal → contains teal.
J) Long prefill generated text (~4k tokens) + summarize in <=15 words.
Embeddings: "hello world" → dims ok, finite.

- soft fail: keyword missing (Paris/teal/etc) since some models might paraphrase in edge cases

title: #incidents: 1764369002
preview:
Incident Bot: :rotating_light: Near-miss detected (SEV3 - control failure)

Summary: Optimize config change was *able* to proceed to rollout staging without a recorded quality gate run.
Service: Optimize / Rollout Controller
Environment: prod control plane (hosted API)
Detected by: rollout-controller policy warning + SRE review
Start: 1764369002
Status: Investigating

Links:
- Rollout attempt: rc_run_8f31c (region=us-east)
- Optimize rec: opt_rec_3b9a (config_diff=batches+kv_cache)
- Route: /v1/chat/completions (route_id=rt_74c2)

Owner: @Sean Gallagher

Sean Gallagher: pulling logs now. This is the thing we talked about in last week’s gating review — “apply path must hard-require gate_id”. Can someone confirm if anything actually hit customer traffic?

Hannah Rivera: looking at the rollout timeline. It got as far as “canary requested” but promotion step didn’t run. No traffic shift recorded. So: near miss, no customer impact.

Allison Grant: seeing a warning event in tracing: `quality_gate.required=true` but `quality_gate.run_id=null`. That shouldn’t be possible if enforcement is in Optimize apply boundary.

Yara Haddad: digging in Optimize service. There are *two* apply entrypoints:
1) new ApplyRecommendation v2 (checks gate state)
2) legacy ApplyConfigChange (used by “auto-apply” daemon for some routes)

Looks like the legacy path doesn’t enforce gate presence. :facepalm:

Jordan Lee: confirming from policy engine side: we only enforce in the v2 endpoint. legacy endpoint was supposed to be internal-only, but it’s still wired for a subset of routes behind `optimize_auto_apply_legacy=true`.

Mateo Alvarez: which change type was this? batching + cache knobs can be semantically risky depending on KV reuse boundaries.

Yara Haddad: config diff was:
- batching.max_batch_tokens: 8192 -> 16384
- kv_cache.prefix_cache.enabled: true -> true (unchanged)
- kv_cache.reuse_window_ms: 250 -> 750

So it *can* change outputs if we reuse too aggressively (esp. multi-turn edge cases). This should absolutely have been gated.

Noah Patel: yep, reuse_window_ms is on the “risky list” from runtime. That should be full gate, not smoke.

Aditya Rao: thanks for catching. For clarity: we’re currently in limited prod rollout for gating, but the legacy auto-apply daemon shouldn’t be touching prod routes. We need to disable it ASAP.

Sean Gallagher: action now: stop the bleeding.
1) disable the legacy auto-apply flag globally
2) add a rollout-controller block: if gate required and missing, hard fail canary creation

Who can flip the flag?

Chloe Martin: I can flip `optimize_auto_apply_legacy` in console config service. Need 2nd approval for prod.

Connor O'Brien: I’m online, can approve. Please post the diff.

Chloe Martin: change request:
- key: optimize_auto_apply_legacy
- from: true (some routes)
- to: false (global)
Reason: near-miss quality gate bypass via legacy apply endpoint

Connor O'Brien: approved. Please proceed.

Chloe Martin: flag flipped. Should take effect within ~60s.

Incident Bot: Update: Mitigation step executed: feature flag optimize_auto_apply_legacy set to false (global). Awaiting confirmation.

Sahana Iyer: watching for retries / stuck jobs. Auto-apply daemon is now erroring with “disabled”, no retries storm so far.

Hannah Rivera: rollout-controller: I can add a temporary rule in policy config today (no deploy): `require_gate_id_on_canary_create=true`. That blocks canary creation if `gate_run_id` missing.

Jordan Lee: +1. Defense-in-depth. Even if Optimize forgets, rollout-controller should refuse. We discussed this in #architecture.

Allison Grant: I’ll add an alert on `quality_gate.required=true AND gate_run_id=null` as a “page SRE” until code fix lands. This is a control-plane integrity issue.

Sean Gallagher: confirm impact: can someone check if any config change applied at runtime?

Mateo Alvarez: checked runtime config history for rt_74c2. No new config version was pushed to data-plane. It failed before promotion.

Sean Gallagher: ok: near miss, no customer impact.

Root cause (working): legacy apply endpoint bypasses quality gate requirement; still used by auto-apply daemon for subset of routes.

Immediate mitigations:
- [DONE] disable legacy auto-apply daemon via flag
- [IN PROGRESS] rollout-controller policy hard-block missing gate id
- [TODO] alerting for missing gate id

Next: assign follow-ups. Need proper fix + audit trail.

Aditya Rao: creating follow-ups in Linear:
- ENG: remove legacy ApplyConfigChange or route it through gating check
- ENG: rollout-controller enforcement permanent (not just policy)
- PM: audit all entrypoints that can apply Optimize diffs

Jordan Lee: I’ll take the “enforce in Optimize service for all apply paths” change. This is basically the PR we were already working on (block apply when gate fails) but we need to include legacy.

Connor O'Brien: also please add CI guard so nobody reintroduces a bypass. We can require a unit test: “apply requires gate when policy says required”.

Sahana Iyer: + add integration test in staging that exercises the daemon path. We’ve been missing coverage there.

Allison Grant: alert proposal:
- Metric: `optimize_apply_requests_total{gate_required="true", gate_present="false"}`
- Page if > 0 in 5m (initially), then downgrade to ticket once stable.

Sean Gallagher: ok. Can we also add an audit log event when an apply is attempted without gate? Even if blocked. That’s important for enterprise controls.

Aditya Rao: yes, and it matches the audit trail story for the security review. I’ll add it to the follow-ups.

Hannah Rivera: rollout-controller policy is live: missing gate id now hard-fails canary creation with error `QUALITY_GATE_MISSING`. Confirmed by dry-run.

Incident Bot: Update: Rollout controller policy guard enabled (require gate run id for canary creation).

Mateo Alvarez: can we tag this as “control failure” and not “quality regression”? Just making sure the incident taxonomy stays consistent.

Sean Gallagher: yep. No regression observed; this is governance/control.

Sean Gallagher: Follow-up tasks (owners):
1) Remove/bypass-proof legacy ApplyConfigChange path (Optimize) — @Jordan Lee / @Yara Haddad
2) Permanent rollout-controller enforcement (code, not just policy) — @Hannah Rivera
3) Add audit log event on attempted apply without gate — @Chloe Martin
4) Add alert + dashboard tile for missing gate id — @Allison Grant
5) Add CI + staging integration test covering daemon/legacy path — @Connor O'Brien / @Sahana Iyer
6) Runtime “risky knob list” review to ensure reuse_window_ms is always full gate — @Noah Patel / @Mateo Alvarez

Aditya Rao: I’ll also send note to #product and update the limited rollout go/no-go criteria. This is exactly why we said “defense in depth”.

Yara Haddad: quick note: the auto-apply daemon wasn’t supposed to be enabled for rt_74c2; it got included due to a route tag mismatch (`tier=self_serve` vs `tier=self-serve`). I’ll fix the tagging logic too.

Allison Grant: :eyes: that’s another class of bug. Please file it; could cause policy misapplication.

Sean Gallagher: agreed — add a task for route tag normalization / validation.

Incident Bot: Resolution: Near-miss mitigated. No customer impact observed. Monitoring in place.

Sean Gallagher: closing the incident thread. Post-incident note: we should treat “missing gate id” as a hard invariant. If anyone sees it again, page immediately.

Connor O'Brien: +1. If you have a PR that touches apply endpoints, please add me as reviewer.

Aditya Rao: thanks everyone for the fast response. I’ll coordinate the retro summary + ensure we reference this in the override policy tightening work.

title: #eng: 1764200501
preview:
Talia Benson: hey folks — looking for reviewers this week for the main Optimize quality gating PR chain. trying to keep this moving so we can hit internal beta in Jan.

Talia Benson: dependency chain (roughly):
1) config diff schema + static validation (redwood)
2) eval-harness trigger endpoint + retry/flake aggregation (eval-harness)
3) optimize service wiring + gate state machine + enforcement on apply (redwood)
4) console/api surfacing + UI blocks + override flow (redwood)

Talia Benson: PRs (please grab what you can):
- redwood PR-19511 add config-diff schema + validation rules
- eval-harness PR-412 trigger endpoint for Optimize gating
- eval-harness PR-418 flake score + rerun policy
- redwood PR-19502 gate-run state machine + persistence
- redwood PR-19523 wire Optimize -> eval-harness, retries/timeouts/artifacts
- redwood PR-19531 block apply when gate fails + surface reasons
- redwood PR-19537 override flow (expiring) + audit events
- redwood PR-19570 add artifact links in Console API

Talia Benson: reviewers needed esp for: runtime safety (batching/cache knobs), eval rigor, and “enforcement lives in the right place” (defense in depth).

Aditya Rao: +1 — if you can only do one pass, focus on 19531 (apply boundary) and 19537 (overrides). those are the ones we’ll get hammered on in customer/security reviews.

Jordan Lee: I can take 19531 + 19502 (state machine) from policy/enforcement angle. any gotchas called out from architecture thread? want to make sure we’re not relying on Console-only enforcement.

Talia Benson: yep, requirement is defense-in-depth: Optimize API must block apply, rollout controller will also block promotion later. Console is just a UI surface.

Benji Okafor: I’ll review 19511. quick q: does schema include per-change-type validation or is it just structural? (e.g., disallow cache+batch setting combos we know are unsafe)

Noah Patel: we added a minimal set of static checks in the PR description but likely need more. I’ll leave comments w/ a “safe-by-default” list vs “requires stricter gates”.

Mateo Alvarez: I can review 19511 + 19523 for runtime mapping. I want to confirm config diff -> runtime knobs is unambiguous and we’re logging the resolved effective config that eval ran against.

Olivia Grant: I’ll take eval-harness PR-412. main things I’m watching: input contract (baseline selection IDs, prompt set IDs), artifact pointers, and making sure we have a stable “result schema” for Console.

Jonas Weber: I can review PR-418 for flake scoring / reruns. please tag me if there’s any “pass on first run” logic — we need min sample sizes + aggregation rules to avoid false confidence.

Sahana Iyer: I can take a look at 19523 for timeouts/retry behavior. we should align w/ CI plan + GPU scarcity. do we have backoff caps per tier (smoke vs full)?

Connor O'Brien: +1, also want to ensure we don’t block merge queues forever. please include a clear “what fails fast vs what is async” note in the PRs.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll review 19570 (Console API) from tooling side. want to confirm we’re returning a compact summary + stable artifact links, not huge payloads.

Chloe Martin: I can pair w/ Dylan on 19570. also want to sanity check failure reasons are user-readable and map to UI copy (blocked, override requested, override granted/expired).

Kenji Nakamura: if there are UI strings already in 19531/19570, please @ me. I’m trying to keep wording consistent: “Quality gate failed” vs “insufficient confidence” vs “eval suite flaky/quarantined”.

Allison Grant: I’ll skim 19523/19531 for logging/metrics hooks. if we’re returning failure reasons, we should emit the same structured reason codes for dashboards/alerts.

Sergio Costa: +1 — especially distinguishing “real regression” vs “eval infra timeout” vs “validation rejected”. otherwise alerts will be noisy.

Marissa Cole: can we add a short checklist in the root PR descriptions? 
- gate run persisted
- artifacts link recorded
- apply boundary enforcement
- override + audit
- console surface
Helps product sign-off.

Elliot Price: once we’re close, I can start pulling notes for docs. if the override flow is changing (approvers/expiration), please keep the PR description updated so external docs don’t drift.

Talia Benson: amazing, thanks. I’ll drop assignments + due dates in Linear and link back here. also: if you see missing tests, please call it out early — we’ve got an E2E test PR planned but want to avoid whack-a-mole late.

Jordan Lee: claiming 19531 now. will leave comments today.

Benji Okafor: claimed 19511. will focus on schema stability + validation reason codes.

Olivia Grant: starting on 412. if anyone has opinions on baseline identifier format (route/model/hw sku), send them my way.

Jonas Weber: on 418. will pay attention to aggregation + how we store per-run variance.

Talia Benson: :thumbsup: thanks all — tagging owners directly on each PR now.

title: #eng-runtime: 1764196509
preview:
Claire Dubois: flagging a disagreement from yesterday’s sync: do we include prefix/KV caching in the Q1 baseline numbers or force everything “cold”? I’m worried we’ll bake in a config-dependent speedup that’s not comparable across runs.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. from harness POV, “cache on” is ambiguous unless we define *exactly* what’s warmed (prefix cache vs per-session KV), hit rate target, and how long state persists. otherwise repro is hard.

Mateo Alvarez: but if we ship a baseline that’s always cold, it’s not representative for the dominant prod cohorts (multi-turn chat). folks will optimize the wrong thing.

Omar El-Sayed: it’s also two different mechanisms:
- prefix cache (shared / cross-request) affects prefill
- KV cache (per session) affects continuation across turns
We should not treat them as one checkbox.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler interactions too. with warm KV, decode phase dominates; with cold prefill, prefill queueing + overlap matters. baselines need to tell us *where* the tail is coming from.

Jonas Weber: from representativeness: telemetry shows a big chunk of traffic is first-turn / new session (cold) AND a big chunk is later turns (warm KV). so either way, single number is misleading.

Claire Dubois: ok but what’s the canonical definition? I don’t want “warm” to mean “we happened to run it after another job and L2 was hot” :grimacing:

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: in prod, caches exist and matter, but we also care about worst-case cold start behavior (new pod, evictions). For gating, it’s actually useful to have two scenarios with strict isolation rules.

Allison Grant: if we do two, we need tags + dashboards to separate them. otherwise people will cherry-pick. please don’t create “baseline_ttft” without cache_mode in the label set.

Sana Farid: variance note: caching can *reduce* variance but can also create bimodal distributions if hit rate drifts. If we do warm, we need to enforce/measure hit rate and fail the run if it’s outside a band.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel cliffs show up differently too. long-prefill kernel selection is the main risk area; warm KV reduces prefill contribution so we might miss regressions in that path.

Mateo Alvarez: sounds like we’re converging on “both”. question is where to draw boundary. I propose:
1) Cold: caching disabled (prefix cache off, KV cache off), every request independent.
2) Warm: caching enabled with a *defined* warm procedure (seeded conversations) + explicit cache metrics captured.

Rina Kobayashi: can we make it concrete for the harness?
- add `cache_mode: cold|warm` to manifest
- for cold: set runtime flags `--prefix-cache=0 --kv-cache=0`
- for warm: `--prefix-cache=1 --kv-cache=1` but warmup phase is deterministic

Omar El-Sayed: KV cache “warm” needs session continuity. Suggest: each virtual user does N turns, where turn1 is cold prefill, turns2..N reuse KV. Report metrics for turn1 separately from continuation turns.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. Otherwise warm can hide queueing issues. For chat streaming workload: we should have metrics buckets:
- TTFT_turn1 (cold)
- TTFT_turnK (warm)
- TPOT_turnK
And for tool-calling: bursts where KV may/may not help.

Claire Dubois: I like that but then we have “warm” scenario still containing a cold component (turn1). Is that ok?

Jonas Weber: yes, that’s actually representative: a session starts somewhere. just label it explicitly.

Allison Grant: please keep the naming consistent: `cache_mode` + `turn_index` or `turn_bucket=first|followup`. We can build Grafana panels off that.

Sean Gallagher: also define eviction behavior. For baseline warm, we should *avoid* evictions by sizing cache generously, otherwise hit rate becomes a function of concurrency. Then have a separate “stress” workload later if we want eviction.

Sana Farid: agreeing with Sean. For Q1 baseline, warm should target >= 95% KV hit rate on followup turns (or whatever we can reliably achieve) and we record actual hit rate. If it drops under, fail the run as invalid (not a perf regression).

Hiro Tanaka: warm prefix cache is trickier (shared). Are we enabling cross-request prefix cache in baseline warm? That could be unrealistic depending on tenant isolation.

Mateo Alvarez: good call. Let’s separate:
- Warm KV: yes (per-session)
- Prefix cache: report both off/on? or keep it off for baseline?

Rina Kobayashi: I’d keep prefix cache OFF in baseline unless we can define a stable, non-leaky corpus. prefix cache hit rate depends on prompt reuse patterns and could be sensitive.

Jonas Weber: from telemetry, prefix reuse exists but it’s very cohort-specific. I’m ok leaving prefix cache out of baseline v1 and adding it as an explicit “optional scenario” later.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. also prefix cache can raise privacy questions. we can still capture a “prefix_cache_enabled=false” in manifest so it’s explicit.

Claire Dubois: so decision:
- publish baseline numbers for two scenarios: cold and warm-KV
- warm-KV defined as multi-turn sessions with deterministic warmup; prefix cache remains disabled (for Q1 baseline)
- dashboards + manifest capture the flags + hit rate

Connor O'Brien: from release gating POV, two scenarios is fine as long as thresholds are per-scenario. We can gate on cold-turn1 TTFT p99 and warm-followup TPOT p99 separately.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: as long as the metric taxonomy is crisp. I’ll add `cache_mode`, `kv_cache_enabled`, `prefix_cache_enabled`, `kv_hit_rate`, and `turn_bucket` to the metrics dictionary draft.

Sean Gallagher: and add to runbook: for cold runs, we should flush caches / restart worker between runs (or run with flags so state can’t persist). for warm runs, explicitly *don’t* restart between warmup and measure phases.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll file a harness task:
- manifest schema update (add cache fields)
- workload generator update for multi-turn + labeling
- validator checks for warm run must include hit rate + min turns

Mateo Alvarez: cool. Claire can you update methodology doc to reflect “report both cold and warm-KV” and call out prefix cache deferred?

Claire Dubois: yup. will update the Confluence page + link ADR-0172 section on run manifest fields. Also adding a note: baseline tables must show cold + warm columns, no single blended number.

Sofia Ivanova: :white_check_mark: thank you. this will save us a ton of confusion when someone says “TTFT regressed” but only for cold prefill.

Hiro Tanaka: works for me. kernel baselines will still catch long-prefill regressions via cold.

Mateo Alvarez: Decision locked then: Baseline reporting includes BOTH cold and warm-KV scenarios; prefix cache stays off in baseline v1; all runs must record cache config + hit rate and label first vs followup turns.

(Sean Gallagher reacted with :thumbsup:)
(Allison Grant reacted with :white_check_mark:)
(Rina Kobayashi reacted with :hammer_and_wrench:)

title: #eng-runtime: 1763849021
preview:
Talia Benson: ok folks, need to lock the baseline sweep schedule for next week (models x regions x SKUs) so we can reserve windows on the benchmark fleet. pls comment on constraints + owners.

Talia Benson: tracker sheet (please use this one, it has the capacity tab + conditional formatting): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1rwiQ1_Engineering_ServingRuntime_Q1_2026 ("q1-2026-baseline-run-tracker")

Rina Kobayashi: thx. proposing we split into 2 tracks:
- track A: us-east (H100 + A100)
- track B: eu-west (H100 + L40S)
so we can parallelize but keep env stable per track.

Rina Kobayashi: for models, minimal set for baseline freeze:
- llama-3.1-70b-instruct (fp16 + int8 where supported)
- qwen2.5-72b-instruct
- mistral-large-ish (the one we ship in catalog, exact tag in sheet)
- embeddings: bge-large-en-v1.5 (or whatever catalog tag Jonas picked)

Mateo Alvarez: +1 on parallel tracks. pls make sure each run manifest pins runtime build/tag + scheduler baseline. we can’t have “oops different build” across regions again.

Sofia Ivanova: for scheduling: i’d like long-prefill runs to be isolated from chat streaming if we’re sharing the same node pool window. long-prefill will swing queueing metrics and makes comparison gross.

Sean Gallagher: fleet reality check:
- H100 pool is the bottleneck. we can reserve 8xH100 blocks but only in 4-hour chunks.
- A100 pool is easier (can do overnight).
- L40S pool is shared with console perf folks; need to book early.

i can reserve windows in bench-scheduler but need exact start/end + how many nodes.

BenchmarkBot: Reminder: bench-scheduler reservations require a ticket reference in the description (ENG-4821 or sub-issue). Unreferenced reservations will be auto-expired after 24h.

Talia Benson: noted. we’ll tag all reservations with ENG-4821 + workload id.

Sana Farid: sample size ask: for each (model, workload, region, sku) we need >= 30 iterations post-warmup for p99 stability. if we’re doing cold+warm caching scenarios, that doubles.

Marcus Reed: warmup: current harness default is 5 min warmup + 3 min cool-down between workloads. for long-prefill we may need 10 min warmup to stabilize GPU clocks.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel note: for Qwen on L40S we’re still seeing fallback when seq_len > 8k unless we pin the "flashattn_v3" path. if we don’t want noise, we should either (a) cap seq lengths in long-context workload on L40S or (b) explicitly pin kernel for baseline run.

Mateo Alvarez: baseline policy is “representative, but stable + attributable.” i’m ok with pinning kernel for baseline, but we must record it in the manifest and call it out in the baseline report.

Rina Kobayashi: ok. in the tracker i added a column: "kernel pin?" and "reason".

Sergio Costa: dashboard side: pls standardize run names so links work.
format i need: `q1-2026/{region}/{sku}/{model}/{workload}/{cold|warm}`
otherwise i can’t generate the drilldown URL reliably.

Sahana Iyer: CI / artifact export: if these are running in eu-west, make sure the service account can write to the eu bucket. last time we had a silent failure on artifacts.

Sean Gallagher: yep, that’s still flaky depending on which nodegroup the job lands on. i can force nodegroup `bench-eu-west-1a` for baseline windows. but it reduces capacity.

Talia Benson: can we do this schedule?
Mon:
- us-east H100: chat streaming (llama, qwen) + embeddings
- eu-west H100: long-prefill (llama) + tool-calling bursty
Tue:
- us-east A100: long-prefill (llama, qwen)
- eu-west L40S: chat streaming (llama) + embeddings
Wed:
- us-east H100: tool-calling (llama, qwen) + long-prefill (qwen if time)
- eu-west H100: chat streaming (mistral) + long-prefill (mistral)
Thu:
- cleanup/re-runs + variance investigations + anything that failed export

Rina Kobayashi: workable but i want to avoid mixing tool-calling + chat in same reservation if we can help it (burstiness messes with scheduler counters). could group by “workload family” per window.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. suggest:
- one reservation = one workload generator config (chat, long-prefill, embeddings, tool).
- swap model within the reservation, but keep the workload constant.

Mateo Alvarez: agree. that’s also cleaner for “attribution” when we compare kernel/scheduler deltas.

Sean Gallagher: if that’s the plan, i need reservation blocks like:
- H100 us-east: 4h chat
- H100 us-east: 4h long-prefill
etc.

Sana Farid: also pls don’t schedule “first run of the day” to be a baseline datapoint. we should do a throwaway stabilization run after node provision.

Marcus Reed: i can add a “burn-in” step to the harness invocation for baseline mode. basically:
`--burn_in_runs 5 --warmup_seconds 600`
then record only after.

Hiro Tanaka: if we’re doing long-prefill on H100, i want one slot to capture kernel timelines (nsys) for the p99 tail we saw last week. doesn’t have to be every run, just one per (model, sku).

Rina Kobayashi: ok but note that profiling runs should be labeled `profile=true` so they don’t contaminate the baseline stats. (they run slower)

Sergio Costa: +1. dashboard will filter out `profile=true` if tag exists.

Talia Benson: updating schedule to “workload-constant blocks.” Rina can you assign owners per block in the tracker today?

Rina Kobayashi: yep. i’ll assign:
- chat blocks: Marcus
- long-prefill blocks: Sofia + Marcus
- embeddings blocks: me
- tool-calling blocks: Mateo + Marcus
and will list Hiro’s profiling slots explicitly.

Sean Gallagher: once that’s in the sheet w/ exact times (including timezone), i’ll reserve. pls avoid overlapping H100 blocks across regions if we don’t have capacity.

Sahana Iyer: can someone verify we’re using the same runtime container digest in both regions? last week eu-west pulled latest.

Mateo Alvarez: i’ll pin digest in the harness config today and post in #eng-runtime. baseline anchor is `runtime-2026.01.14-rc3` (unless we change after freeze thread).

Talia Benson: action items:
1) Rina: fill tracker w/ block times + owners + kernel pin flags by EOD
2) Sean: reserve windows once tracker updated (ENG-4821 refs)
3) Mateo: pin runtime digest + confirm baseline tag
4) Sahana: sanity check artifact export permissions for eu-west (before Monday)

Rina Kobayashi: added a tab "Reservations" in the sheet. please don’t DM me times; put them there.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: will watch the sheet. once i reserve, i’ll paste reservation IDs back here.

BenchmarkBot: Reservation guideline: include "cold" vs "warm" in job name; include `manifest_schema_version` in artifacts. See ADR-0172.

Sergio Costa: when you paste reservation IDs, also paste the run-name prefix you’ll use. i’ll wire it into the baseline dashboard panels.

Talia Benson: cool. let’s aim to have reservations locked by tomorrow 11am PT so we don’t lose H100 windows.
