BenchmarkBot: Reminder: bench-scheduler reservations require a ticket reference in the description (ENG-4821 or sub-issue). Unreferenced reservations will be auto-expired after 24h.

Talia Benson: noted. we’ll tag all reservations with ENG-4821 + workload id.

Sana Farid: sample size ask: for each (model, workload, region, sku) we need >= 30 iterations post-warmup for p99 stability. if we’re doing cold+warm caching scenarios, that doubles.

Marcus Reed: warmup: current harness default is 5 min warmup + 3 min cool-down between workloads. for long-prefill we may need 10 min warmup to stabilize GPU clocks.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel note: for Qwen on L40S we’re still seeing fallback when seq_len > 8k unless we pin the "flashattn_v3" path. if we don’t want noise, we should either (a) cap seq lengths in long-context workload on L40S or (b) explicitly pin kernel for baseline run.

Mateo Alvarez: baseline policy is “representative, but stable + attributable.” i’m ok with pinning kernel for baseline, but we must record it in the manifest and call it out in the baseline report.

Rina Kobayashi: ok. in the tracker i added a column: "kernel pin?" and "reason".

Sergio Costa: dashboard side: pls standardize run names so links work.
format i need: `q1-2026/{region}/{sku}/{model}/{workload}/{cold|warm}`
otherwise i can’t generate the drilldown URL reliably.

Sahana Iyer: CI / artifact export: if these are running in eu-west, make sure the service account can write to the eu bucket. last time we had a silent failure on artifacts.

Sean Gallagher: yep, that’s still flaky depending on which nodegroup the job lands on. i can force nodegroup `bench-eu-west-1a` for baseline windows. but it reduces capacity.

Talia Benson: can we do this schedule?
Mon:
- us-east H100: chat streaming (llama, qwen) + embeddings
- eu-west H100: long-prefill (llama) + tool-calling bursty
Tue:
- us-east A100: long-prefill (llama, qwen)
- eu-west L40S: chat streaming (llama) + embeddings
Wed:
- us-east H100: tool-calling (llama, qwen) + long-prefill (qwen if time)
- eu-west H100: chat streaming (mistral) + long-prefill (mistral)
Thu:
- cleanup/re-runs + variance investigations + anything that failed export

Rina Kobayashi: workable but i want to avoid mixing tool-calling + chat in same reservation if we can help it (burstiness messes with scheduler counters). could group by “workload family” per window.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. suggest:
- one reservation = one workload generator config (chat, long-prefill, embeddings, tool).
- swap model within the reservation, but keep the workload constant.

Mateo Alvarez: agree. that’s also cleaner for “attribution” when we compare kernel/scheduler deltas.

Sean Gallagher: if that’s the plan, i need reservation blocks like:
- H100 us-east: 4h chat
- H100 us-east: 4h long-prefill
etc.

Sana Farid: also pls don’t schedule “first run of the day” to be a baseline datapoint. we should do a throwaway stabilization run after node provision.

Marcus Reed: i can add a “burn-in” step to the harness invocation for baseline mode. basically:
`--burn_in_runs 5 --warmup_seconds 600`
then record only after.

Hiro Tanaka: if we’re doing long-prefill on H100, i want one slot to capture kernel timelines (nsys) for the p99 tail we saw last week. doesn’t have to be every run, just one per (model, sku).

Rina Kobayashi: ok but note that profiling runs should be labeled `profile=true` so they don’t contaminate the baseline stats. (they run slower)

Sergio Costa: +1. dashboard will filter out `profile=true` if tag exists.

Talia Benson: updating schedule to “workload-constant blocks.” Rina can you assign owners per block in the tracker today?

Rina Kobayashi: yep. i’ll assign:
- chat blocks: Marcus
- long-prefill blocks: Sofia + Marcus
- embeddings blocks: me
- tool-calling blocks: Mateo + Marcus
and will list Hiro’s profiling slots explicitly.

Sean Gallagher: once that’s in the sheet w/ exact times (including timezone), i’ll reserve. pls avoid overlapping H100 blocks across regions if we don’t have capacity.

Sahana Iyer: can someone verify we’re using the same runtime container digest in both regions? last week eu-west pulled latest.

Mateo Alvarez: i’ll pin digest in the harness config today and post in #eng-runtime. baseline anchor is `runtime-2026.01.14-rc3` (unless we change after freeze thread).

Talia Benson: action items:
1) Rina: fill tracker w/ block times + owners + kernel pin flags by EOD
2) Sean: reserve windows once tracker updated (ENG-4821 refs)
3) Mateo: pin runtime digest + confirm baseline tag
4) Sahana: sanity check artifact export permissions for eu-west (before Monday)

Rina Kobayashi: added a tab "Reservations" in the sheet. please don’t DM me times; put them there.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: will watch the sheet. once i reserve, i’ll paste reservation IDs back here.

BenchmarkBot: Reservation guideline: include "cold" vs "warm" in job name; include `manifest_schema_version` in artifacts. See ADR-0172.

Sergio Costa: when you paste reservation IDs, also paste the run-name prefix you’ll use. i’ll wire it into the baseline dashboard panels.

Talia Benson: cool. let’s aim to have reservations locked by tomorrow 11am PT so we don’t lose H100 windows.

title: #eng-runtime: 1763608899
preview:
Rina Kobayashi: seeing a surprising p99 regression on long-prefill workload in the baseline sweep (the 16k prefill / 256 decode scenario). A100 us-east. p50 looks flat, p99 TTFT jumped ~+28-35% vs last week’s anchor. Anyone around to help triage?

Rina Kobayashi: quick numbers from the dashboard export (same workload seed, same concurrency target=12):
- build: runtime@9f2c1d7 (from last night)
- baseline ref: runtime@7c1a0b4 (12/02)
- model: llama-3.1-70b-instruct (bf16)
- p99 TTFT: 2.41s -> 3.18s (+31.9%)
- p99 TPOT: basically unchanged (+2-3%)
- throughput tokens/s: within noise

Sofia Ivanova: +31% on p99 TTFT with TPOT stable smells like queueing/backpressure or prefill kernel path change (not decode). do you have scheduler queue-wait breakdown from the new metrics?

Rina Kobayashi: yep, grabbed the run artifacts. queue_wait_p99 increased a lot.
- queue_wait_p99: 180ms -> 640ms
- batch_assembly_p99: 22ms -> 31ms
- prefill_gpu_time_p99: 1.9s -> 2.0s (small)
so most of it is “waiting to get on GPU” not GPU time.

Sean Gallagher: that’s consistent with fleet noise too (placement / noisy neighbor) BUT 640ms is big. which node pool? did we have clock locking on for that run?

Rina Kobayashi: node pool: bench-a100-80g-ue1. clock locking enabled per standard warmup (same as baseline ref). warmup completed without errors.

Omar El-Sayed: can you paste the scheduler log snippet around overload? i want to see if we’re hitting the backpressure thresholds (prefill_inflight / kv_cache pressure).

Rina Kobayashi: from `scheduler_stats.jsonl` summary (last 5 min):
```
backpressure_events_total=147
load_shed_total=0
prefill_inflight_limit_hit=139
kv_cache_evictions_total=0
batch_size_prefill_p50=3
batch_size_prefill_p95=6
prefill_queue_depth_p99=29
```
this didn’t happen in the baseline ref (backpressure_events_total was ~0-5).

Sofia Ivanova: that’s… not good. why are we hitting prefill inflight limit now on same concurrency? did default change in the runtime build?

Marcus Reed: there was a change merged last week to protect decode throughput under mixed workloads. i don’t remember it touching the long-prefill-only scenario though. let me check if that build includes the backpressure tuning.

Mateo Alvarez: I’m here. Let’s treat this as real until proven fleet noise. Rina: can you link the run manifest for both runs? want to diff scheduler defaults + kernel selection.

Rina Kobayashi: manifests:
- regressed: https://bench-artifacts.redwood-internal/runs/1763608120/manifest.json
- baseline ref: https://bench-artifacts.redwood-internal/runs/1762931102/manifest.json
(permissions: internal)

Hiro Tanaka: pulling the manifests now. if prefill GPU time p99 only +~5% but scheduler is throttling, might still be kernel-selection causing longer “GPU occupied per request” in a way that reduces batch cadence (e.g., worse overlap).

Yuki Sato: +1. also we’ve seen kernel auto-selection flip around the 12k-16k regime when seq_len crosses heuristic boundaries. if that build changed the kernel matrix, could change how long a prefill holds the stream.

Grace Kim: do we have kernel debug dumps enabled for the regressed run? if not, rerun with `RW_KERNEL_DEBUG_DUMP=1` / `RW_KERNEL_DEBUG_SAMPLE_RATE=1.0` just for this workload.

Rina Kobayashi: kernel dumps were OFF (i didn’t want the overhead). can rerun with dumps ON.

Omar El-Sayed: any profiler trace? even a quick nsys timeline screenshot would help.

Rina Kobayashi: I did a quick nsys capture on a single replica (not full harness) to get a feel. screenshots:
- https://slack-files.com/TMP-nsys_prefill_timeline.png
- https://slack-files.com/TMP-kernel_summary.png
what I notice: fewer concurrent prefill kernels in flight; more gaps between launches.

Sofia Ivanova: “more gaps” suggests we’re throttling prefill submission (inflight limit / fairness) not just slower kernels.

Marcus Reed: I found it: runtime@9f2c1d7 includes PR-21541 “tune backpressure thresholds for long prefill” (merged behind a default flag flip). It tightened prefill_inflight_limit for A100 to reduce p99 *decode* in mixed traffic. Might be biting us in prefill-only.

Mateo Alvarez: ok, that’s likely. but we still need to confirm with data: same workload, only default changed => queueing. Sofia can you validate what knobs changed between manifests?

Sofia Ivanova: diffing now. from manifest (regressed) scheduler_config:
- `prefill_inflight_limit`: 8 (was 16)
- `prefill_fairness_weight`: 0.65 (was 0.5)
- `max_wait_before_force_batch_ms`: 6 (was 10)
that would absolutely increase queue_wait under high prefill time.

Sean Gallagher: + note: if prefill inflight is 8 and concurrency=12, you’re guaranteed backlog. if per-request prefill is ~2s, queue will stack.

Sana Farid: from a noise-model POV this is too large to be “variance”. If manifests show knob delta, we should treat it as a real regression for the long-prefill suite and decide if baseline uses old knobs or we need to re-anchor.

Hiro Tanaka: still want to rule out kernel flip too. if prefill inflight limit dropped, throughput should drop / p50 TTFT might move too, unless batching hides it. but your throughput is flat, so maybe the system is still saturating GPU, just increasing tail waits.

Rina Kobayashi: throughput in tokens/s is flat because the decode is short; prefill dominates and GPU stays mostly busy. the tail is queue wait.

Omar El-Sayed: can you compute “GPU busy %” from the run? (the harness exports `gpu_active_time_ratio` right?)

Rina Kobayashi: yep:
- regressed gpu_active_time_ratio: 0.93
- baseline ref: 0.91
so GPU is busy in both.

Grace Kim: if we rerun with old prefill_inflight_limit (16), we should get back baseline TTFT p99. that would isolate scheduler knob as primary.

Sofia Ivanova: agree. Two experiments:
1) rerun regressed build with `prefill_inflight_limit=16` (override) and compare
2) rerun baseline ref build with `prefill_inflight_limit=8` and see if it reproduces

Mateo Alvarez: perfect. Rina can you run those two A/B runs on the bench pool today?

Rina Kobayashi: yep. I can kick those off now. will take ~45min each including warmup.

Yuki Sato: while that runs, I’ll prep a rerun config with kernel debug dumps enabled (sampled) just in case. if the scheduler knob isn’t the full story, we’ll need kernel attribution.

Hiro Tanaka: if kernel path changed, expect to see attention kernel name differences in dumps (flash-attn v2 vs fallback). For long prefill, we care about the prefill attention path and any rope/scaling kernels that show up.

Sean Gallagher: also sanity check: same driver/CUDA? we’ve been doing some fleet maintenance.

Rina Kobayashi: manifests show same:
- driver: 550.54
- cuda: 12.4
- triton: 3.1.0
- container image: bench-runtime:2025.12.08
so environment looks consistent.

Marcus Reed: note: PR-21541 was meant to improve p99 TTFT on long-prefill in *mixed* workloads by preventing decode starvation. But in a pure prefill stress test it might just be artificial throttling. We might need workload-specific knobs or change the representative suite to include a mixed prefill+chat scenario for gating.

Sana Farid: we should not change the suite to fit the change. suite should reflect prod. question is: do we actually see pure long-prefill saturation in prod? Applied ML said summarization cohort is spiky but not usually isolated.

Omar El-Sayed: still, the suite is intended to catch “worst-case” tails and protect us. If defaults cause huge TTFT tails under plausible concurrency, that’s actionable.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. We can decide to either (a) revert default flip for this quarter baseline, or (b) keep new default but accept the new baseline and adjust thresholding. But this looks like an unintended regression for the workload definition we already agreed on.

Mateo Alvarez: decision later. First isolate.
Action items:
- Rina: run A/B with inflight limit overrides; post results + links
- Sofia: confirm exact config delta + propose guardrail (maybe dynamic inflight based on workload type)
- Hiro/Yuki: prepare kernel dump rerun template (not necessarily run yet)
- Sean: double-check bench pool stability wasn’t compromised (clock locking, MIG config, etc)

Sean Gallagher: on it. I’ll check recent bench-a100 events. no obvious node churn but I’ll confirm.

Rina Kobayashi: starting run #1 now (regressed build + override inflight=16). Run name: `lprefill_a100_ue1_9f2c1d7_inflight16`.

[~55 min later]

Rina Kobayashi: results are in.
Run #1 (regressed build + inflight=16): https://bench-artifacts.redwood-internal/runs/1763612201/
- p99 TTFT: 3.18s -> 2.46s (basically back to baseline)
- queue_wait_p99: 640ms -> 210ms
- backpressure_events_total: 147 -> 11

Rina Kobayashi: starting run #2 (baseline ref build + override inflight=8). Run name: `lprefill_a100_ue1_7c1a0b4_inflight8`.

[~50 min later]

Rina Kobayashi: run #2 done: https://bench-artifacts.redwood-internal/runs/1763615410/
- p99 TTFT: 2.41s -> 3.12s
- queue_wait_p99: 190ms -> 615ms
- backpressure_events_total: 4 -> 133
So yeah, it’s the inflight limit change.

Sofia Ivanova: great, isolated. We should treat as regression vs our current long-prefill workload definition. I’ll open a follow-up to adjust defaults: either bump inflight for “prefill-dominant” workload mode or make it adaptive (based on observed decode share).

Marcus Reed: I’ll comment on PR-21541 thread and propose we gate the default flip behind a traffic-shape detector, not unconditional. It helped mixed traffic but hurts this extreme case.

Hiro Tanaka: given the isolation, kernel path probably not the culprit here. but we should still run a kernel dump once this is fixed to ensure no silent flip. (I’ve been burned by that before.)

Yuki Sato: agreed. once we land a scheduler fix, I’ll do one kernel-dump run per SKU for long-prefill to ensure selection matches kernel matrix expectations.

Sana Farid: for the baseline program: we need a consistent stance on “what config defines baseline.” If scheduler defaults are changing, we need to re-anchor baseline with a freeze tag + config snapshot. Otherwise we’ll keep chasing moving targets.

Mateo Alvarez: aligned. We’ll pin the baseline scheduler config (and record overrides) for Q1 2026. Sofia, please add a note to the scheduler baseline doc: long-prefill suite uses inflight=16 for now pending adaptive policy.

Sean Gallagher: checked bench pool. clocks locked, no MIG changes, no anomalies. variance here is config-driven.

Omar El-Sayed: can we add an explicit metric panel on dashboard for `prefill_inflight_limit_hit` / backpressure events? would make this obvious in future.

Rina Kobayashi: +1, I had to dig into logs. I can add it to the export template / metrics dictionary.

Mateo Alvarez: next steps:
1) Sofia to propose fix + rollout plan (keep mixed-traffic gains)
2) Rina to mark the regressed datapoint in the run tracker as “expected due to inflight limit flip” + link this thread
3) Sana to update regression threshold config so that until fix lands, canary doesn’t page us repeatedly on this known issue (route to triage channel only)

Sana Farid: ack. I’ll add a temporary exception keyed by build range + workload id, with expiry (2 weeks). not turning it off entirely.

Sofia Ivanova: I’ll draft a patch: make prefill inflight limit higher when decode_target_tokens <= 512 and prompt_len_bin == long_context, OR make it proportional to expected prefill time. will share proposal in #eng-runtime later today.

Grace Kim: once scheduler fix is in, I’ll run a quick validation sweep on L40S too; we’ve seen different behavior on those cards.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks all. good triage.

1) Sofia to propose fix + rollout plan (keep mixed-traffic gains)
2) Rina to mark the regressed datapoint in the run tracker as “expected due to inflight limit flip” + link this thread
3) Sana to update regression threshold config so that until fix lands, canary doesn’t page us repeatedly on this known issue (route to triage channel only)

Sana Farid: ack. I’ll add a temporary exception keyed by build range + workload id, with expiry (2 weeks). not turning it off entirely.

Sofia Ivanova: I’ll draft a patch: make prefill inflight limit higher when decode_target_tokens <= 512 and prompt_len_bin == long_context, OR make it proportional to expected prefill time. will share proposal in #eng-runtime later today.

Grace Kim: once scheduler fix is in, I’ll run a quick validation sweep on L40S too; we’ve seen different behavior on those cards.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks all. good triage.

title: #eng-sre: 1763512044
preview:
Sean Gallagher: seeing a bunch of variance in the baseline sweeps this week (esp p99 TTFT). before we chase runtime ghosts, can we align on “benchmark fleet hygiene” + a standard warmup/isolation protocol?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. same commit / same manifest, but L40S runs in us-east swing ~8-12% tokens/s. feels like placement/noisy neighbor.

Claire Dubois: methodology-wise we need to be able to say “this delta is real” vs “this was a different box having a bad day”. what are the top suspected sources?

Allison Grant: in order of likelihood from past infra perf stuff:
- GPU clocks not pinned / auto-boost variance
- power limits inconsistent across hosts
- thermal throttling (esp if other jobs just ran)
- NUMA / CPU contention (shared host, bad pinning)
- storage/network noise if artifacts/logging is heavy mid-run
- kernel cache/warm cache effects (first run after deploy)

Aisha Bello: also: are we mixing MIG/non-MIG hosts in the same “SKU” bucket? that’s bitten us in SLO dashboards before.

Sean Gallagher: MIG should be off for benchmark fleet, but I don’t think we *assert* it anywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: we don’t. harness assumes “one job == one node”, but scheduler can still pack if the queue isn’t exclusive.

Sana Farid: from noise model side: we can tolerate some variance, but right now we’re seeing multimodal distributions (looks like two different regimes). that usually screams placement/clocking.

Rina Kobayashi: concrete example: H100 eu-west, chat-streaming workload, concurrency=32. Run A p50 TPOT ~28ms, Run B ~31ms. Both “pass” but p99 swings more.

Sean Gallagher: ok, proposal:
1) make benchmark fleet “exclusive” (no other workloads) + label/taint nodes
2) standard warmup steps for every run, and record them in manifest
3) pin GPU clocks + enforce persistence + log power/temps at start/end

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, if we can export “gpu_clock_locked=true/false” + “host_profile=bench” as tags, I can make a variance panel that breaks out by that.

Allison Grant: +1. also add “container co-tenancy count” if we can. even if you *intend* exclusivity, it’s good to assert.

Sean Gallagher: warmup protocol draft (tell me if this is sane):
- On node init:
  - `nvidia-smi -pm 1`
  - lock graphics+sm clocks to a known range (per SKU) via `nvidia-smi -lgc <min,max>`
  - set app clocks if supported / align power limit (`nvidia-smi -pl`), log the value
  - verify MIG disabled: `nvidia-smi -L` + check MIG mode
- Run warmup workload for 8-10 min:
  - use the *same model* as the actual run
  - fixed seed, moderate concurrency
  - discard metrics, but keep logs of achieved clock/temps
- Then execute measured trials (N>=10) with 60s cool-down between trials
- Capture at start/end of each trial: clocks, temps, power draw, CPU freq, and `numa` topology

Aisha Bello: +1 on start/end sampling. can we also sample once mid-run? we’ve seen clocks sag 3-4 min in.

Allison Grant: mid-run sampling is good, but keep it lightweight. `nvidia-smi dmon` in background to a local file (then upload at end) works.

Rina Kobayashi: how do we do cool-down? we don’t want to flush caches that are realistic, but we also don’t want thermal creep.

Claire Dubois: we should define what baseline intends: “warm steady-state” vs “cold start”. earlier thread decided we report both cold and warm scenarios, right?

Sean Gallagher: yeah. so:
- “cold” scenario: first measured trial after deploy + after clearing KV/prefix caches (harness-level)
- “warm” scenario: after warmup, steady-state
Thermal cool-down: just a short idle (60s) to stabilize, not a full cool-to-ambient.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. baseline is mostly steady-state serving. we can still publish cold TTFT separately.

Sana Farid: re: trial count, if we’re going to gate on small deltas, we need enough samples to estimate variance. N=10 might be borderline for p99.

Allison Grant: agree. p99 from N=10 is basically vibes unless each trial has lots of requests. maybe define in terms of “total requests observed per cohort” rather than trials.

Rina Kobayashi: current harness does ~3k requests per trial for chat streaming at those concurrencies. so N=5 already gives ~15k reqs.

Sana Farid: ok that’s better. can we just say: minimum 20k requests per cohort, and the harness can decide how many trials.

Sean Gallagher: works for me.

Sergio Costa: tagging ask for dashboarding:
- `benchmark_host_id`
- `gpu_uuid`
- `gpu_sku`
- `gpu_clock_lock` (actual min/max)
- `power_limit_watts`
- `mig_mode`
- `exclusive_node=true/false`
- `warmup_minutes`
- `warmup_workload_id`
If we have these in the run manifest and results export, I can slice variance quickly.

Allison Grant: also: CPU isolation. if we’re on k8s, we should request guaranteed QoS + CPU manager static policy + pinning. otherwise scheduler overhead itself is noise.

Sean Gallagher: yep. for benchmark queue we can enforce:
- `requests == limits` for CPU/mem
- `cpuManagerPolicy=static`
- `topologyManagerPolicy=restricted`
And for bare metal jobs, we can use cpusets.

Mateo Alvarez: will that be available in the benchmark cluster by early Q1?

Sean Gallagher: mostly, yes. we’ll need one cluster config change + a node pool dedicated to bench.

InfraBot: (auto) Change request created: BENCH-CLUSTER-1127 "Enable CPU manager static + topology restricted on benchmark node pool" (owner: Sean Gallagher)

Aisha Bello: placement: can we force “one job per node” with hard anti-affinity? I’m nervous we’ll *think* we’re exclusive and we’re not.

Sean Gallagher: yep. we can set a taint on bench nodes and tolerate only from bench jobs, and also enforce `podAntiAffinity` on hostname.

Rina Kobayashi: can we get a quick doc / checklist? right now every engineer is running “their own special ceremony” and it’s chaos.

Claire Dubois: +1. needs to be canonical in methodology. also needs to state what to do when runs violate the protocol.

Allison Grant: suggestion: “run rejected” criteria:
- clocks not locked
- MIG enabled
- more than 1 container on node
- gpu temp > threshold at start (e.g. >75C)
- power limit deviates from baseline for that SKU

Sean Gallagher: I’ll draft a runbook + add a preflight check that fails fast if those aren’t satisfied.

Sana Farid: please include the “accepted variance envelope” we’re using so people don’t rerun forever. if preflight passes and we still see weirdness, that’s a different escalation path.

Sergio Costa: if you can log all that preflight state as a single line JSON, we can parse into metrics easily.

Mateo Alvarez: action items?

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- Sean: write “Benchmark Fleet Hygiene” runbook + preflight checks (clocks/MIG/exclusivity) and propose cluster changes (taints/anti-affinity/CPU pinning)
- Rina: update harness to run warmup phase + record warmup metadata in manifest
- Sergio: add dashboard panels for variance by host/clock lock/power limit
- Sana/Claire: update methodology doc with min request counts + rejection criteria + how to classify noise vs regression

Rina Kobayashi: :thumbsup: I’ll start with warmup + host sampling. do we have SKU-specific clock lock values somewhere?

Allison Grant: we can derive from partner recs. for now, safe defaults:
- H100: lock to a stable band (don’t max out). we’ve used ~1.3–1.4GHz SM in other benches.
- A100/L40S similar. but we should confirm w/ the fleet configs.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll pull current fleet power/clock configs and post a table. if we need partner guidance, we have that Nvidia best-practices notes from Dec.

Aisha Bello: once this is in place, can we flag “noncompliant runs” in the run tracker sheet? would help triage.

Sergio Costa: yep, if the manifest has a `compliance_status=pass/fail` (or reasons), I can surface it.

Sean Gallagher: cool. I’ll post the doc draft in this thread later today.

title: #eng-ml: 1763429902
preview:
Jonas Weber: dropping the latest telemetry-derived length hists + proposed bins for v1 representative suite. need ML signoff that this is “close enough” to prod shapes (w/o leaking prompt content).

Jonas Weber: doc: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1RedwoodPerfSuiteTelemetry (see `len_hists_us-east_eu-west_2025-11-XX.pdf`)

Logan Wright: quick context: these are from aggregated route-level histograms (no raw prompts). Filtered to top 15 tenants by QPS + sampled across tiers. Excludes internal load tests.

Claire Dubois: proposed input (prefill) token bins:
- A: 0–128
- B: 129–512
- C: 513–2,048
- D: 2,049–8,192
- E: 8,193–32,768

Claire Dubois: output (decode) bins:
- 1–32
- 33–128
- 129–512
- 513+

Claire Dubois: and a first-pass request-mix (by request count):
- chat streaming: 55%
- tool/function calling (multi-turn bursts): 10%
- long-context summarization (big prefill): 15%
- embeddings: 20%

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: thx. first question: are those bins aligned to *model context windows* for our top catalog? (llama/qwen/mistral variants). esp the E bin — will it just be “mostly failures” on some models?

Jonas Weber: good call. for baseline runs we’ll only run E where model context supports it (or cap at model max). but suite definition wants to preserve that tail b/c prod has a real long-context cohort.

Mateo Alvarez: from runtime POV: we’d prefer to keep E, even if some models cap, because it’s exactly where scheduler/backpressure gets spicy. we can make it conditional in the workload config per model.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. harness can validate “requested_ctx <= model_max_ctx” and log when we clip. clipping needs to be explicit in manifest though (so we don’t silently change the workload).

Sana Farid: please don’t do silent clipping. it’ll destroy comparability + makes noise model weird (input lengths drifting). better: define E as “target 16k (or 32k) if supported else skip cohort.”

Logan Wright: re: representativeness — telemetry shows clear elbow at ~2k and ~8k, so C/D split is correct. E is tiny by count (~0.6–1.1% depending on region) but large by token volume.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: ok, but if E is <1% by count, why include it at all in v1? (not arguing, just want rationale spelled out)

Jonas Weber: because it’s disproportionately responsible for tail TTFT incidents + regressions. we want a sentinel workload even if it’s low frequency.

Claire Dubois: + it’s the main knob that stresses KV cache + prefill/decode overlap. perf changes often look fine on A/B/C and then p99 blows up on D/E.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: makes sense. can we make weighting explicit as both “by request” and “by tokens”? product folks keep asking about “what’s most expensive” vs “most common.”

Sana Farid: strongly recommend we report both. for suite composition we can keep the driver as request-count weights, but for analysis we should add token-weighted view.

Rina Kobayashi: harness already exports tokens_in/tokens_out per request. adding a token-weighted aggregation is easy.

Logan Wright: here are the region diffs FYI:
- eu-west has slightly higher median prefill (more enterprise doc summarization)
- us-east has higher chat streaming share
but bins are stable across both.

Jonas Weber: on request mix: the 20% embeddings is from /embeddings route histogram. do we believe that’s fair for “top customer traffic shapes”? (it’s high-QPS but small payload)

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: embeddings share depends heavily on cohort (some customers do tons of retrieval). but we *do* need it in v1 because it exercises different queueing (many small requests) and can cause batching pathologies.

Mateo Alvarez: embeddings is also where we see scheduler fairness issues. + it’s easier to regress “req/s” w/o noticing if we only look at chat.

Claire Dubois: propose we tweak weights slightly:
- chat streaming: 60%
- tool calling: 10%
- long-context: 15%
- embeddings: 15%
Reason: matches overall count share a bit better when excluding a few embedding-heavy outliers.

Logan Wright: i can confirm: if we winsorize tenants at p95 for embeddings-heavy, embeddings share drops from ~20% -> ~14–16% depending on week.

Jonas Weber: ok, that’s a clean story. question for Maya: tool/function calling — do we have enough telemetry to define “bursty multi-turn” realistically, or are we guessing?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: we don’t have perfect visibility into “tools” because it’s app-level, but we can approximate with:
- short prefill (A/B)
- moderate decode (32–128)
- tight inter-arrival in session (client tends to fire next call quickly)
and use a synthetic “session” generator with 3–6 turns + 10–30% turns invoking tools.

Rina Kobayashi: we can implement “sessions” as a workload primitive (N turns, think time distribution). but for perf we need to be careful: think time shouldn’t dominate wall clock.

Sana Farid: yes please keep “think time” small/controlled and include it in manifest. otherwise it’ll add variance and obscure runtime changes.

Claire Dubois: current draft uses think_time_ms ~ Uniform(50, 250). that’s enough to create burstiness without turning it into a soak test.

Mateo Alvarez: fwiw, for scheduler stress we might also want a “microburst” mode: concurrency ramps 50 -> 200 in <10s, then holds. that’s closer to prod launch spikes.

Jonas Weber: yep, but trying to keep v1 minimal. maybe tool-calling workload gets the burst ramp, while chat streaming stays steady-state.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: on length bins: output bins look fine but i’d add a special case for “very long decode” (1k+ tokens) — do we see that in prod?

Logan Wright: long decode exists but mostly in non-streaming endpoints (batch summarization / report generation). by count it’s small (<2%), but by tokens_out it matters.

Claire Dubois: if we add another bin, suite blows up combinatorially. alternative: keep 513+ as a bin, but for the long-context workload specifically set decode target to 256–768 (not tiny). and maybe add a separate “long decode sentinel” in v2.

Sana Farid: +1. keep v1 stable; add v2 backlog item for “long decode sentinel” once noise model is nailed.

Jonas Weber: sounds like consensus:
1) keep prefill bins A–E, but E is conditional on model context; do not clip silently
2) request-mix weights updated to 60/10/15/15 (chat/tool/longctx/emb)
3) report both request-weighted and token-weighted summaries
4) tool calling approximated via session generator (3–6 turns) with small controlled think time + optional ramp

Jonas Weber: Maya, ok to mark “approved for v1” w/ those edits?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: approved w/ two asks:
- add a short paragraph in the suite doc explaining the winsorization choice (why embeddings weight isn’t 20%)
- explicitly note that E is a *sentinel* cohort (low count, high tail risk)

Claire Dubois: ack, will update the representative suite doc + add rationale section.

Rina Kobayashi: i’ll add manifest fields: `workload.cohort_id`, `workload.target_ctx_tokens`, `workload.skip_if_unsupported=true`, and ensure validator fails if someone “clips” without stating it.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks all. pls ping me when the session generator PR is up; want to sanity check scheduler knobs for burst mode.

Jonas Weber: will do. logging decision into ADR-0173 notes as well.

Sana Farid: and once we have first runs with these weights, i want to sanity-check variance per cohort before we freeze thresholds.

Logan Wright: i’ll post the updated telemetry snapshot (same filters) so it’s reproducible. will include query hash + time range.

Jonas Weber: perfect. closing loop here.

- embeddings: 15%

Logan Wright: i can confirm: if we winsorize tenants at p95 for embeddings-heavy, embeddings share drops from ~20% -> ~14–16% depending on week.

Jonas Weber: ok, that’s a clean story. question for Maya: tool/function calling — do we have enough telemetry to define “bursty multi-turn” realistically, or are we guessing?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: we don’t have perfect visibility into “tools” because it’s app-level, but we can approximate with:
- short prefill (A/B)
- moderate decode (32–128)
- tight inter-arrival in session (client tends to fire next call quickly)
and use a synthetic “session” generator with 3–6 turns + 10–30% turns invoking tools.

Rina Kobayashi: we can implement “sessions” as a workload primitive (N turns, think time distribution). but for perf we need to be careful: think time shouldn’t dominate wall clock.

Sana Farid: yes please keep “think time” small/controlled and include it in manifest. otherwise it’ll add variance and obscure runtime changes.

Claire Dubois: current draft uses think_time_ms ~ Uniform(50, 250). that’s enough to create burstiness without turning it into a soak test.

Mateo Alvarez: fwiw, for scheduler stress we might also want a “microburst” mode: concurrency ramps 50 -> 200 in <10s, then holds. that’s closer to prod launch spikes.

Jonas Weber: yep, but trying to keep v1 minimal. maybe tool-calling workload gets the burst ramp, while chat streaming stays steady-state.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: on length bins: output bins look fine but i’d add a special case for “very long decode” (1k+ tokens) — do we see that in prod?

Logan Wright: long decode exists but mostly in non-streaming endpoints (batch summarization / report generation). by count it’s small (<2%), but by tokens_out it matters.

Claire Dubois: if we add another bin, suite blows up combinatorially. alternative: keep 513+ as a bin, but for the long-context workload specifically set decode target to 256–768 (not tiny). and maybe add a separate “long decode sentinel” in v2.

Sana Farid: +1. keep v1 stable; add v2 backlog item for “long decode sentinel” once noise model is nailed.

Jonas Weber: sounds like consensus:
1) keep prefill bins A–E, but E is conditional on model context; do not clip silently
2) request-mix weights updated to 60/10/15/15 (chat/tool/longctx/emb)
3) report both request-weighted and token-weighted summaries
4) tool calling approximated via session generator (3–6 turns) with small controlled think time + optional ramp

Jonas Weber: Maya, ok to mark “approved for v1” w/ those edits?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: approved w/ two asks:
- add a short paragraph in the suite doc explaining the winsorization choice (why embeddings weight isn’t 20%)
- explicitly note that E is a *sentinel* cohort (low count, high tail risk)

Claire Dubois: ack, will update the representative suite doc + add rationale section.

Rina Kobayashi: i’ll add manifest fields: `workload.cohort_id`, `workload.target_ctx_tokens`, `workload.skip_if_unsupported=true`, and ensure validator fails if someone “clips” without stating it.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks all. pls ping me when the session generator PR is up; want to sanity check scheduler knobs for burst mode.

Jonas Weber: will do. logging decision into ADR-0173 notes as well.

Sana Farid: and once we have first runs with these weights, i want to sanity-check variance per cohort before we freeze thresholds.

Logan Wright: i’ll post the updated telemetry snapshot (same filters) so it’s reproducible. will include query hash + time range.

Jonas Weber: perfect. closing loop here.

title: #architecture: 1763335520
preview:
Claire Dubois: Dropping ADR-0172 draft here for review (metrics + run manifest schema). Would love eyes on: schema fields, versioning, and how we encode workload params + env capture without making it impossible to diff / store. Doc: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/ADR-0172-draft

Claire Dubois: Current proposal is 2 JSON artifacts per run:
1) `run_manifest.json` (inputs + env)
2) `results.json` (metrics + aggregations)

Claire Dubois: Biggest open questions called out in ADR:
- Do we version the schema (semver) vs a date stamp?
- Workload params: flatten vs nested (e.g., sequence length bins / distributions)
- Env capture: how strict is “required” (driver/CUDA, clocks, container digest, git SHA, region, etc.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 on splitting manifest vs results. For harness it’s way easier to treat manifest as immutable. But we need to be careful that results references manifest by hash or filename, otherwise dashboards get messy.

Allison Grant: Reading. Two immediate comments:
1) metrics definitions need to be explicit about boundary (server-only vs client-observed). We already have confusion in SLO land.
2) env capture: please include “measurement boundary” fields too, not just driver versions.

Sean Gallagher: For env capture, we should distinguish “must for reproducibility” vs “nice to have”. Otherwise CI is going to fail constantly when a field is missing on some runner.

Noah Patel: On schema versioning: I’d prefer `schema_version: 1` + `schema_revision` semver-ish. Date stamp is ambiguous (and people will cargo-cult it).

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Also: if we’re going to gate releases on this, we need backwards compatibility story. E.g., runtime adds new scheduler knob → manifests should still validate.

Jonas Weber: For workload params: pls keep them structured, not flattened. If we flatten everything into `workload_param_*` it’ll be unreadable. But we should be consistent about naming and units.

Logan Wright: Dashboarding/data pipeline angle: nested is ok as long as we have a stable path for key fields we group by. E.g. `model.name`, `hardware.gpu_sku`, `region`, `workload.id`, `workload.mode` should be top-level or at least fixed paths.

Connor O'Brien: On CI gating: validator should allow “unknown fields” so we can extend without breaking old runners. But we should lock down required fields.

Sahana Iyer: +1 and please keep the schema compatible with GitHub Actions artifacts size constraints. If env capture includes giant dumps it will blow up. (Put big stuff in separate artifacts and reference by URL/path.)

Claire Dubois: ok, let’s go point by point. Starting w/ versioning: ADR currently proposes
- `manifest_schema_version`: "0.1.0" (semver)
- `results_schema_version`: "0.1.0"
- `workload_suite_version`: "2026q1.v1" (string)
Feedback: are we ok with semver strings? Or do we want integer + changelog?

Noah Patel: Semver strings are fine, but define what increments mean. E.g. major = breaking validation/backwards incompatible, minor = new optional fields, patch = doc/typo.

Allison Grant: +1. And define a migration rule: when we bump major, we need a converter script in repo (even if best-effort).

Rina Kobayashi: From harness: semver string is easiest. We can embed it in filenames too (`run_manifest.v0.1.0.json`) but not required.

Sean Gallagher: Please keep schema versions independent from workload suite versions. Workload suite will change often; schema shouldn’t.

Claire Dubois: Next: workload params. Current sketch:
```
"workload": {
  "id": "chat_streaming",
  "variant": "prod_mix_v1",
  "mode": "streaming",
  "seed": 12345,
  "request_mix": {
    "chat": 0.65,
    "tool": 0.20,
    "summarize": 0.10,
    "other": 0.05
  },
  "distributions": {
    "prompt_tokens": {"p50": 180, "p90": 900, "max": 4096},
    "output_tokens": {"p50": 220, "p90": 800, "max": 2048}
  },
  "concurrency": {"target": 64, "ramp_seconds": 90}
}
```
Question: do we allow arbitrary keys under `distributions`? Or do we standardize on bins/histograms only?

Jonas Weber: For representativeness we often need bins, not just p50/p90/max. p50/p90 hides multimodal mixes. I’d propose we support two forms:
- `histogram_bins`: explicit bin edges + weights
- `summary`: p50/p90/max for quick glance

Logan Wright: From pipeline, histogram bins are fine but please keep the structure consistent. Something like:
`"histogram": {"bins": "0-32,33-64,...", "weights": "0.1,0.2,..."}` is ugly. Better:
```
"prompt_tokens": {
  "type": "histogram",
  "bins": "0-64;65-128;129-256",
  "weights": "0.12;0.34;0.54"
}
```
But I know ADR wants JSON not CSV strings…

Allison Grant: We can keep arrays in JSON but remember our file generation constraints? (Wait—this is internal, arrays are fine in the manifest itself. The Slack-exported file is separate.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 arrays are fine in the manifest in GitHub. In this thread we’re just agreeing on structure.

Mateo Alvarez: Standardize what matters to perf: prompt token distribution, output token distribution, and arrival process. Everything else optional.

Sean Gallagher: Also need to specify caching scenario. There was a whole thread about cold vs warm. I’d like explicit:
- `cache_mode: cold|warm_prefix|warm_kv` (or similar)
Otherwise results are incomparable.

Connor O'Brien: +1, and if cache_mode impacts gating thresholds it must be a tag in results.

Claire Dubois: Great call. ADR has a footnote but not explicit field. Adding `cache_mode`.

Claire Dubois: Now env capture. Proposed required:
- runtime git SHA + build id
- container image digest
- region + cluster
- GPU sku + count
- driver version
- CUDA version
- runtime flags (scheduler policy id)

Optional:
- GPU clocks locked? (yes/no + value)
- NUMA / CPU pinning
- kernel selection dump path
- tracing artifacts paths

Sean Gallagher: I’d push GPU clocks + power settings into “required” for baseline runs at least. Otherwise variance will be huge and we’ll waste weeks. It can be optional for developer local runs.

Sahana Iyer: How do we encode “required for baseline runs” vs “optional for local”? maybe `run_type: baseline|canary|local` and validator enforces more fields based on run_type.

Allison Grant: Please don’t make validator super complex. But we can do conditional requirements with JSON schema `if/then`. That’s acceptable.

Noah Patel: +1. Keep it simple: `run_intent: baseline` triggers strictness.

Rina Kobayashi: I can implement conditional schema checks in the validator easily (we’re using ajv). We already do this for “streaming requires stream metrics”.

Jonas Weber: Also for env capture: include model config that affects quality/perf: quantization profile, max context, tensor parallel degree, dtype, kv cache dtype, etc.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 and scheduler knobs. But we need a line somewhere; otherwise manifest becomes a dump of every config option. Maybe:
- `config_fingerprint`: hash of full config
- `config_effective`: include only the subset that impacts perf (documented)

Logan Wright: Hash is helpful for de-duping. But dashboards need human-readable fields. So include both hash + a curated set.

Claire Dubois: ok. Drafting: `effective_config` section with curated keys + `full_config_ref` as artifact link.

Allison Grant: Make sure the curated keys include measurement boundary, like:
- `ttft_definition`: `server_first_token_generated` vs `first_byte_on_wire`
- `tpot_definition`: `server_token_interval` (exclude client read)
If we don’t record that, people will “fix” bugs and accidentally change definitions.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. In harness we can set those constants and write them into manifest.

Connor O'Brien: For results: propose we output both raw samples (maybe capped) + aggregated percentiles? Or only percentiles?

Sean Gallagher: For gating, we need percentiles. For triage, raw samples help a lot (to see multimodal). But raw samples get big.

Sahana Iyer: Could do: results has percentiles + a separate `samples.parquet` artifact. Results includes URL. Keep JSON small.

Claire Dubois: Works. ADR already suggests results JSON should be <= ~1MB.

Noah Patel: Another subtlety: multi-model fallback. If the runtime falls back to another kernel/model variant, we should record that. Otherwise baseline might accidentally include fallback path.

Mateo Alvarez: That’s in PR-21519 (kernel selection dump). In manifest we can include `kernel_selection: {"mode": "record", "sample_rate": 0.05}` + results include summary counts.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a results field like `kernel_fallback_rate` and `top_fallback_reasons`. That will help catch silent regressions.

Allison Grant: Careful: “top reasons” is a list. But for JSON results, list is fine. For dashboards we might need it as tags? Might be too high-cardinality. Maybe keep it as a drilldown link.

Logan Wright: +1. High-cardinality reasons will explode metrics. Put detailed reasons in artifact, keep a single scalar in results (e.g., fallback_rate) and a boolean `fallback_occurred`.

Claire Dubois: Summarizing decisions from this thread:
- Versioning: semver strings for manifest/results schema. Define major/minor/patch semantics + provide converter for major bumps.
- Workload params: structured `workload` object; support histograms (bins + weights) AND summary stats; add explicit `cache_mode`.
- Env capture: add `run_intent` to allow conditional required fields; baseline intent requires clock/power settings.
- Config: include curated `effective_config` + `config_fingerprint` + reference to full config artifact.
- Measurement boundary: record TTFT/TPOT definitions in manifest.
- Results: percentiles in JSON; raw samples in separate artifact.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update ADR text + schema sketch accordingly today. If anyone objects, please reply in-thread by EOD tomorrow.

Sean Gallagher: Looks good. Please ping me when you add the baseline strictness part, I want to make sure we capture the exact fields SRE cares about (clocks, placement, isolation).

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a short section in ADR for “metrics tag cardinality guidelines” so people don’t try to put fallback reasons as tags.

Rina Kobayashi: ack. I’ll take an action item to update validator once ADR lands. (PR-21432 will need a follow-up.)

Connor O'Brien: Thanks. Once schema is stable-ish, I’ll wire the version fields into perf-canary gating config so we can refuse unknown majors.

Claire Dubois: Perfect. Closing loop after edits.

title: #eng-runtime: 1763261084
preview:
Claire Dubois: we need to settle canonical defs for the suite before we lock the manifest schema. main confusion: TTFT = “first token on wire” vs “first token produced” (server-side), and TPOT w/ or w/o client overhead. can we decide measurement boundaries today?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. harness can do either, but if we leave it ambiguous we’ll end up comparing apples/oranges across runs.

Sofia Ivanova: from runtime POV, I care about scheduler+decode behavior. “first token produced” (after queueing + prefill + first decode) is the clean signal.

Sean Gallagher: SRE POV: for release gating, we need something stable. client-side measurements can get noisy if the client can’t read fast enough, esp streaming.

Jonas Weber: also applied ML wants comparability across model variants. if TTFT includes network/client parsing, it’ll hide real runtime deltas.

Allison Grant: what’s the current SLO definition? our prod “ttft” dashboard is based on server instrumentation at the gateway (request_received -> first_token_flushed). not purely model.

Omar El-Sayed: right, and the “first_token_flushed” part matters because we’ve seen cases where token is generated but stuck behind buffering/flush behavior. tail pain.

Claire Dubois: so we have 3 candidates:
1) ttft_model: request_enqueued -> first_token_generated
2) ttft_on_wire: request_received_at_gateway -> first_token_flushed
3) ttft_client: client_send_complete -> first_token_byte_received

Rina Kobayashi: harness can capture #3 easily and (with headers) correlate to #2/#1 if runtime exports timestamps.

Sofia Ivanova: but which is THE metric for baseline tables? we can’t have 3 TTFTs everywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: propose: baseline “primary TTFT” should match what we can gate and what maps to prod SLO views. that’s #2. but we *must* also record #1 for attribution.

Sean Gallagher: +1. gating should use server-side. client-side is “debug only” because it depends on bench harness + region routing.

Jonas Weber: if we pick #2 as primary, need to be explicit about “request_received” boundary. eg do we include auth, request parsing, rate limit check? those do change occasionally.

Allison Grant: prod SLO includes gateway overhead by design. if baseline ignores it, we’ll keep shipping changes that look fine in perf suite but break SLO.

Sofia Ivanova: ok but for kernel/scheduler work, I don’t want noise from gateway changes. can we tag these layers separately?

Omar El-Sayed: we already have planned scheduler metrics: queue_wait_ms, batch_assembly_ms, prefill_ms, decode_ms. if we always record those, TTFT_on_wire becomes explainable.

Rina Kobayashi: on the TPOT question: folks keep calculating it as “(end_time - start_time)/num_tokens” with different start times.

Claire Dubois: yeah. and sometimes “client perceived” includes the time it takes the client to read the streaming chunks.

Sean Gallagher: we had a previous incident where client TPOT got worse because the harness did synchronous JSON parsing in the read loop. we don’t want that.

Rina Kobayashi: for the harness I can do:
- record raw byte arrival timestamps from the socket read, no JSON parse in the loop
- decode token boundaries based on SSE chunk boundaries (or length-delimited frames)
- compute tpot_client_wire = mean(inter-arrival between token chunks)

Sofia Ivanova: SSE chunk boundaries are not guaranteed “one chunk per token” though. we sometimes coalesce.

Omar El-Sayed: exactly. server can flush multiple tokens in one write depending on pacing config. so “client inter-arrival” is not stable unless we enforce pacing.

Jonas Weber: which we do plan to do for the representative chat workload (token pacing), right?

Rina Kobayashi: yes, but we still should define TPOT on server side by token emission events, not chunk emission events.

Allison Grant: do we have token emission timestamps in prod? (or only for perf runs)

Omar El-Sayed: we can emit token_event timestamps in perf mode (sampled), but for baseline suite it’s fine. otherwise we can approximate from decode loop stats.

Mateo Alvarez: decision framework:
- Primary metrics used for baseline comparisons + gating: server-side, not client.
- Also record “debug/e2e” metrics but not gating.

Claire Dubois: can we write the definitions down concretely? proposing:
TTFT (canonical) = t(first_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(request_received_at_gateway)
TPOT (canonical) = (t(last_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(first_token_flushed_at_gateway)) / (output_tokens - 1)
Throughput = output_tokens / (t(last_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(request_received_at_gateway))

Sofia Ivanova: for non-streaming endpoints, “first_token_flushed” == “first_byte_of_response”. do we still call it TTFT?

Rina Kobayashi: in harness output I can keep the name TTFT but annotate mode=streaming|non_streaming. (or store ttft_ms for both; semantics described in methodology doc)

Sean Gallagher: ok as long as doc is explicit: non-streaming TTFT behaves like TTFB.

Jonas Weber: and for embeddings there’s no tokens. so just latency_ms and qps/req_s.

Allison Grant: small nit: request_received_at_gateway should be AFTER connection reuse, no DNS/TLS. i.e., we require keepalive in the harness.

Rina Kobayashi: yes. harness uses warm connections, and we’ll document: exclude DNS + TCP + TLS by reusing sessions.

Claire Dubois: back to #1 vs #2. we still want model-only for attribution:
TTFT_model = t(first_token_generated) - t(request_enqueued)
TPOT_model = (t(last_token_generated) - t(first_token_generated)) / (output_tokens - 1)

Omar El-Sayed: request_enqueued should be “enter scheduler queue” not “arrived at gateway”. otherwise queue_wait_ms double counts.

Sofia Ivanova: yes: enqueue_ts = when scheduler accepts it into queue. (after admission control)

Mateo Alvarez: ok. we can live with two sets:
- ttft_ms / tpot_ms = gateway wire-level canonical
- ttft_model_ms / tpot_model_ms = runtime attribution
plus optional e2e_client_ttft_ms, e2e_client_tpot_ms as informational.

Sean Gallagher: and we should state explicitly: TPOT excludes client read/parse and excludes pauses due to client backpressure; if client stalls, we treat that as harness issue.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a guardrail: if we detect client read loop lagging (buffer grows / event loop blocked), we flag the run as invalid.

Allison Grant: can we align naming with metrics dictionary? prefer:
- ttft_ms (wire)
- tpot_ms (wire)
- ttft_server_ms (model)
- tpot_server_ms (model)

Sofia Ivanova: “server” is ambiguous (gateway vs runtime). maybe:
- ttft_wire_ms
- tpot_wire_ms
- ttft_model_ms
- tpot_model_ms

Claire Dubois: +1, clearer.

Jonas Weber: also need to define token count. output_tokens should be “generated tokens” excluding prompt. and for streaming, include the final token? (output_tokens-1 divisor)

Omar El-Sayed: standard: TPOT uses (N-1) intervals between N tokens. if N<2, TPOT is undefined; set null/NaN and omit from aggregates.

Rina Kobayashi: in results export I’ll emit tpot_* only when output_tokens >= 2, else empty.

Mateo Alvarez: sounds good. this is basically what we want in ADR-0172.

Claire Dubois: ok decision:
1) Canonical baseline + gating metrics: wire-level at gateway: ttft_wire_ms, tpot_wire_ms, tokens_per_sec, plus p50/p95/p99.
2) Required attribution metrics: runtime/model-level: ttft_model_ms, tpot_model_ms + queue_wait_ms + batch_assembly_ms + prefill_ms + decode_ms.
3) Optional: client e2e (not gating).
4) Connection setup excluded (keepalive required).

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please make sure methodology doc says “first token on wire = first token flushed by gateway” not “first token received by client”. that distinction keeps SRE sane.

Allison Grant: +1. I’ll sanity check with the SLO dashboard mapping doc. prod uses gateway_received -> first_token_flushed.

Rina Kobayashi: action items:
- I’ll update manifest schema fields + results exporter names to *_wire_ms and *_model_ms.
- add a note in harness: non-streaming TTFT == first_byte.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update methodology page + add examples.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll confirm we have the needed runtime timestamps behind perf flag and that we don’t add overhead in prod path.

Mateo Alvarez: locking this for Q1 baseline. any objections, speak now.

Sofia Ivanova: no objections.

Jonas Weber: good.

Sean Gallagher: good.

Allison Grant: good.
text:
Claire Dubois: we need to settle canonical defs for the suite before we lock the manifest schema. main confusion: TTFT = “first token on wire” vs “first token produced” (server-side), and TPOT w/ or w/o client overhead. can we decide measurement boundaries today?

Rina Kobayashi: +1. harness can do either, but if we leave it ambiguous we’ll end up comparing apples/oranges across runs.

Sofia Ivanova: from runtime POV, I care about scheduler+decode behavior. “first token produced” (after queueing + prefill + first decode) is the clean signal.

Sean Gallagher: SRE POV: for release gating, we need something stable. client-side measurements can get noisy if the client can’t read fast enough, esp streaming.

Jonas Weber: also applied ML wants comparability across model variants. if TTFT includes network/client parsing, it’ll hide real runtime deltas.

Allison Grant: what’s the current SLO definition? our prod “ttft” dashboard is based on server instrumentation at the gateway (request_received -> first_token_flushed). not purely model.

Omar El-Sayed: right, and the “first_token_flushed” part matters because we’ve seen cases where token is generated but stuck behind buffering/flush behavior. tail pain.

Claire Dubois: so we have 3 candidates:
1) ttft_model: request_enqueued -> first_token_generated
2) ttft_on_wire: request_received_at_gateway -> first_token_flushed
3) ttft_client: client_send_complete -> first_token_byte_received

Rina Kobayashi: harness can capture #3 easily and (with headers) correlate to #2/#1 if runtime exports timestamps.

Sofia Ivanova: but which is THE metric for baseline tables? we can’t have 3 TTFTs everywhere.

Mateo Alvarez: propose: baseline “primary TTFT” should match what we can gate and what maps to prod SLO views. that’s #2. but we *must* also record #1 for attribution.

Sean Gallagher: +1. gating should use server-side. client-side is “debug only” because it depends on bench harness + region routing.

Jonas Weber: if we pick #2 as primary, need to be explicit about “request_received” boundary. eg do we include auth, request parsing, rate limit check? those do change occasionally.

Allison Grant: prod SLO includes gateway overhead by design. if baseline ignores it, we’ll keep shipping changes that look fine in perf suite but break SLO.

Sofia Ivanova: ok but for kernel/scheduler work, I don’t want noise from gateway changes. can we tag these layers separately?

Omar El-Sayed: we already have planned scheduler metrics: queue_wait_ms, batch_assembly_ms, prefill_ms, decode_ms. if we always record those, TTFT_on_wire becomes explainable.

Rina Kobayashi: on the TPOT question: folks keep calculating it as “(end_time - start_time)/num_tokens” with different start times.

Claire Dubois: yeah. and sometimes “client perceived” includes the time it takes the client to read the streaming chunks.

Sean Gallagher: we had a previous incident where client TPOT got worse because the harness did synchronous JSON parsing in the read loop. we don’t want that.

Rina Kobayashi: for the harness I can do:
- record raw byte arrival timestamps from the socket read, no JSON parse in the loop
- decode token boundaries based on SSE chunk boundaries (or length-delimited frames)
- compute tpot_client_wire = mean(inter-arrival between token chunks)

Sofia Ivanova: SSE chunk boundaries are not guaranteed “one chunk per token” though. we sometimes coalesce.

Omar El-Sayed: exactly. server can flush multiple tokens in one write depending on pacing config. so “client inter-arrival” is not stable unless we enforce pacing.

Jonas Weber: which we do plan to do for the representative chat workload (token pacing), right?

Rina Kobayashi: yes, but we still should define TPOT on server side by token emission events, not chunk emission events.

Allison Grant: do we have token emission timestamps in prod? (or only for perf runs)

Omar El-Sayed: we can emit token_event timestamps in perf mode (sampled), but for baseline suite it’s fine. otherwise we can approximate from decode loop stats.

Mateo Alvarez: decision framework:
- Primary metrics used for baseline comparisons + gating: server-side, not client.
- Also record “debug/e2e” metrics but not gating.

Claire Dubois: can we write the definitions down concretely? proposing:
TTFT (canonical) = t(first_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(request_received_at_gateway)
TPOT (canonical) = (t(last_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(first_token_flushed_at_gateway)) / (output_tokens - 1)
Throughput = output_tokens / (t(last_token_flushed_at_gateway) - t(request_received_at_gateway))

Sofia Ivanova: for non-streaming endpoints, “first_token_flushed” == “first_byte_of_response”. do we still call it TTFT?

Rina Kobayashi: in harness output I can keep the name TTFT but annotate mode=streaming|non_streaming. (or store ttft_ms for both; semantics described in methodology doc)

Sean Gallagher: ok as long as doc is explicit: non-streaming TTFT behaves like TTFB.

Jonas Weber: and for embeddings there’s no tokens. so just latency_ms and qps/req_s.

Allison Grant: small nit: request_received_at_gateway should be AFTER connection reuse, no DNS/TLS. i.e., we require keepalive in the harness.

Rina Kobayashi: yes. harness uses warm connections, and we’ll document: exclude DNS + TCP + TLS by reusing sessions.

Claire Dubois: back to #1 vs #2. we still want model-only for attribution:
TTFT_model = t(first_token_generated) - t(request_enqueued)
TPOT_model = (t(last_token_generated) - t(first_token_generated)) / (output_tokens - 1)

Omar El-Sayed: request_enqueued should be “enter scheduler queue” not “arrived at gateway”. otherwise queue_wait_ms double counts.

Sofia Ivanova: yes: enqueue_ts = when scheduler accepts it into queue. (after admission control)

Mateo Alvarez: ok. we can live with two sets:
- ttft_ms / tpot_ms = gateway wire-level canonical
- ttft_model_ms / tpot_model_ms = runtime attribution
plus optional e2e_client_ttft_ms, e2e_client_tpot_ms as informational.

Sean Gallagher: and we should state explicitly: TPOT excludes client read/parse and excludes pauses due to client backpressure; if client stalls, we treat that as harness issue.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a guardrail: if we detect client read loop lagging (buffer grows / event loop blocked), we flag the run as invalid.

Allison Grant: can we align naming with metrics dictionary? prefer:
- ttft_ms (wire)
- tpot_ms (wire)
- ttft_server_ms (model)
- tpot_server_ms (model)

Sofia Ivanova: “server” is ambiguous (gateway vs runtime). maybe:
- ttft_wire_ms
- tpot_wire_ms
- ttft_model_ms
- tpot_model_ms

Claire Dubois: +1, clearer.

Jonas Weber: also need to define token count. output_tokens should be “generated tokens” excluding prompt. and for streaming, include the final token? (output_tokens-1 divisor)

Omar El-Sayed: standard: TPOT uses (N-1) intervals between N tokens. if N<2, TPOT is undefined; set null/NaN and omit from aggregates.

Claire Dubois: ok decision:
1) Canonical baseline + gating metrics: wire-level at gateway: ttft_wire_ms, tpot_wire_ms, tokens_per_sec, plus p50/p95/p99.
2) Required attribution metrics: runtime/model-level: ttft_model_ms, tpot_model_ms + queue_wait_ms + batch_assembly_ms + prefill_ms + decode_ms.
3) Optional: client e2e (not gating).
4) Connection setup excluded (keepalive required).

Allison Grant: +1. I’ll sanity check with the SLO dashboard mapping doc. prod uses gateway_received -> first_token_flushed.

- add a note in harness: non-streaming TTFT == first_byte.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll confirm we have the needed runtime timestamps behind perf flag and that we don’t add overhead in prod path.

title: #eng-runtime: 1763184027
preview:
Talia Benson: Kicking off the “hardware tuning profiles pack” workstream. Goal: ship a versioned pack of safe-by-default runtime configs tuned per GPU SKU (H100 / L40S initially) + rollout/rollback guardrails. Can we confirm owners + where the draft schema doc lives?

Talia Benson: Proposed initial scope for v1:
- Profile schema + validation + loader + override order
- H100 + L40S tuned presets (scheduler/batching, kernel-selection toggles, KV cache / memory behavior)
- Benchmark suite updates + workload matrix + perf canary gates
- Docs: expected behavior + failure modes + rollout runbook
Non-goals (v1): new kernels from scratch, bespoke per-customer tuning.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: for scope. Kernels team can own H100 kernel-selection heuristics + any microbench-driven toggles we’re confident are safe to freeze. Suggest we keep “adaptive” knobs out of the profile unless we have hard guardrails.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling side ok. We can propose 2 modes per SKU (balanced vs throughput-first) but we need to be explicit about tail latency impact. Also please add “streaming stability” to acceptance checks.

Ethan Park: Schema doc is in Confluence draft (WIP): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENGSRV/Profile+Schema+and+Compatibility+Rules
I’ll take schema + compatibility rules + validation checklist. Benji can own loader + precedence if that works.

Benji Okafor: Yep, I can own runtime loader + override order + safe fallback behavior. We should nail down precedence early (fleet default vs per-pool vs env var vs per-route) so we don’t ship something we’ll regret.

Noah Patel: +1. Also suggest we log the effective profile selection at startup + expose a debug endpoint for oncall. Otherwise we’ll spend weeks arguing “which profile was actually applied” in incidents.

Rina Kobayashi: Perf suite: Claire + I can own. We’ll need a workload matrix that includes:
- seq len buckets (prompt + completion)
- concurrency sweep
- streaming vs non-streaming
- at least 2 model families (one dense, one MoE if available)
We can produce baseline curves per GPU before we start tweaking knobs.

Claire Dubois: Agree. Also we should pin prompt sets + runner versions for reproducibility. I’ll draft the workload matrix today and link it here.

Irene Choi: Infra angle: initial target SKUs = H100 + L40S. B200 should be gated/experimental until we have stable access + we’ve validated driver/CUDA constraints. We can plan staged rollout by pool/region once perf canary gates are in.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 on gating B200. Fleet detection is messy too (different providers stringify SKUs differently). Please build normalization into detection logic.

Mateo Alvarez: Aligning with leadership expectations: ship H100/L40S profiles first, treat B200 as “experimental behind a flag” with explicit rollback plan. Don’t block the pack release on B200.

Omar El-Sayed: I can help on KV cache / memory behavior presets. L40S needs strict budgeting defaults + OOM prevention guardrails; H100 can be more aggressive but we should still define max cache footprint + fragmentation mitigation.

Elliot Price: Docs: I can pair with whoever owns each profile to capture “expected behavior” vs “guarantees” (we should be careful in wording). Once schema is stable, I’ll start a doc outline for reference pages per SKU.

Talia Benson: Great. Capturing owners:
- Schema/compat: Ethan
- Loader/override order: Benji (with Noah advising on debug/observability)
- Kernel tuning: Hiro (Grace/Yuki/Isabella contributing)
- Scheduling presets: Sofia (Lena/Marcus contributing)
- Perf suite + workload matrix: Rina + Claire
- KV cache/memory presets: Omar (with Samir)
- Rollout sponsor: Irene + Vanessa
- Docs program: Elliot

Talia Benson: Decision check: v1 targets H100 + L40S. B200 is gated/experimental only. Everyone ok? (react with :thumbsup:)

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup:

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: :thumbsup:

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup:

Rina Kobayashi: :thumbsup:

Claire Dubois: :thumbsup:

Irene Choi: :thumbsup:

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup:

Talia Benson: Next steps:
1) Ethan posts schema WIP sections + open questions by EOW
2) Claire posts workload matrix draft today; Rina adds runner details
3) Benji drafts precedence order proposal + fallback behavior
4) Hiro/Sofia propose initial “safe knobs to freeze” list per SKU
We’ll do a 30m weekly sync; calendar invite coming.

Vanessa Ortiz: Please also add an action item: “list of SKU string variants” once we have first logs from each pool. Happy to provide what we’ve seen in fleet telemetry.

Noah Patel: One more: we should define acceptance metrics now (p50/p95 latency, tokens/sec, OOM rate, retry rate) and what counts as a regression. Otherwise perf canary gates will be bikesheddy.

Talia Benson: Good call. I’ll draft success metrics + regression thresholds strawman and share in thread before weekly. (also adding Vanessa’s SKU variants item).
