title: #eng-runtime: 1763167421
preview:
Mateo Alvarez: kicking off the Q1’26 perf baseline + representative workload suite work. want to get owners + logistics locked today so we can start runs next week.

Mateo Alvarez: umbrella Linear is here: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4821/q1-2026-perf-baseline-kickoff-and-scope

Mateo Alvarez: initial scope (v0): top models (llama/qwen/mistral families), regions us-east + eu-west, SKUs H100/A100/L40S. workloads: chat streaming, long-prefill summarization, tool calling (bursty multi-turn), embeddings.

Talia Benson: :wave: I can run the weekly cadence + notes. what’s the target day/time for the standing sync?

Mateo Alvarez: thinking Tue 10am PT, 45 min. owners please confirm.

Rina Kobayashi: confirmed. I’ll own harness + run orchestration. ENG-4822 is the schema work (manifest/results). will post a checklist later today.

Claire Dubois: confirming on methodology. I’ll write the measurement boundaries + noise model assumptions doc, and we’ll align w/ perf regression policy.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduling side: I’ll own baseline scheduler config + the instrumentation requests (queue wait / batch assembly / load shed). already started a doc w/ proposed counters.

Hiro Tanaka: kernels: I’ll cover kernel auto-selection rules + known cliffs. Yuki is out today but I’ll loop them in on kernel pinning questions.

Sean Gallagher: SRE: I’ll take benchmark fleet stability / run hygiene. strong preference we define “standard warmup + isolation steps” before we burn too many GPU hours.

Sergio Costa: dashboards: I can wire the baseline dashboard panels once we know the exact metric tags + where results live. need a stable run naming convention.

Connor O'Brien: release gating: I’ll coordinate perf-canary integration once the suite is runnable end-to-end. we’ll need a “dry run” phase before any hard gate.

Kira Thompson: I can take runbook / docs (reruns, triage, how to repro locally vs in the fleet). just point me at the decisions once they’re made.

Jonas Weber: applied ML/evals here. I’ll help validate workload representativeness + add the lightweight quality spotcheck hook (must-not-break set).

Mateo Alvarez: great. two immediate logistics questions:
1) where do baseline run artifacts live (manifests, raw metrics, traces)?
2) what’s the canonical “source of truth” doc set (confluence vs drive vs repo docs)?

Rina Kobayashi: for artifacts, my proposal:
- manifests + summarized metrics in `benchmark-lab` repo outputs (JSON/Parquet) pushed to `s3://redwood-benchmark-artifacts/prod/perf-suite/…`
- each run writes `run_manifest.json`, `results.json`, and optional `traces/` directory (nsys, chrome trace, scheduler logs)
- we store a short index row in a “run registry” table (so dashboards can query by tags)

Sean Gallagher: +1 S3. please avoid “everyone uploads to Drive” for primary artifacts; Drive is fine for analysis scratch but not the source.

Sergio Costa: dashboards are happiest if there’s a single table-like thing (even if it’s just parquet w/ partitions) keyed by `{run_id, model, region, sku, workload, runtime_sha, manifest_version}`.

Claire Dubois: doc-wise: I’d like confluence to hold the frozen methodology + baseline report, but repo should have the runnable spec (workloads) + schema in version control.

Mateo Alvarez: makes sense. can we set a single folder path convention now?

Rina Kobayashi: yep. propose:
`perf-suite/v1/{year}-{month}/run_id={uuid}/…`
and include a `baseline_anchor` tag for the baseline freeze runs.

Marcus Reed: jumping in—please include the runtime build ID and container digest in the manifest. we’ve been burned by “same SHA, different image” before.

Rina Kobayashi: already in my draft. manifest will capture:
- runtime git SHA
- container digest
- driver/CUDA versions
- region/zone
- GPU SKU + count
- scheduler config blob hash
- workload seed + params

Allison Grant: observability note: let’s align metric names w/ existing prod conventions (ttft_ms, tpot_ms, tokens_per_sec, queue_wait_ms) so dashboards don’t fork.

Aisha Bello: +1. also please explicitly define TTFT boundary for streaming (server first token generated vs first token on wire). SLO dashboards currently use “first token on wire”.

Claire Dubois: I’ll capture that in the methodology doc + propose we report both if needed (gen vs wire). but we need a single gating metric.

Mateo Alvarez: for this kickoff, let’s pick a default:
- TTFT = first token on wire (server-side timestamp), excluding client read delay
- TPOT = avg per-output-token time over streamed tokens (server-side), excluding network
if anyone strongly disagrees, flag now.

Sofia Ivanova: ok w/ that. we should add scheduler timestamps so we can break down TTFT into queue_wait + batch_assembly + prefill.

Hiro Tanaka: ok. for kernel dumps, we’ll want to annotate the prefill length regime + selected kernel + fallback reason. can be sampled (not every request).

Logan Wright: data pipeline question: who’s owning the “run registry” table? happy to help but need a clear interface.

Rina Kobayashi: I can own the initial registry writer in the harness. might need your help making it queryable in our standard analytics stack.

Logan Wright: cool. loop me in once schema is stable. if it’s parquet in S3 w/ partitioning, we can hook it into the existing query layer pretty quickly.

Talia Benson: re cadence: Tue 10am PT works. I’ll send a calendar invite + agenda doc (owners, decisions, run status).

Mateo Alvarez: action items:
- Rina: post artifact storage spec + run naming convention in ENG-4821
- Claire: start methodology doc outline + measurement boundaries (TTFT/TPOT) and link in ENG-4821
- Sean: propose benchmark fleet “standard operating procedure” (warmup, clock settings if any, isolation)
- Sergio/Allison/Aisha: agree on metric tags + dashboard minimum viable panels
- Connor: draft gating phases (PR vs nightly vs release branch) but no hard gate until noise model validated

Linear Bot: ENG-4821 updated by Mateo Alvarez

Kira Thompson: once those docs exist, I’ll start the runbook skeleton: “how to rerun baseline”, “how to triage regression”, “how to attach traces”.

Sean Gallagher: quick note: we should reserve benchmark fleet windows for the baseline sweep. if Neha already approved budget, we still need an internal booking process to avoid contention.

Mateo Alvarez: yep. I’ll ping Neha + infra today and we’ll get a reservation doc. Sean can you drop a strawman for required GPU-hours (H100/A100/L40S) based on your earlier estimates?

Sean Gallagher: will do. I’ll post in-thread later today.

Jonas Weber: also: please don’t forget “cold vs warm cache” decision. telemetry says a meaningful fraction of prod has prefix cache hits; we probably need both in reporting.

Mateo Alvarez: agreed, but let’s not block kickoff. we’ll run cold by default for first pass, and add warm scenario once caching knobs are stable. Jonas/Claire can you propose how to define “warm” reproducibly?

Claire Dubois: yep. definition should be “fixed cache priming sequence + fixed seed” (no real prompt content). will draft.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a harness flag: `--cache_mode=cold|warm` with warm executing a deterministic priming phase before measurement.

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, I’ll treat cache_mode as a top-level filter so folks don’t mix the distributions.

Mateo Alvarez: perfect. thanks all—let’s keep updates on ENG-4821 and use this thread for quick decisions. :thumbsup: (reactions: :thumbsup: from Rina Kobayashi, Sofia Ivanova, Sean Gallagher, Claire Dubois)
text:
Mateo Alvarez: kicking off the Q1’26 perf baseline + representative workload suite work. want to get owners + logistics locked today so we can start runs next week.

Mateo Alvarez: umbrella Linear is here: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4821/q1-2026-perf-baseline-kickoff-and-scope

Mateo Alvarez: initial scope (v0): top models (llama/qwen/mistral families), regions us-east + eu-west, SKUs H100/A100/L40S. workloads: chat streaming, long-prefill summarization, tool calling (bursty multi-turn), embeddings.

Talia Benson: :wave: I can run the weekly cadence + notes. what’s the target day/time for the standing sync?

Mateo Alvarez: thinking Tue 10am PT, 45 min. owners please confirm.

Rina Kobayashi: confirmed. I’ll own harness + run orchestration. ENG-4822 is the schema work (manifest/results). will post a checklist later today.

Claire Dubois: confirming on methodology. I’ll write the measurement boundaries + noise model assumptions doc, and we’ll align w/ perf regression policy.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduling side: I’ll own baseline scheduler config + the instrumentation requests (queue wait / batch assembly / load shed). already started a doc w/ proposed counters.

Hiro Tanaka: kernels: I’ll cover kernel auto-selection rules + known cliffs. Yuki is out today but I’ll loop them in on kernel pinning questions.

Sean Gallagher: SRE: I’ll take benchmark fleet stability / run hygiene. strong preference we define “standard warmup + isolation steps” before we burn too many GPU hours.

Sergio Costa: dashboards: I can wire the baseline dashboard panels once we know the exact metric tags + where results live. need a stable run naming convention.

Connor O'Brien: release gating: I’ll coordinate perf-canary integration once the suite is runnable end-to-end. we’ll need a “dry run” phase before any hard gate.

Kira Thompson: I can take runbook / docs (reruns, triage, how to repro locally vs in the fleet). just point me at the decisions once they’re made.

Jonas Weber: applied ML/evals here. I’ll help validate workload representativeness + add the lightweight quality spotcheck hook (must-not-break set).

Mateo Alvarez: great. two immediate logistics questions:
1) where do baseline run artifacts live (manifests, raw metrics, traces)?
2) what’s the canonical “source of truth” doc set (confluence vs drive vs repo docs)?

Rina Kobayashi: for artifacts, my proposal:
- manifests + summarized metrics in `benchmark-lab` repo outputs (JSON/Parquet) pushed to `s3://redwood-benchmark-artifacts/prod/perf-suite/…`
- each run writes `run_manifest.json`, `results.json`, and optional `traces/` directory (nsys, chrome trace, scheduler logs)
- we store a short index row in a “run registry” table (so dashboards can query by tags)

Sean Gallagher: +1 S3. please avoid “everyone uploads to Drive” for primary artifacts; Drive is fine for analysis scratch but not the source.

Sergio Costa: dashboards are happiest if there’s a single table-like thing (even if it’s just parquet w/ partitions) keyed by `{run_id, model, region, sku, workload, runtime_sha, manifest_version}`.

Claire Dubois: doc-wise: I’d like confluence to hold the frozen methodology + baseline report, but repo should have the runnable spec (workloads) + schema in version control.

Mateo Alvarez: makes sense. can we set a single folder path convention now?

Rina Kobayashi: yep. propose:
`perf-suite/v1/{year}-{month}/run_id={uuid}/…`
and include a `baseline_anchor` tag for the baseline freeze runs.

Marcus Reed: jumping in—please include the runtime build ID and container digest in the manifest. we’ve been burned by “same SHA, different image” before.

Rina Kobayashi: already in my draft. manifest will capture:
- runtime git SHA
- container digest
- driver/CUDA versions
- region/zone
- GPU SKU + count
- scheduler config blob hash
- workload seed + params

Allison Grant: observability note: let’s align metric names w/ existing prod conventions (ttft_ms, tpot_ms, tokens_per_sec, queue_wait_ms) so dashboards don’t fork.

Aisha Bello: +1. also please explicitly define TTFT boundary for streaming (server first token generated vs first token on wire). SLO dashboards currently use “first token on wire”.

Claire Dubois: I’ll capture that in the methodology doc + propose we report both if needed (gen vs wire). but we need a single gating metric.

Mateo Alvarez: for this kickoff, let’s pick a default:
- TTFT = first token on wire (server-side timestamp), excluding client read delay
- TPOT = avg per-output-token time over streamed tokens (server-side), excluding network
if anyone strongly disagrees, flag now.

Sofia Ivanova: ok w/ that. we should add scheduler timestamps so we can break down TTFT into queue_wait + batch_assembly + prefill.

Hiro Tanaka: ok. for kernel dumps, we’ll want to annotate the prefill length regime + selected kernel + fallback reason. can be sampled (not every request).

Logan Wright: data pipeline question: who’s owning the “run registry” table? happy to help but need a clear interface.

Rina Kobayashi: I can own the initial registry writer in the harness. might need your help making it queryable in our standard analytics stack.

Logan Wright: cool. loop me in once schema is stable. if it’s parquet in S3 w/ partitioning, we can hook it into the existing query layer pretty quickly.

Talia Benson: re cadence: Tue 10am PT works. I’ll send a calendar invite + agenda doc (owners, decisions, run status).

Mateo Alvarez: action items:
- Rina: post artifact storage spec + run naming convention in ENG-4821
- Claire: start methodology doc outline + measurement boundaries (TTFT/TPOT) and link in ENG-4821
- Sean: propose benchmark fleet “standard operating procedure” (warmup, clock settings if any, isolation)
- Sergio/Allison/Aisha: agree on metric tags + dashboard minimum viable panels
- Connor: draft gating phases (PR vs nightly vs release branch) but no hard gate until noise model validated

Sean Gallagher: quick note: we should reserve benchmark fleet windows for the baseline sweep. if Neha already approved budget, we still need an internal booking process to avoid contention.

Mateo Alvarez: yep. I’ll ping Neha + infra today and we’ll get a reservation doc. Sean can you drop a strawman for required GPU-hours (H100/A100/L40S) based on your earlier estimates?

Sean Gallagher: will do. I’ll post in-thread later today.

Jonas Weber: also: please don’t forget “cold vs warm cache” decision. telemetry says a meaningful fraction of prod has prefix cache hits; we probably need both in reporting.

Mateo Alvarez: agreed, but let’s not block kickoff. we’ll run cold by default for first pass, and add warm scenario once caching knobs are stable. Jonas/Claire can you propose how to define “warm” reproducibly?

Claire Dubois: yep. definition should be “fixed cache priming sequence + fixed seed” (no real prompt content). will draft.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a harness flag: `--cache_mode=cold|warm` with warm executing a deterministic priming phase before measurement.

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, I’ll treat cache_mode as a top-level filter so folks don’t mix the distributions.

title: #eng-runtime: 1763080129
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: digging into yesterday’s mixed-workload behavior (long prompt prefill + short chat) and it looks like our backpressure thresholds are too forgiving for long-prefill. tail latency shoots up *before* admission control kicks in.

Julian Moore: do we have numbers? was this us-east canary or eu-west?

Sofia Ivanova: us-east canary. from the runtime side: when we get a couple of 12–20k prompt requests, they monopolize prefill slots and we start building scheduler lag for everyone.

Sofia Ivanova: quick snapshot from metrics (5m window during burst):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95` went 45ms -> 650ms
- `runtime.queue_depth` flat-ish at ~40–60 (so the queue depth trigger didn’t fire)
- `runtime.prefill_inflight` pegged at max (8)
- `ttft_ms_p95` for “normal” chat (1–2k prompts) went 900ms -> 3.8s

Vikram Singh: that lines up with what routing saw. queue depth wasn’t huge, but tokens-in-flight was. we were gating on queue depth and shedding best-effort too late.

Robert King: +1. capacity signal right now is basically `queue_depth` and `gpu_busy`. but gpu_busy stays high even in “healthy” batching, so it’s noisy. scheduler lag looks like a better leading indicator.

Lena Fischer: also note: long prefill requests don’t show up as “queue depth” because they’re *admitted* and then just occupy the prefill stage longer. so admission control based on queue depth misses the pain.

Mateo Alvarez: so we need a new trigger or adjust thresholds. what’s the minimal change? (don’t want to overfit)

Sofia Ivanova: proposal: lower backpressure threshold when the request mix is “long prompt heavy”. basically a guardrail on prefill occupancy.

Sofia Ivanova: concrete idea:
- today: backpressure starts when `queue_depth > 120` for 3s (example), and shedding starts at `queue_depth > 200`
- new: also start backpressure when `prefill_inflight >= (max_prefill_inflight - 1)` *and* `scheduler_lag_ms_p95 > 250ms` for 5s
- and for shedding best-effort: if `prefill_inflight == max` and `scheduler_lag_ms_p95 > 500ms` for 10s

Julian Moore: we have per-route info on “this is long prompt”?

Sofia Ivanova: not reliably at runtime without parsing, but we have prompt token estimate in the request metadata (from gateway) on the hot routes now. it’s in the envelope. (we already use it for some batching heuristics)

Peter Holtz: if we wire prompt token estimate into the runtime scheduler, can we avoid per-request high-cardinality tags? SRE is going to scream if we start emitting “prompt_len=12345”

Julian Moore: yeah we should bucket it. like `prompt_len_bucket={0-1k,1-4k,4-8k,8k+}` only.

Lena Fischer: +1 buckets. also, I’d prefer we *don’t* fully rely on gateway estimate for the trigger. can we just use prefill occupancy + lag? that’s intrinsic.

Vikram Singh: routing/policy wants deterministic reasons. if we shed, we need to explain “overload:prefill_saturation” vs “overload:queue_depth”. that’s fine.

Sofia Ivanova: yep. no need for prompt length in the trigger. prompt length comes in when we decide who to backpressure first (best-effort + very long prompts) if we want.

Mateo Alvarez: what’s the risk of oscillation? lag spikes can be noisy.

Sofia Ivanova: we’d add hysteresis (time-based) and release threshold lower than engage threshold. e.g.
- engage when `lag_p95 > 500ms` for 10s and prefill full
- release when `lag_p95 < 250ms` for 30s OR prefill_inflight <= max-2

Julian Moore: can you share the actual timeline from the burst? would help tune.

Sofia Ivanova: pasted from my notes (times relative):
- T+0: 6 long-prefill requests arrive (enterprise + best-effort mixed)
- T+8s: prefill_inflight hits 8/8
- T+20s: lag p95 crosses 300ms
- T+35s: ttft p95 starts climbing rapidly
- T+60s: queue_depth finally crosses 120 (too late)
- T+75s: routing starts throttling best-effort, but enterprise already impacted

Robert King: that’s pretty clear. gating on queue depth is just late for this failure mode.

Peter Holtz: also, this maps to the incident thread where we saw “flat queue depth but tail latency rising” and couldn’t explain it quickly.

Lena Fischer: implementation-wise: we already compute scheduler lag. adding a boolean “prefill_saturated” signal exported to routing would be cheap.

Vikram Singh: if you can export `prefill_saturated` + lag, routing can treat it as capacity pressure and preserve enterprise budget (shed best-effort earlier). that’s exactly what hot-route protection wants.

Mateo Alvarez: action items?

Sofia Ivanova: I can take a small PR:
1) add `runtime.prefill_saturated` gauge (0/1) and `runtime.prefill_inflight` already exists
2) add `runtime.backpressure_mode` enum-ish metric (careful) or just counters when we enter/exit
3) implement new admission guard: when saturated+lag, slow accept for best-effort queue first

Julian Moore: for (2) please don’t do free-form enum labels. maybe `runtime.backpressure_enter_total{reason=...}` with a small bounded set: `queue_depth`, `prefill_saturation`, `manual`.

Peter Holtz: +1. also add a log line in the admission decision path with reason and thresholds (sampled).

Lena Fischer: do we need to adjust thresholds specifically for “long prompts”? or are we just adding the missing signal?

Sofia Ivanova: mostly missing signal. but I also want to tweak the existing “prefill max” from 8 to 6 under mixed workloads. long-prefill concurrency of 8 is just too high; it starves decode stage.

Mateo Alvarez: lowering max prefill concurrency is risky for throughput on pure prefill workloads (embeddings-ish). can we make it conditional?

Sofia Ivanova: conditional on observed decode queue starvation. we can do a simple heuristic: if `decode_queue_depth > X` and `prefill_inflight` high, clamp prefill concurrency down temporarily.

Vikram Singh: that’s getting close to scheduler policy changes. but even just exporting the saturation signal + earlier backpressure might be enough for this project.

Sofia Ivanova: agreed. I’ll keep the first PR to signals + trigger. leave the dynamic clamp for later unless we keep seeing it.

Julian Moore: any chance we can reproduce in load test? I’d like a deterministic scenario.

Sofia Ivanova: yes. scenario:
- 70% short chat (1–2k prompt, 256 out)
- 30% long prompt (12–16k prompt, 256 out)
- burst factor 3x for 90s
success criteria: enterprise chat ttft p95 stays < 1.5s and lag p95 < 300ms while best-effort gets 429s.

Robert King: if you put that in the runtime load harness, routing can reuse it in the game day.

Mateo Alvarez: ok. Sofia owns PR for runtime signals + new trigger. Vikram/Robert to wire into routing policy once signal is present. Peter please sanity-check metrics/cardinality before merge.

Peter Holtz: will do. also pls add to the runbook: “if queue depth looks fine, check scheduler lag + prefill saturation”.

Sofia Ivanova: ack. I’ll start a PR today. will ping when up for review.

Julian Moore: :eyes: when you have a diff, tag me for the metrics bits.

Lena Fischer: :thumbsup: and I’ll review the trigger logic/hysteresis.

Sofia Ivanova: thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1762319020
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: aligning on the runtime metrics export for hot-route protection. routing folks are asking for queue depth + scheduler lag signals but we *cannot* blow up cardinality again 🙃

Julian Moore: yep. current WIP has per-(tenant, route, model, pool) tags which is… not gonna fly.

Vikram Singh: +1. needs to be low overhead + bounded tags. data plane can’t do high-card label sets on every enqueue/dequeue.

Mateo Alvarez: can we agree on the exact “contract” metrics that routing consumes? if we lock that, runtime can aggregate accordingly.

Logan Wright: for observability standards: anything used for automated admission should be stable + low-card. think region + route_group + tier + pool at most.

Allison Grant: also please no raw `customer_id` / `api_key_hash` tags in metrics. if we need tenant-level debugging, do it via logs/traces sampled, not metrics.

Sofia Ivanova: ok, proposal:
- export 2 primary gauges per runtime shard:
  1) `runtime_queue_depth` (requests)
  2) `runtime_scheduler_lag_ms` (ms since eligible -> scheduled)
- plus optional histogram `runtime_wait_time_ms` (coarse buckets)
- tags limited to: `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `route_group`, `priority_class`

Lena Fischer: define `route_group` as the gateway/routing group name, not raw path. e.g. `chat_completions`, `embeddings`, `rerank`. keeps it bounded.

Robert King: routing side can map route -> route_group already. priority_class we’ll set to enterprise/best_effort/internal.

Julian Moore: what about model? scheduler lag differs a lot by model size.

Nadia Rahman: if we tag by model_id we’re dead. could do `model_family` or `compute_class` (small/med/large) but that’s still risk.

Logan Wright: recommendation: don’t tag by model at the runtime metric layer for the “admission” signals. instead, export per-pool metrics. pools are already model-constrained most of the time.

Vikram Singh: yep, pool is the right abstraction. pool cardinality is bounded + controlled by us.

Julian Moore: k. pool implies model mix anyway. but for mixed pools, lag signal might be noisy.

Sofia Ivanova: if we have truly mixed pools, we can also add a `workload_class` tag derived from max_seq_len bucket (short/long) *but only if it’s strictly a fixed enum*.

Allison Grant: careful: anything derived from user input tends to drift. keep it fixed buckets (e.g. `short`, `medium`, `long`) and document mapping.

Mateo Alvarez: do we need queue depth in “tokens” too? routing wants to avoid admitting long prompts when backlog is mostly long.

Lena Fischer: runtime can approximate “pending_tokens” but it’s more compute + has lots of edge cases.

Sofia Ivanova: let’s start with request queue depth + scheduler lag. add pending_tokens later if we prove it adds value.

Caleb Johnson: ingestion constraints: histograms are ok but keep bucket count small (<= 10). also prefer gauges/counters over high-frequency hist updates if possible.

Julian Moore: implementation detail: we currently have per-queue structures: per priority class, per route_group. if we expose per (priority_class, route_group, pool) we’re still bounded.

Logan Wright: what’s the upper bound? route_group ~ 6-10, priority_class 3, pool maybe ~ 20 per region. that’s 600 time series per region per metric. ok.

Nadia Rahman: plus cluster + shard id? please don’t add shard id as label.

Sofia Ivanova: agreed: no shard id tag. runtime will aggregate across shards before export if possible.

Julian Moore: we can do node-local aggregation and emit per node. but then you still get node label via infra (`instance`).

Allison Grant: infra labels happen, but we can avoid *explicit* shard labels. dashboards can aggregate `sum without(instance)`.

Vikram Singh: also: routing doesn’t want per-node, it wants region/pool-level signals. can we emit to a local aggregator (statsd/otel collector) that rolls up before routing reads it?

Robert King: routing service will consume from fleet signals API, not scrape prometheus directly. as long as fleet layer can aggregate to pool/region, we’re good.

Caleb Johnson: fleet signals pipeline can aggregate if the metric names + tags are consistent. but you need deterministic tag set.

Sofia Ivanova: ok let’s lock the tags:
- REQUIRED labels: `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `route_group`, `priority_class`
- FORBIDDEN labels: `tenant`, `api_key`, `model_id`, `request_id`, `path`, `user_agent`
- OPTIONAL (only if we see need): `workload_class` (short|medium|long)

Julian Moore: naming: do we prefer `scheduler_lag_ms` vs `scheduler_delay_ms`?

Allison Grant: we have a standard for “lag” as time since event. use `*_lag_ms`.

Logan Wright: +1. also add a counter for shed/backpressure events from runtime?

Lena Fischer: shedding happens in gateway/routing mainly. runtime does backpressure. we can export `runtime_backpressure_active` (0/1) per (pool, route_group, priority_class).

Mateo Alvarez: sounds useful for incident debugging.

Nadia Rahman: backpressure flag is fine. no per-reason explosion though.

Sofia Ivanova: reason codes will live at gateway (429). runtime just exports the activation bit + maybe a single enum in logs.

Julian Moore: posting a concrete snippet for review (OTel metrics):
```
name: runtime_queue_depth
unit: {requests}
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class

name: runtime_scheduler_lag_ms
unit: ms
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class

name: runtime_backpressure_active
unit: {bool}
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class
```

Vikram Singh: 👍 this is sane.

Robert King: routing will treat `scheduler_lag_ms` as primary admission input. queue depth is secondary.

Allison Grant: please add doc note: gauges should be computed as EWMA over 5s to avoid jitter causing routing flaps.

Sofia Ivanova: yes. we’ll smooth in runtime before export (5s window), and routing also has a 15s hold-down.

Logan Wright: and let’s add a quick cardinality test in CI (assert label keys). we got burned last time.

Julian Moore: can do. will add unit test to fail build if someone adds `tenant` label to these instruments.

Mateo Alvarez: ok, next steps:
- Julian: update PR to remove model_id/tenant labels + add smoothing
- Sofia/Lena: define `route_group` mapping in runtime (accept from routing header?)
- Logan/Allison: update observability standard doc with these metric names + label set

Sofia Ivanova: runtime can take `x-redwood-route-group` header from routing. fallback to `unknown` if missing.

Nadia Rahman: make sure `unknown` doesn’t get spammy (if header missing across traffic). alert if header missing rate > threshold.

Sofia Ivanova: good call. adding counter `runtime_route_group_missing_total` (no labels except region/cluster/pool) so we notice.

Allison Grant: cool. thanks all. keeping this thread as the canonical agreement for label set + names.

Julian Moore: will push updates today and ping in #eng-platform when ready.
text:
Sofia Ivanova: aligning on the runtime metrics export for hot-route protection. routing folks are asking for queue depth + scheduler lag signals but we *cannot* blow up cardinality again 🙃

Julian Moore: yep. current WIP has per-(tenant, route, model, pool) tags which is… not gonna fly.

Vikram Singh: +1. needs to be low overhead + bounded tags. data plane can’t do high-card label sets on every enqueue/dequeue.

Mateo Alvarez: can we agree on the exact “contract” metrics that routing consumes? if we lock that, runtime can aggregate accordingly.

Logan Wright: for observability standards: anything used for automated admission should be stable + low-card. think region + route_group + tier + pool at most.

Allison Grant: also please no raw `customer_id` / `api_key_hash` tags in metrics. if we need tenant-level debugging, do it via logs/traces sampled, not metrics.

Sofia Ivanova: ok, proposal:
- export 2 primary gauges per runtime shard:
  1) `runtime_queue_depth` (requests)
  2) `runtime_scheduler_lag_ms` (ms since eligible -> scheduled)
- plus optional histogram `runtime_wait_time_ms` (coarse buckets)
- tags limited to: `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `route_group`, `priority_class`

Lena Fischer: define `route_group` as the gateway/routing group name, not raw path. e.g. `chat_completions`, `embeddings`, `rerank`. keeps it bounded.

Robert King: routing side can map route -> route_group already. priority_class we’ll set to enterprise/best_effort/internal.

Julian Moore: what about model? scheduler lag differs a lot by model size.

Nadia Rahman: if we tag by model_id we’re dead. could do `model_family` or `compute_class` (small/med/large) but that’s still risk.

Logan Wright: recommendation: don’t tag by model at the runtime metric layer for the “admission” signals. instead, export per-pool metrics. pools are already model-constrained most of the time.

Vikram Singh: yep, pool is the right abstraction. pool cardinality is bounded + controlled by us.

Julian Moore: k. pool implies model mix anyway. but for mixed pools, lag signal might be noisy.

Sofia Ivanova: if we have truly mixed pools, we can also add a `workload_class` tag derived from max_seq_len bucket (short/long) *but only if it’s strictly a fixed enum*.

Allison Grant: careful: anything derived from user input tends to drift. keep it fixed buckets (e.g. `short`, `medium`, `long`) and document mapping.

Mateo Alvarez: do we need queue depth in “tokens” too? routing wants to avoid admitting long prompts when backlog is mostly long.

Lena Fischer: runtime can approximate “pending_tokens” but it’s more compute + has lots of edge cases.

Sofia Ivanova: let’s start with request queue depth + scheduler lag. add pending_tokens later if we prove it adds value.

Caleb Johnson: ingestion constraints: histograms are ok but keep bucket count small (<= 10). also prefer gauges/counters over high-frequency hist updates if possible.

Julian Moore: implementation detail: we currently have per-queue structures: per priority class, per route_group. if we expose per (priority_class, route_group, pool) we’re still bounded.

Logan Wright: what’s the upper bound? route_group ~ 6-10, priority_class 3, pool maybe ~ 20 per region. that’s 600 time series per region per metric. ok.

Nadia Rahman: plus cluster + shard id? please don’t add shard id as label.

Sofia Ivanova: agreed: no shard id tag. runtime will aggregate across shards before export if possible.

Julian Moore: we can do node-local aggregation and emit per node. but then you still get node label via infra (`instance`).

Allison Grant: infra labels happen, but we can avoid *explicit* shard labels. dashboards can aggregate `sum without(instance)`.

Vikram Singh: also: routing doesn’t want per-node, it wants region/pool-level signals. can we emit to a local aggregator (statsd/otel collector) that rolls up before routing reads it?

Robert King: routing service will consume from fleet signals API, not scrape prometheus directly. as long as fleet layer can aggregate to pool/region, we’re good.

Caleb Johnson: fleet signals pipeline can aggregate if the metric names + tags are consistent. but you need deterministic tag set.

Sofia Ivanova: ok let’s lock the tags:
- REQUIRED labels: `region`, `cluster`, `pool`, `route_group`, `priority_class`
- FORBIDDEN labels: `tenant`, `api_key`, `model_id`, `request_id`, `path`, `user_agent`
- OPTIONAL (only if we see need): `workload_class` (short|medium|long)

Julian Moore: naming: do we prefer `scheduler_lag_ms` vs `scheduler_delay_ms`?

Allison Grant: we have a standard for “lag” as time since event. use `*_lag_ms`.

Logan Wright: +1. also add a counter for shed/backpressure events from runtime?

Lena Fischer: shedding happens in gateway/routing mainly. runtime does backpressure. we can export `runtime_backpressure_active` (0/1) per (pool, route_group, priority_class).

Mateo Alvarez: sounds useful for incident debugging.

Nadia Rahman: backpressure flag is fine. no per-reason explosion though.

Sofia Ivanova: reason codes will live at gateway (429). runtime just exports the activation bit + maybe a single enum in logs.

Julian Moore: posting a concrete snippet for review (OTel metrics):
```
name: runtime_queue_depth
unit: {requests}
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class

name: runtime_scheduler_lag_ms
unit: ms
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class

name: runtime_backpressure_active
unit: {bool}
type: gauge
labels: region, cluster, pool, route_group, priority_class
```

Vikram Singh: 👍 this is sane.

Robert King: routing will treat `scheduler_lag_ms` as primary admission input. queue depth is secondary.

Allison Grant: please add doc note: gauges should be computed as EWMA over 5s to avoid jitter causing routing flaps.

Sofia Ivanova: yes. we’ll smooth in runtime before export (5s window), and routing also has a 15s hold-down.

Logan Wright: and let’s add a quick cardinality test in CI (assert label keys). we got burned last time.

Julian Moore: can do. will add unit test to fail build if someone adds `tenant` label to these instruments.

Mateo Alvarez: ok, next steps:
- Julian: update PR to remove model_id/tenant labels + add smoothing
- Sofia/Lena: define `route_group` mapping in runtime (accept from routing header?)
- Logan/Allison: update observability standard doc with these metric names + label set

Sofia Ivanova: runtime can take `x-redwood-route-group` header from routing. fallback to `unknown` if missing.

Nadia Rahman: make sure `unknown` doesn’t get spammy (if header missing across traffic). alert if header missing rate > threshold.

Sofia Ivanova: good call. adding counter `runtime_route_group_missing_total` (no labels except region/cluster/pool) so we notice.

Allison Grant: cool. thanks all. keeping this thread as the canonical agreement for label set + names.

Julian Moore: will push updates today and ping in #eng-platform when ready.

title: #eng-ml: 1758888888
preview:
Priya Desai: Heads-up — I'm exploring a lightweight "quant-sidecar" approach to bridge FP8 <-> INT8 model variants at runtime. Thinking it'll let us avoid large runtime changes for a migration window. Thoughts?

Jin Park: interesting. do you mean an out-of-process translator that rewrites weights/activations on the fly?

Priya Desai: Yeah. Two modes: 1) offline stage where we generate a compact delta/adapter for model shards, 2) an in-flight shim that applies conversions for hot requests. The runtime talks to sidecar via tiny rpc.

Mateo Alvarez: main win is we don't have to change kernel scheduling in the serving runtime, right? sidecar takes on quant-format mapping complexity.

Priya Desai: Exactly. Runtime sees same tensor shapes/addresses. Sidecar exposes a stable contract: convert(request_meta, KV_payload) -> compatible_blob. Keeps runtime oblivious.

Sami Noor: tradeoffs?

Priya Desai: Latency: extra hop ~0.5-2ms (local socket) depending on conversion complexity. Memory: duplication while converting. Operational: need canary + fallbacks because conversion could degrade quality.

Jin Park: curious about KV cache compatibility. If FP8 and INT8 caches have different encoding, we need a cache-layer canonicalization.

Priya Desai: Proposed: sidecar also exposes a cache canonicalizer interface. On a cache miss: runtime asks sidecar to materialize a canonicalized prefix blob. On hit: we keep the canonical form in the shared memory region.

Mateo Alvarez: storage format for canonicalized blob must be versioned. add a 4-byte header with format id + model version hash. That lets runtime skip conversion when header matches.

Priya Desai: yep. header: `0xRINF` + format_id + model_hash. simple.

Sami Noor: operational hazards: crash surface grows. Sidecar restart during request could fail conversion. How do we fall back?

Priya Desai: fallback strategies:
- retry via a direct runtime-built fallback (lower precision path) when sidecar unreachable
- degrade to host-side FP32 (higher cost) for critical paths
- circuit-breaker with exponential backoff and alerting

Jin Park: how about throughput? per-request conversion could be expensive for large batches.

Priya Desai: plan to amortize via batch-aware converters: accept multiple KV blobs and do vectorized conversion. Also a microcache for recently converted prefixes.

Mateo Alvarez: security: sidecar will see model shards/weights — must run in same pod namespace and inherit KMS creds. Need strict ACLs and fips-mode for enterprise tenants.

Priya Desai: agree. sidecar should be a same-node process using unix domain sockets and ephemeral creds. For Private deployments, we provide an option to link statically at build-time instead of out-of-process.

Jin Park: metrics/telemetry we need:
- convert_latency_ms (p95/p99)
- convert_error_rate
- quality_regressions_by_promptset (hook into evals)
- cache_hit_ratio

Priya Desai: Adding: conversion_type histogram (per op: weight, activation, KV) and format_transition_count.

Sami Noor: rollout playbook?

Priya Desai: Proposed:
1) build sidecar + local integration test
2) smoke canary on dev models with synthetic prompts
3) staged canary in prod for <1% of traffic — compare output diffs and token-level metrics
4) ramp with automatic abort if qualitative thresholds exceed (deg_score > threshold)
5) full switch after 7 days stable

Mateo Alvarez: how do we compute deg_score for conversion induced noise? we already have degscore infra but need per-conversion tagging.

Priya Desai: sidecar will attach `X-Quant-Conversion-ID` header to responses, and degscore pipelines can slice by that id.

Jin Park: one more: versioning contract between runtime and sidecar. We should maintain a minimal API: /convert, /health, /metrics, /cache_info. Keep it protobuf/gRPC but with a clear minimal JSON fallback for debugging.

Priya Desai: noted. I'm drafting a mini-RFC with interface sketches and perf targets (p50 <1ms local, p99 <5ms). I'll ping infra for perf bench infra.

canary-bot: deploy plan created: canary/quant-sidecar/2025-03-12 -> 1% traffic, 6h window

ops-bot: FYI: ensure SLO update request filed if p99 conversion latency > 10ms across any region.

Jin Park: looks reasonable. If we can prove minimal perf hit and manageable ops surface, this seems like a pragmatic migration path.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Also let's prototype a tiny Rust converter for perf and safety.

Priya Desai: I'll iterate and post the RFC link here once ready. thanks everyone :rocket: 

Priya Desai: (follow-up) RFC draft: https://rwd.internal/rfc/quant-sidecar — comments welcome. I'm going to include sample thrift/gRPC proto and the header format.

Sami Noor: left small comments on the portability section. Will follow up with a checklist for private deployments.

title: #finance: 1754326789
preview:
Amrita Rao: FYI ClearWave ACH returned (R01). Invoice C-4091 is 37 days outstanding, $12,430. They emailed asking for a short payment plan and to waive the $50 late fee.
Luca Martel: Thanks. I pulled their ledger — steady usage historically, just low this month. Any verbal promises from sales?
Janelle Kim: No promises logged. We sent 2 dunning emails and left a voicemail last week. Customer replied to support earlier today asking if we could waive fee if they pay soon.
Mateo Alvarez: R01 often means wrong routing/account closed. If they can CC-pay now it's safest. CC token for partial capture reduces exposure.
Amrita Rao: Thinking two paths: A) 7-day full pay with $50 waiver, or B) split into 2 installments (50/50) with CC auth for first and ACH retry for second but keep fee. Thoughts?
Priya Singh: Legal: we can approve a one-off waiver if documented. Need customer to accept terms in writing. If split, require a signed payment agreement.
Luca Martel: My vote: offer A first (simpler), mention split as fallback but require signed plan + CC auth. I'll draft the text.
Janelle Kim: I'll message them from Intercom with the offer: 7-day full pay + $50 waived or signed 2-pay plan with CC now. Include payment link and deadline.
CollectionsBot: NOTE: Customer reply window open until 2025-09-07T15:00Z. Hold for response.
Amrita Rao: Janelle, include copy to billing@redwood and request explicit acceptance. Also note "conditional waiver" on the invoice record.
Janelle Kim: Draft sent to @Priya Singh for sign-off, then I'll post.
Priya Singh: Reviewing now. Add clause: waiver void if chargeback/ reversal occurs within 90 days.
Mateo Alvarez: Also add: if CC auth declines, escalate to senior collections.
Luca Martel: Added account tag `fee-waiver-provisional` and a short note: "Awaiting signed acceptance or payment link click."
Amrita Rao: Great. If no response by 7 days we escalate on day 10 to senior collections (per process). I'll update the ledger when payment clears.
Janelle Kim: Message posted to customer — included payment link and legal terms. Waiting on reply. Link: https://payments.redwood/inv/C-4091
Amrita Rao: Thanks all. Marking thread pending.

title: #architecture: 1753332100
preview:
Karthik Rao: quick design check-in on rollout metadata schema v2. idea: keep runtime wire payload compact, add an optional signed enrichment blob for private installs and for rich clients. want to discuss backwards compatibility, tie-break rules for overlapping pins, and telemetry probe shape.

Zoe Park: love the signed enrichment idea for Private. quick Q: do we need both expires_at ISO and ttl_seconds? i'd prefer single canonical field to avoid conversions in SDKs.

Karthik Rao: agree we should pick one. proposal: canonical on-wire uses ttl_seconds (int) to keep JSON tiny; control plane can surface expires_at in console/ops UI. SDKs can compute expiration if they want human-readable.

Ben Cho: careful: older SDKs may not read ttl_seconds if field name changes. we should include a tiny compatibility hint: "sv" (schema version) integer. older SDKs ignoring unknown fields will be fine; new SDKs use sv to validate.

Mateo Alvarez: tie-breakers: when two pins overlap, deterministic resolver should be: tenant_override > priority_score > earliest_created_ts > lexical_origin_id. compute priority_score server-side. do not put priority on-wire except optional debug.

Karthik Rao: agreed. we'll keep priority server-computed. but include optional debug fields: origin_id and dbg_priority (int). flagged optional so normal clients skip.

Zoe Park: about enrichment signing: propose we add an optional field "enrichment_sig" with base64(sig) and "enrichment_payload" base64(json) so host-side runtime can verify integrity. make the signing key rotation-friendly by including key_id header.

Ben Cho: sounds good. for hosted we don't require signature but we do version the enrichment so SDKs can tolerate missing enrichment. also note: for Private we must document offline key provisioning flow.

Mateo Alvarez: telemetry: add a lightweight probe called rollout_probe: {last_seen_pin, probe_ts_unix, sdk_semver}. keep it sampled (1%) and tolerant of missing fields. this helps measure adoption and stale pins.

Karthik Rao: nice. on collision resolution, we should expose a deterministic resolver endpoint for debugging: /v1/rollouts/resolve?tenant=... returns the chosen pin + resolution trace (list of short tokens describing reasons). for privacy, trace tokens are short codes, not full texts.

Zoe Park: one more: consider binary compacting for devices with bandwidth constraints (e.g., edge runtimes). we could offer an alternative compact encoding (msgpack/base64) but only behind an accept-encoding header. default remains JSON.

Ben Cho: operational concern: TTL semantics. if sdk ignores ttl_seconds, server must enforce a grace window (default 48h) after ttl expiry but mark pins expired in telemetry. tenants can configure grace. must avoid accidental long-lived pins.

Mateo Alvarez: migration plan idea: rollout schema v2 behind a feature flag. step 1: control plane emits sv=2 alongside sv=1 payloads for 2 weeks; step 2: SDK opt-in parse v2 fields; step 3: default switch. sample telemetry to check >95% SDKs can parse.

deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 integration tests: 62/64 cases passed. failures: legacy parser test, signed-enrichment missing-key handling.

Karthik Rao: action list: (a) finalize wire fields: sv, model, variant, pin_mode, ttl_seconds, optional: origin_id, dbg_priority, enrichment_payload, enrichment_sig, probe_sample; (b) write Private signing spec + key rotation + provisioning; (c) implement server resolver-trace endpoint; (d) add grace-window config and default to 48h; (e) telemetry probe sampling plan; (f) doc migration plan and SDK changes.

Zoe Park: I'll draft the signing spec and the provisioning playbook.

Ben Cho: I'll add grace-window server defaults and the resolver endpoint sketch.

Mateo Alvarez: I'll wire up the telemetry probe proto and the migration gating checks.

Karthik Rao: i'll assemble the PR for the wire schema + examples and tag you all. thanks.

title: #general: 1749520010
preview:
Camila Reyes: It’s live! Benchmark report + interactive page are out in the world 🚀
Links:
• Landing page: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
• PDF: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/report.pdf
• Methodology: https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar/methodology
Huge thanks to Applied ML + Runtime + Infra + Docs + PMM + Design + Sales Enablement. Drop early feedback / weirdness you see here.

Jordan Blake: Congrats team. This is a big “we can execute cross-functionally” moment. :clap: Please keep an eye out for any community replies/questions we should respond to quickly.

Soojin Lee: +1 and THANK YOU everyone. If you see anyone asking “how did you measure?” please point them to the methodology link above and ping me for anything that needs an official response.

Ethan Wallace: Seeing folks already sharing screenshots of the tables on X. If you spot any confusing chart/tooltips or phrasing, paste the link + quote in-thread and we’ll triage.

Ben Carter: Early funnel numbers (first ~15 min): landing page sessions tracking above forecast. CTA clicks are healthy. Export clicks higher than expected (good sign). Will share a snapshot in #marketing once dashboards settle.

Logan Wright: Confirming analytics events are firing:
- `benchmark_filter_change`
- `benchmark_metric_toggle`
- `benchmark_export_click`
- `benchmark_cta_click`
If anyone sees missing attribution UTM params, send me the URL.

Felix Schneider: Watching the interactive table performance. We’re getting a lot of “filter spam” (people toggling model/region rapidly). Should be fine but if you see slow loads, tell me which browser + region.

Alicia Perez: Design note: mobile is always the risky one for dense tables. If you can, test on your phone and let me know if anything looks broken (especially the disclaimers + tooltips).

Wei Chen: Docs side: links are green on CI and on live. If anyone sees 404s or weird redirects, paste them here.

Jonas Weber: Applied ML: data + methodology are final as published. If you see anyone challenging fairness, we can point to:
- workload definition
- prompt set v3 production mix
- repeated runs + stability gates
We should avoid debating individual point estimates in replies; emphasize distributions/variance + reproducibility.

Claire Dubois: +1. Also reminder: our “latency/$” is normalized; the PDF footnotes explain it. If you see confusion around “why not tokens/sec”, we have an approved answer.

Markus Klein: SE field note: already got 2 inbound pings asking “can I reproduce this in my PoC?” I’m sending the short version: use our harness + same prompt set + same concurrency + caching policy, and expect some regional variance.

Stephanie Nguyen: Got a Slack from an AE: prospect loved the “p95 heatmap” but asked if Dedicated changes the numbers. I told them: same runtime, different capacity isolation + more predictable queueing. Anything else we want to standardize there?

Mateo Alvarez: That’s correct. For public messaging: don’t promise “better latency” on Dedicated; promise predictability/consistency and capacity guarantees. Runtime path is the same.

Lukas Meyer: EMEA folks asking about EU region wording (data residency). The page says “EU regions available” but doesn’t over-specify. Looks good. If someone asks, we should stick to “available regions listed in the filter; talk to sales for residency requirements.”

Naomi Feldman: Quick security scan: public artifacts look clean (no request IDs, no internal cluster names). If you see someone screenshotting raw JSON endpoints, please send me the URL so we can confirm redaction.

Vanessa Ortiz: Infra: traffic is spiky but within reserved headroom. Biggest variable is cache behavior on first load. We’re watching EU + APAC edges.

Sean Gallagher: FYI we saw a brief CDN cache-miss burst right after announcement went out (more origin hits than expected). Not customer-impacting so far. If anyone hears “page slow” from external folks, capture rough timestamp + location.

Neha Kapoor: On-call is staffed for the next few hours. If you see any 5xx/429 chatter, please paste details (screenshots welcome) and we’ll correlate.

inc-bot: Status update: Monitoring alert "Marketing site origin RPS" triggered (warning). Current: 1,820 rps. Threshold: 1,500 rps. Investigating.

Tyler Benson: lol at our own benchmark page getting benchmarked. :)

Ben Carter: Seeing a few reports of “took ~5–8 seconds to load table” from folks in EU. Not consistent. Might be first-load + cache warming.

Felix Schneider: That aligns with what I’m seeing: first paint is fine, but the table data fetch is slower on cold cache. We have a short TTL right now; can bump if needed.

Sean Gallagher: We can tune cache TTL without changing content. If Marketing is okay with slightly less “instant refresh” for the day, we can reduce origin pressure.

Soojin Lee: Yes, please optimize for reliability today. If we need to refresh numbers later, we’ll plan it.

inc-bot: Status update: Mitigation applied: increased CDN cache TTL for benchmark JSON responses. Origin RPS trending down. No incident declared.

Jasmine Liu: CS heads up: Support queue has 3 tickets asking “are these numbers w/ caching?” We’re replying with the methodology link + a short explanation. If we want a one-liner snippet for frontline, send it to me.

Elliot Price: Suggested snippet (approved language from methodology summary): “Results include Redwood’s default production serving settings for this workload, with caching/batching configured as specified in the methodology. We publish the exact configs and rerun thresholds so teams can reproduce and compare.”

Camila Reyes: This thread is exactly why I love this company. Thank you all. Keep dropping feedback + any external questions you see.

Jordan Blake: +1. And remember: be humble in public replies. Lead with methodology + caveats, not victory laps.

Ethan Wallace: Noted. Also, if you see any typos/unclear phrasing, drop a screenshot + location (page section / PDF page #) and we’ll patch.

Alicia Perez: Someone DM’d me: on iPhone Safari the sticky header overlaps the “Disclaimers” accordion when expanded. I’m looking. If anyone can reproduce, please confirm.

Wei Chen: Confirmed on my iPhone 14 / iOS 18.2. It’s minor but real. I’ll file a quick ticket + propose CSS fix.

Felix Schneider: I can ship a quick CSS adjustment today. Ping me the ticket link.

Ben Carter: Last note for now: traffic is still climbing. Biggest source is organic/social, then newsletter. If you’re posting, please use UTM links so we can attribute.

Camila Reyes: Alright, go enjoy the moment for a minute. Then back to watching the dashboards 🙂

title: #eng-infra: 1749189022
preview:
Vanessa Ortiz: Need to lock capacity reservations for benchmark sweeps in eu-west + APAC. @Jonas Weber @Rina Kobayashi can you confirm model list + rough run window next week?

Jonas Weber: Yep. Publishing regions: us-east, eu-west, apac-sg. Models: Llama 3.1 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/72B, Mixtral 8x7B. We want to start sweeps Mon-Wed and rerun if stability gate trips.

Rina Kobayashi: Runner can do region sweep matrix but I need to know what hardware we’re standardizing on per model. If we mix instance types across regions, p95 variance gets messy.

Vanessa Ortiz: That’s the main issue: eu-west has decent H100 coverage; apac-sg is constrained on H100 and the A100 pool is fragmented.

Harish Reddy: Adding current fleet reality:
- eu-west: h100x8 nodes available (80GB), plus a100x8 (80GB) as fallback
- apac-sg: limited h100x8, more a100x8 but several are already allocated to customer Dedicated
- apac-tokyo: basically no spare H100 next week (maintenance + existing commits)

Claire Dubois: For 70B/72B we can run TP=2 on H100 80GB fine. On A100 80GB TP=2 is OK but we may need to lower max batch / be careful on long context.

Mateo Alvarez: Runtime POV: if we do 70B/72B on A100 in apac-sg but H100 in eu-west, the “latency/$” story will be questioned. Ideally same GPU class across publish regions.

Vanessa Ortiz: Agree. But if we insist on H100 in apac-sg we might only get a narrow window overnight local.

Jonas Weber: Overnight is fine. We can schedule. We mainly need deterministic throughput + minimal queueing noise.

Sean Gallagher: +1 please don’t run during APAC business hours if we’re sharing pool with hosted traffic. Also we need to pre-warm caches / avoid thundering herd on the cluster.

Harish Reddy: Proposed reservation plan (draft):
- eu-west: reserve 2x h100x8 nodes for 48h (Mon 00:00 UTC -> Wed 00:00 UTC)
- apac-sg: reserve 1x h100x8 node for 12h blocks (Mon/Tue 18:00-06:00 SGT)
If APAC H100 slips, fallback is a100x8 but we annotate + potentially keep APAC as “directional” until we can rerun on H100.

Rina Kobayashi: If apac-sg switches to A100, I’d rather hold publish and keep the run internal. Otherwise every external person will ask “why is APAC worse.”

Vanessa Ortiz: Understood. Let’s treat A100 fallback as emergency to unblock harness testing, not publish.

Neha Kapoor: Decision needed: are we OK delaying APAC publish if H100 doesn’t free up? I’d prefer to publish fewer regions than publish shaky data.

Jonas Weber: Yes. We can do internal APAC A100 run as a sanity check but only publish APAC once we get clean H100 sweep.

Vanessa Ortiz: Cool. Then I’ll request H100 only for apac-sg.

Harish Reddy: Instance type constraint callout: apac-sg h100x8 are split across two racks; cross-rack latency isn’t an issue for inference but if you’re doing multi-node it matters. Please keep TP within one node (no multi-node tensor parallel).

Claire Dubois: We’re single-node TP only. No multi-node.

Mateo Alvarez: For Mixtral 8x7B: it’s more sensitive to batching. Can we reserve enough headroom so we don’t hit queueing? I’d like to keep utilization <70% during measurement.

Jonas Weber: Concurrency target is 32–64 depending on workload slice. We can run in “steady state” and sample for ~20 mins per configuration. We can cap at 60% GPU util.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max QPS limiter on the benchmark runners so a misconfig doesn’t hammer the shared control plane.

Rina Kobayashi: I can set `--qps-cap` and `--max-inflight` per runner. Also will randomize start offsets to avoid synchronized bursts.

Vanessa Ortiz: Ok, what’s the exact node count needed? If you reserve 1x h100x8 in apac-sg, can you run all models sequentially on that one node?

Jonas Weber: Yes, sequential is fine. We just need time. 8B/7B can even run on a single GPU but we’ll keep the same node class for consistency.

Harish Reddy: One more constraint: apac-sg has only 1 spare h100x8 at the moment and it’s currently earmarked for a potential Dedicated expansion. I can block it for the benchmark window but we need sign-off from @Neha Kapoor.

Neha Kapoor: Approved for the Mon/Tue overnight blocks as long as we release by Wed 06:00 SGT. If Sales screams, we may need to adjust.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: Thanks. I’ll put in the reservation and label it “BENCHMARK_SWEEP_APAC_SG_H100” so it doesn’t get reclaimed.

Noah Patel: Quick check: do we have eu-west H100 of the same stepping/driver as apac-sg? Kernel selection differences have bitten us before.

Harish Reddy: eu-west H100 pool is on driver 550.54; apac-sg is 550.54 as well. CUDA minor versions match. We’re good.

Rina Kobayashi: Great. I’ll pin runner image digest anyway (dockerized runner) so deps are deterministic.

Vanessa Ortiz: Action items:
- Harish: file the reservations in fleet tool for eu-west 2x h100x8 (Mon-Wed UTC) + apac-sg 1x h100x8 (Mon/Tue 18:00-06:00 SGT)
- Jonas/Rina: confirm sweep duration estimate + send me the manifest matrix so I can sanity check runtime
- Sean: share the launch-week oncall window so we don’t collide with any planned maintenance

Sean Gallagher: No maintenance planned. But please post in #eng-oncall when runners start, so we know if alerts are benchmark-related.

Jonas Weber: Will do. Also: if apac-sg H100 becomes unavailable, we pause APAC publish and only publish us-east + eu-west in v1.

Vanessa Ortiz: Works. I’ll update the capacity doc + drop a note in the GTM thread so PMM isn’t surprised.

Harish Reddy: Reservations submitted. Pending approval in fleet system for eu-west H100 because one node is currently “warm spare” for an enterprise customer. I’ll resolve today.

Vanessa Ortiz: thx. Keep me posted if eu-west drops to 1 node; we can still run but reruns will take longer.

Rina Kobayashi: FYI runner settings I’m planning:
`--region eu-west --node-type h100x8 --qps-cap 40 --max-inflight 256 --warmup-minutes 15 --sample-minutes 20`
Same for apac-sg. Will tune if we see queueing.

Claire Dubois: Looks good. Please ensure we capture “capacity reserved” in run manifest so we can state results weren’t affected by noisy neighbors.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ll include that in the methodology notes.

Vanessa Ortiz: Closing loop: once fleet approvals clear I’ll paste reservation IDs here.

title: #eng-runtime: 1749162405
preview:
Omar El-Sayed: Seeing pretty ugly variance in p95 on the region sweep reruns. Same commit/config, 3 runs back-to-back.

Omar El-Sayed: Snapshot (A100 80GB, us-east):
- p50: 410-430ms (stable)
- p95: 980ms, 1.9s, 1.1s (not stable)
Throughput is within ~5%.

Rina Kobayashi: is that end-to-end including queue, or just model time?

Omar El-Sayed: e2e (client send -> last token). harness is closed-loop concurrency=32, but we *do* include server-side queue.

Noah Patel: p50 stable + p95 jumping smells like queue tail, not kernel perf.

Jonas Weber: +1. Also: are we mixing prompt shapes? the v3 prod mix has some long prefill + short decode, right?

Omar El-Sayed: yep. prompt_set=v3-production-mix. prefill distribution is wide.

Claire Dubois: can we check whether the runtime is switching kernels between runs? autotune can choose different fused-attn paths depending on observed shapes.

Noah Patel: It shouldn’t if shapes are the same, but if the prompt mix shuffles order, the initial calibration can land on different kernel variants.

Mateo Alvarez: What’s the GPU utilization like on the bad p95 run? If we’re near saturation, small arrival jitter can spike queue.

Omar El-Sayed: pulled server stats from logs:

```text
run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:10Z
gpu_util_avg=71%
queue_depth_p95=14
batch_size_avg=6.2

run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:24Z
gpu_util_avg=73%
queue_depth_p95=41
batch_size_avg=6.4

run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:38Z
gpu_util_avg=72%
queue_depth_p95=18
batch_size_avg=6.3
```

Noah Patel: queue_depth_p95=41 explains the 1.9s p95 if max_wait is permissive.

Rina Kobayashi: what are we using for scheduler settings in these sweeps? (max_wait_ms, max_batch_tokens, etc)

Omar El-Sayed: from the manifest:

```yaml
scheduler:
  policy: continuous-batching
  max_wait_ms: 8
  max_batch_tokens: 8192
  max_batch_size: 16
```

Jonas Weber: max_wait 8ms is fine; but if we’re getting bursts, queue can still build even with low wait.

Claire Dubois: could be a “convoy” effect: long-prefill requests arrive clustered early in the run, they occupy compute longer, queue builds, tail gets hammered.

Noah Patel: Exactly. p95 is sensitive to ordering with heterogeneous service times.

Mateo Alvarez: Are we randomizing the prompt order each run? If yes, that’s a big source of variance.

Rina Kobayashi: we do shuffle with seed derived from timestamp right now (oops). for fairness, maybe we need deterministic seed.

Omar El-Sayed: ah that’s probably it. I’ll confirm. :facepalm:

Vanessa Ortiz: (from infra side) also flagging: on the 18:24Z run, fleet had a noisy neighbor alert in that cluster. Not a hard preemption, but some PCIe retries logged. Not sure if related.

Noah Patel: Even without noisy neighbor, prompt shuffle alone can do this. But good to know.

Claire Dubois: Can we separate “service time” (runtime compute) vs “queue time” in the exported metrics? For the report we probably want both, or at least to be explicit that latency includes queue under a fixed concurrency.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ve been asked to publish latency/$, but if p95 is dominated by queue ordering, that’s still “real,” just needs explanation.

Omar El-Sayed: I can add `queue_time_ms` and `model_time_ms` to the per-request log + export. We already have spans internally.

Mateo Alvarez: Also check kernel selection. If autotune is happening at startup, we should log kernel IDs chosen for key ops (attn, GEMM).

Noah Patel: There’s an env var to pin kernel profile:
`RW_KERNEL_PROFILE=stable_v2` (disables some heuristics).
We could run a quick A/B: stable_v2 vs default.

Rina Kobayashi: If we do that, we need to note it in methodology. But for debugging right now, it’ll isolate whether kernel drift is contributing.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll rerun locally with fixed prompt seed + pinned kernel profile to see if p95 tightens.

Jonas Weber: What about KV cache behavior? Are we seeing evictions / cache fragmentation that could affect tail?

Noah Patel: In this harness each request is independent, so KV cache reuse shouldn’t matter unless we’re enabling prefix cache (are we?)

Rina Kobayashi: prefix cache is ON in these runs (policy: "auto"). It might help mean cost but can introduce variability if hit rate swings.

Claire Dubois: For the public benchmark, do we want prefix cache on? It’s a production feature, but it can be workload-dependent.

Jonas Weber: for now: keep it consistent and disclose. but for debugging variance, maybe turn it off to understand baseline.

Omar El-Sayed: ok plan:
1) rerun with `--prefix-cache=off` and fixed prompt order seed.
2) rerun with cache on + fixed seed.
3) compare p95 and queue_depth distributions.

Mateo Alvarez: Also confirm harness mode: you said closed-loop concurrency=32, which *should* reduce arrival jitter vs open-loop. But if each worker is sync and we have variable response sizes, the effective arrival pattern can still cluster.

Noah Patel: We might consider adding an open-loop mode for stability checks (Poisson arrivals) and report both. But that’s bigger scope.

Rina Kobayashi: for report, we committed to closed-loop as “fixed concurrency,” right? (I think Claire wrote that draft.)

Claire Dubois: yes, closed-loop fixed concurrency. But we should add the queue-time breakdown so people don’t interpret it as pure model compute.

perf-bot: Perf alert: p95 regression > 25% detected for suite=latency_per_dollar region=us-east model=ri-llama-70b run_id=us-east-2025-06-05T18:24Z. Threshold=20%. Auto-rerun enabled.

Omar El-Sayed: lol perf-bot piling on.

Vanessa Ortiz: If the auto-rerun lands on a different host, that’ll also help rule out node noise.

Noah Patel: One more thought: are clocks stable? Some nodes run with different power caps after maintenance.

Vanessa Ortiz: power caps should be uniform, but I’ll double-check that pool.

Omar El-Sayed: Here’s a trace sample from the bad run (one request in the p95 tail):

```text
req_id=... 
queue_ms=812
prefill_ms=512
decode_ms=146
stream_overhead_ms=18
```

Omar El-Sayed: Tail is overwhelmingly queue.

Jonas Weber: That’s good news for runtime perf (ish). But it means we need to control ordering / publish stability gates.

Claire Dubois: Action items:
- Omar: add queue/model time fields to export + run the fixed-seed experiments
- Rina: update sweep runner to use deterministic prompt shuffle seed (configurable)
- Noah: add kernel selection logging + check stability of autotune
- Vanessa: sanity-check noisy-neighbor / power cap notes on that pool

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Let’s aim to have a clean explanation by tomorrow so PMM doesn’t get surprised by “why did p95 change?” questions.

Omar El-Sayed: ack. I’ll post rerun graphs once I have them.
text:
Omar El-Sayed: Seeing pretty ugly variance in p95 on the region sweep reruns. Same commit/config, 3 runs back-to-back.

Omar El-Sayed: Snapshot (A100 80GB, us-east):
- p50: 410-430ms (stable)
- p95: 980ms, 1.9s, 1.1s (not stable)
Throughput is within ~5%.

Rina Kobayashi: is that end-to-end including queue, or just model time?

Omar El-Sayed: e2e (client send -> last token). harness is closed-loop concurrency=32, but we *do* include server-side queue.

Noah Patel: p50 stable + p95 jumping smells like queue tail, not kernel perf.

Jonas Weber: +1. Also: are we mixing prompt shapes? the v3 prod mix has some long prefill + short decode, right?

Omar El-Sayed: yep. prompt_set=v3-production-mix. prefill distribution is wide.

Claire Dubois: can we check whether the runtime is switching kernels between runs? autotune can choose different fused-attn paths depending on observed shapes.

Noah Patel: It shouldn’t if shapes are the same, but if the prompt mix shuffles order, the initial calibration can land on different kernel variants.

Mateo Alvarez: What’s the GPU utilization like on the bad p95 run? If we’re near saturation, small arrival jitter can spike queue.

Omar El-Sayed: pulled server stats from logs:

```text
run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:10Z
gpu_util_avg=71%
queue_depth_p95=14
batch_size_avg=6.2

run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:24Z
gpu_util_avg=73%
queue_depth_p95=41
batch_size_avg=6.4

run=us-east-2025-06-05T18:38Z
gpu_util_avg=72%
queue_depth_p95=18
batch_size_avg=6.3
```

Noah Patel: queue_depth_p95=41 explains the 1.9s p95 if max_wait is permissive.

Rina Kobayashi: what are we using for scheduler settings in these sweeps? (max_wait_ms, max_batch_tokens, etc)

Omar El-Sayed: from the manifest:

```yaml
scheduler:
  policy: continuous-batching
  max_wait_ms: 8
  max_batch_tokens: 8192
  max_batch_size: 16
```

Jonas Weber: max_wait 8ms is fine; but if we’re getting bursts, queue can still build even with low wait.

Claire Dubois: could be a “convoy” effect: long-prefill requests arrive clustered early in the run, they occupy compute longer, queue builds, tail gets hammered.

Noah Patel: Exactly. p95 is sensitive to ordering with heterogeneous service times.

Mateo Alvarez: Are we randomizing the prompt order each run? If yes, that’s a big source of variance.

Rina Kobayashi: we do shuffle with seed derived from timestamp right now (oops). for fairness, maybe we need deterministic seed.

Omar El-Sayed: ah that’s probably it. I’ll confirm. :facepalm:

Vanessa Ortiz: (from infra side) also flagging: on the 18:24Z run, fleet had a noisy neighbor alert in that cluster. Not a hard preemption, but some PCIe retries logged. Not sure if related.

title: #marketing: 1749012234
preview:
Soojin Lee: Kicking off the benchmark report workstream here. Goal: align owners + first draft dates + lock a short model/region list we can actually run + publish. 

Soojin Lee: Working doc for now (Drive): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/benchmark-report (outline + calendar placeholders). Please drop edits there but use this thread for decisions.

Camila Reyes: :raised_hands: Thanks Soojin. I want us crisp on “what we measured” and not over-claimy. Also need an internal timeline we can hold people to.

Ethan Wallace: I can own report narrative + blog. Need early inputs: what models, what regions, what workload definition (prefill/decode mix) so I can draft the story arc.

Jonas Weber: Applied ML can own methodology + data quality gates. I’ll pair w/ Claire + Rina. We’ll need a shortlist by end of week to start sweeps + reserve capacity.

Claire Dubois: +1. Also: if we’re going to use “latency/$”, we need to be explicit about pricing basis and how we normalize across context lengths.

Soojin Lee: Proposed owners (sanity check):
- Methodology + harness: Jonas/Claire/Rina
- Report narrative + blog: Ethan
- Launch plan + GTM messaging: me
- Interactive page copy + docs: Wei (loop DevEx)
- Capacity reservations: Vanessa (loop Harish)
- Legal claims review: Michael + Sofia

Soojin Lee: First draft dates (aggressive):
- Outline (v0.3): Fri
- Methodology write-up (v0.1): next Tue
- Data snapshot (first sweep NA only): end of next week
- Report draft (v0.1): 2 weeks from today

Ben Carter: For growth funnel: I can support email + landing CTAs but I need the page URL + what we’re gating vs ungated (PDF?) soon. Also need UTM + events list early.

Wei Chen: I can take landing page / docs work. Need to know: are we calling it “latency per dollar” or something softer? Also disclaimer placement matters.

Camila Reyes: We should keep “latency/$” but add “production inference economics” framing. Let’s not make it sound like a single magic number.

Henry Cho: Product POV: we need a “supported vs experimental” designation for models/regions we publish. I’d prefer we only publish what we can keep stable + offer to customers.

Rina Kobayashi: From harness side, if model list keeps changing mid-run it’s a mess. Please lock shortlist ASAP and then we can add an addendum later.

Soojin Lee: Ok—model list debate. Current shortlist from last meeting:
- Llama 3.1 8B + 70B
- Qwen2.5 7B + 72B
- Mistral Large (if we can) / Mixtral 8x7B
- “One small instruction-tuned” + “one larger general” per family

Jonas Weber: I’d push for: Llama 3.1 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/72B, Mixtral 8x7B, and one “reasoning-ish” model IF it’s stable. But we need to be careful about prompt set compatibility.

Claire Dubois: Mixtral can be noisy in tail latency depending on batching. If we include it, we need to show p95 + throughput alongside the latency/$ composite.

Mateo Alvarez: Runtime note: we can make the results look bad or good depending on KV cache policy and continuous batching knobs. We should pick defaults that match “production recommended” and be transparent.

Vanessa Ortiz: Regions: what are we thinking? I can reserve capacity but I need the exact matrix. Please don’t give me 10 regions x 8 models.

Soojin Lee: Proposed regions for publication:
- NA: us-east, us-west
- EU: eu-west (maybe eu-central depending on availability)
- APAC: apac-sg or apac-tokyo (whichever we can keep consistent)

Vanessa Ortiz: eu-west is usually fine; eu-central is tighter. apac-tokyo is the most constrained. apac-sg tends to be smoother.

Ethan Wallace: For story: “regional consistency” is a key pillar, so at least 1 APAC is important. I’d rather do fewer models than drop APAC.

Henry Cho: Agree, but only if we can say it’s a region we support for real customers (not “best effort”).

Jonas Weber: Suggest compromise:
- Publish: us-east, eu-west, apac-sg
- Internal-only (optional): us-west, eu-central for sanity
Then we can talk about “additional regions available” without publishing claims.

Camila Reyes: That works for me. We can keep charts readable too.

Sofia Mendes: Looping in: please avoid “fastest” / “best in class” language. If we do any comparative statement, it must be scoped + substantiated with retained raw data.

Michael Grant: +1. Also we need a review gate for any competitor mention, even “contextual baselines.” Put a checklist in the process.

Soojin Lee: :noted: So decision proposal (reply with 👍 / concerns):
Models to publish (v1): Llama 3.1 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/72B, Mixtral 8x7B. (Optional 4th TBD after stability check.)
Regions to publish (v1): us-east, eu-west, apac-sg.

Rina Kobayashi: 👍 from me if we lock by EOD Thu so I can wire the sweep runner config and not keep re-cutting manifests.

Mateo Alvarez: 👍. For the optional 4th model: only add if it doesn’t require special-case runtime flags.

Vanessa Ortiz: 👍. For capacity, give me approximate concurrency targets + run windows; I’ll pencil reservations for next week.

Ben Carter: 👍. For landing page, I’ll assume the interactive table starts with those 3 regions and we can add a “more regions” dropdown later once stable.

Wei Chen: 👍. I’ll draft copy with “Regions shown: us-east, eu-west, apac-sg” and link to methodology. Will include a tooltip: “Results vary by workload; see details.”

Ethan Wallace: 👍. If we don’t get a 4th model, I’ll adjust narrative to focus on serving economics + consistency across popular open models.

Claire Dubois: 👍 with one caveat: prompt set needs to include both prefill-heavy and decode-heavy distributions or people will argue it’s cherry-picked. We should preempt that in methodology.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ll ship prompt set v3 “production mix” and document token length distributions. I’ll post a draft methodology section early next week.

Soojin Lee: Great. Action items:
- Jonas/Claire/Rina: lock workload + prompt set + caching/batching policy in a short “methodology lock” doc by Tue.
- Vanessa: draft capacity reservation plan for us-east/eu-west/apac-sg, with backup if APAC constrained.
- Ethan: start report outline (sections + where charts go) by Fri.
- Wei: landing page copy skeleton + disclaimer blocks by Fri.
- Michael/Sofia: send us the “claims do/don’t” bullets we can paste into message house.

Camila Reyes: Please also add a weekly 30-min check-in for this group. Keep it tight, decisions only.

Noura El-Khoury: I can set up the recurring + start thinking about webinar timing once we have a launch week. Drop preferred dates.

Soojin Lee: I’ll send a calendar hold. Thanks all — keep model/region changes out of band unless it’s a real blocker. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-oncall: 1745236789
preview:
Alex Kim: incident-bot fired for west1 — seeing a spike in 5xx (~15% over baseline) and support is getting tickets. I'm on for another 5–10m and need to handoff driver + comms. Who can take driver?

incident-bot: [ALERT] service=inference-edge-west1 severity=high 5xx_rate=15% window=3m link=https://incidents.redwood/INC-174523

Priya Singh: logging in now. I can cover comms if someone else drives. Can you summarize current state & mitigations? :+1:

Alex Kim: quick summary: edge-proxy group-4 in west1 had CPU/mem spike, restarted pods. Restart helped 2/6 hosts; errors came down briefly then rebounded. We enabled TTL fallback to regional pool (routes ~20% to east1). Suspect mem leak in proxy v2. Next steps per runbook: try rolling restart, then rollback to v1.9 if restarts don't hold.

Mateo Alvarez: I can take driver. If restarts fail I'll initiate rollback. Do we have a staging-safe rollback command?

Jen O'Connor: I can be comms for the handoff and post to support. I'll prepare an initial customer blurb.

Alex Kim: confirmed — Mateo driver, Jen comms, Priya backup. Note coverage gap: no APAC oncall right now if east1 degrades. I'll call that out to SRE lead.

Mateo Alvarez: copying runbook step 4 snippet here:
```
# rollback proxy to v1.9
kubectl -n infra set image deploy/edge-proxy edge-proxy=registry.redwood/proxy:v1.9
kubectl rollout status deploy/edge-proxy -n infra\```
I'll run that at T+10m if errors don't drop.

incident-bot: escalation_timer=10m remaining until notify:oncall-sre

Priya Singh: monitoring SLOs & top-talkers. Support tickets show a handful of paying customers hit (CS has tags). Jen, draft: "We're aware of elevated errors in the western region; traffic is being shifted and engineers are mitigating. Expect an update in 15 minutes."

Jen O'Connor: Draft posted to #support-downtime: "We are aware of intermittent service errors affecting customers in the western region. Engineers are actively mitigating; temporary routing and restarts are in effect. Next update in ~15 minutes."

Alex Kim: handing over now — I'm off after 5m. Summary for night: suspected proxy mem leak on v2, restarts partial, fallback routing in place, rollback ready. Mateo driving, Jen comms, Priya shadow/backup. Escalate to @oncall-sre and @infra-leads if rollback required. :handshake:

Mateo Alvarez: acknowledged, taking driver. Will run rollback at T+10m if no improvement and report back here.

Priya Singh: monitoring metrics and tickets. I'll prepare a short handoff note at end of my watch for the night oncall.

Jen O'Connor: sent customer update to support and pinned the incident link. Good handoff — thanks all. :tada:

I'll run that at T+10m if errors don't drop.
