title: #incidents: 1741684000
preview:
Incident Bot: :rotating_light: *Quality Guardrail Alert* :rotating_light:
Signal: `quant_profile_quality_regression`
Environment: `beta-hosted` / `perf-canary`
Model: `llama-3.1-70b-instruct` variant `x1`
Profile: `qp_llama31_70b_x1_int8_v3` (library)
Route(s): `dp-acme/chat-prod` (design partner allowlist)
Metric: `eval.delta` exceeded threshold for suite `redwood-pset-summ-v2`
Observed: -1.9pp (threshold -1.0pp)
Fallback rate: 0.2% (normal)
Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/rollback-procedure-quantization-profiles
Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/quant-profiles/rollout?var_model=llama-3.1-70b-instruct&var_profile=qp_llama31_70b_x1_int8_v3

Selene Huang: seeing this fire in both perf-canary and the beta route. looks like *real* quality delta, not a harness flake. :eyes:

Jonas Weber: pulled the latest eval harness run. the delta is concentrated in summarization + a couple of instruction-following items.
- suite: `redwood-pset-summ-v2` -> -1.9pp (p<0.01)
- suite: `redwood-pset-chat-safety-mini` -> within noise
decode latency looks fine. this is quality-only.

Arjun Desai: confirm profile `...int8_v3` was published yesterday w/ “beta” tag for x1. calibration set might be under-representing the summarization prompts.
we should *block* this profile version for `x1` ASAP.

Connor O'Brien: agree. do we want to do route-level disable for acme immediately, or kill-switch? (kill-switch feels too heavy if isolated to one variant/profile)

Selene Huang: route-level disable first. criteria says any statistically sig regression beyond threshold => disable + investigate.
kill-switch only if we see blast radius or can't roll back quickly.

Ravi Menon: I can flip the control-plane policy to resolve `quantization_profile_ref=default` for that route. also can add a temporary denylist entry for `qp_llama31_70b_x1_int8_v3` + variant `x1` so people can't re-enable.

Noah Patel: runtime side is behaving as expected (profile applied, quant kernels selected). no correctness errors, just output drift. logs show `quant_profile_id=qp_llama31_70b_x1_int8_v3` with `resolution=applied`.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. no crash/NaNs. this looks like genuine quant quality tradeoff, not a runtime bug.

Paula Mendes: do we know if any other beta routes enabled this profile? want to scope impact for comms.

Ravi Menon: checking now via route settings query. so far only `dp-acme/chat-prod` has the profile explicitly set. perf-canary has it via scenario config.

Dev Patel: support side: acme had asked for “opt-in only” w/ fast rollback. I can ping Markus/Miguel w/ heads up once we flip it.

Connor O'Brien: ok let's execute:
1) disable on `dp-acme/chat-prod` (route setting)
2) block profile for x1 in allowed list (so UI/API shows incompatible)
3) rerun evals with a recalibrated candidate if/when Applied ML has one

Selene Huang: +1. also please capture artifacts + link in follow-ups. and open a post-incident note even if low severity.

Ravi Menon: I’m applying route change now.

Ravi Menon: change submitted: `route dp-acme/chat-prod` -> `quantization_profile_ref=default`
Audit event should show up in ~1 min.

Incident Bot: :white_check_mark: Audit Log Event
actor: `ravi.menon`
action: `route.quantization_profile_ref.update`
route: `dp-acme/chat-prod`
before: `qp_llama31_70b_x1_int8_v3`
after: `default`

Olivia Grant: perf-canary: I can disable the scenario for tonight’s run so we stop re-triggering alerts while we investigate.

Jonas Weber: please do, but keep a baseline run so we can confirm recovery / rule out harness issues.

Arjun Desai: I’ll mark `qp_llama31_70b_x1_int8_v3` as blocked for `x1` and open a hotfix PR to update the registry constraints. likely same as what we discussed in the “block publication pending recalibration” policy.

Connor O'Brien: Arjun pls link PR here. we should get it into next patch train.

Arjun Desai: will do. plan:
- update profile metadata: `status=deprecated_for_variant` (x1 only)
- add note: “fails redwood-pset-summ-v2 regression threshold”
- ensure Console shows warning + prevents enable

Selene Huang: also add a guardrail test so we catch this earlier: if a profile is newly published, require it to pass `summ-v2` specifically for 70B x1.

Paula Mendes: can we add a dashboard annotation for the disable event + include in beta weekly update?

Connor O'Brien: yes. Paula can you draft a short note for release/beta update? keep it factual: “quality regression detected, rolled back within X minutes, no SLA impact.”

Dev Patel: if acme asks what happened, we can say:
- regression alert fired
- profile disabled on their route
- they’re back to baseline precision
- we’ll share a new candidate after recalibration + eval report

Noah Patel: monitoring right now: after disable, `quant_profile_applied_rate` on that route is dropping to 0. fallback rate steady. p95 latency unchanged.

Jonas Weber: kicking a quick rerun on eval harness comparing baseline vs old profile to get a per-category breakdown for Applied ML.

Ravi Menon: I’ll also add a policy rule so if someone tries to set `qp_llama31_70b_x1_int8_v3` on x1 it resolves as incompatible + returns a clear error (vs silently applying).

Selene Huang: good. “no silent changes” + “no silent incompatible enablement” were explicit beta conditions.

Arjun Desai: PR incoming shortly. also note: the same int8_v3 profile is *passing* on variant `x2` (different kernel path). so we should scope the block narrowly to x1.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. keep scope tight.

Incident Bot: :information_source: Alert Recovery
Signal: `quant_profile_quality_regression`
Status: recovered (no new firing for 20m)

Connor O'Brien: ok marking incident as mitigated. follow-ups:
- [Arjun] hotfix PR to block/deprecate profile for x1 + publish notes
- [Jonas/Selene] attach eval breakdown + confirm against regression thresholds doc
- [Ravi] ensure control plane returns explicit incompat error + Console warning
- [Olivia] adjust perf-canary scenario (disable this profile on x1) + keep baseline
- [Paula] beta update note + dashboard annotation
- [Dev] inform design partner contact + offer to review their own prompt-set eval plan

Selene Huang: :thumbsup: thanks all. please also link the audit event + dashboard snapshot in the post-incident doc.

Arjun Desai: PR link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood/pull/18592 (hotfix: disable profile on model variant x1 due to quality regression)

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: will queue for patch train. please add a short rollback note in the PR description.

Arjun Desai: done. adding “how to re-enable once recalibrated” as well.

Jonas Weber: eval breakdown ETA ~30m. prelim: the biggest hits are long-context summarization prompts (>4k tokens). might need a max-context constraint in the profile metadata for x1.

Noah Patel: that tracks w/ activation quant + kv-cache interactions at long seq. might be worth adding `max_context_tokens` guardrail to compatibility checks.

Ravi Menon: yep we can enforce `max_context_tokens` in policy engine if we have it in the profile schema/metadata. will open a follow-up ENG ticket once we confirm.

Selene Huang: cool. closing loop once eval breakdown is posted.

title: #architecture: 1741247710
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off ADR review for Runtime LTS branching/versioning. Main question: do we maintain a dedicated `runtime-1.18-lts` branch (with patches), or do tags-only on mainline (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts` pointing at selected commits)? Looking for eyes on tradeoffs: dependency mgmt, long-term maint cost, and customer pinning semantics (Private/Helm).

Noah Patel: Tags-only feels attractive on paper but I’m worried it collapses the “stable baseline” story. If main keeps moving (scheduler/kernels), tags-only means we’re constantly re-validating a new head for every patch.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. LTS promise is “slower-change”. If we cut from main each month, that’s not LTS, that’s just regular release train with a different label.

Sahana Iyer: From CI/build: tags-only is painful for reproducible builds. We need deterministic toolchain + pinned deps + provenance artifacts. If mainline build scripts evolve, an older tag may not be buildable without archaeology. A branch lets us freeze build plumbing for that line.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if a Sev1 hits on an LTS customer, we want minimal diff from last known-good. With tags-only you’re effectively pulling in unrelated deltas just to get the fix, unless you do some elaborate cherry-pick dance anyway.

Rafael Mendes: Also operationally: release branch lets us run separate canary + SLO dashboards scoped to that line. If it’s just tags on main, it gets ambiguous what “line” a customer is on when we’re debugging.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side: we already talked about freezing autotune defaults. That’s basically “branch behavior”. Doing it via tags-only means we’re trying to keep incompatible defaults coexisting on main which is a mess.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling: same issue. There are heuristics we explicitly *don’t* want in LTS (experimental batching / load shedding tweaks). Branch gives us a clean “no, not in this line” answer.

Devon Price: The only upside I see for tags-only is fewer branches to babysit + less backport conflict resolution. But… we’re still backporting, just not calling it that.

Quentin Harris: Compatibility matrix / packaging: Private installer and Helm need a stable image tag scheme. It’s much clearer if it’s `runtime:1.18.1-lts` built from `runtime-1.18-lts` branch with a corresponding git tag. Tags-only on main is doable but makes provenance and “what changed since last patch” harder to explain.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Security: branch helps with vulnerability response. We can scope patches to the supported line, document CVEs, and avoid unrelated changes. For audit, “supported branch + signed artifacts + SBOM” is a familiar enterprise pattern.

Talia Benson: Trying to capture: branch = clearer product semantics + stable build/CI config + minimal-diff backports; tags-only = lower overhead but undermines baseline and forces wide diffs over time.

Connor O'Brien: Counterpoint: branch cost. We’ll need to keep dependencies (CUDA toolchain, NCCL, third party libs) pinned and occasionally uplift them for security fixes anyway. Does branch make that worse?

Sahana Iyer: It makes it explicit, not worse. Either way we need patch-level uplift for security. With a branch we can track “toolchain uplift” as an LTS change category and gate it with perf + determinism checks.

Noah Patel: Dependency mgmt detail: runtime has some “generated kernels / codegen outputs” that can drift. If main updates generator, tags-only means backports might require re-running generator with a newer version (non-repro). Branch can freeze generator version and keep outputs stable.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also “micro-architecture tuning tables” change fairly often. We can freeze + document override knobs for LTS, rather than chasing main updates.

Sean Gallagher: Maintenance cost question: how many lines are we expecting concurrently? If it’s 1 active LTS (1.18) and maybe a short overlap during cutover (1.19), that’s manageable. If we promise 3 active LTS lines, different story.

Mateo Alvarez: Plan is single active LTS at a time, 12 months support window, overlap for 4–6 weeks during next LTS branch cut. We should write that in ADR to cap the sprawl.

Rafael Mendes: On-call/escalation: branch lets us attach “mandatory patch” flags to a line. For tags-only, there’s a risk customers pin to an older tag and never take the security update because it doesn’t look like a “patch train”.

Devon Price: Tooling: backport labels + cherry-pick bot assumes a target branch. Tags-only would mean cherry-picking into… what exactly? You’d still need a staging branch or release branch to land the patch before tagging.

Connor O'Brien: Good point: our automation tickets (backport bot, release branch CI) basically assume `runtime-*-lts` exists.

Quentin Harris: Customer semantics: Dedicated/Private want to say “we run runtime-1.18-lts, patch 3” and have that map to artifacts, checksums, notes, and support policy. Branch name shows up in internal dashboards too.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also aligns with compliance norms: “maintenance branch with security backports” is a thing auditors understand. We can map CVE remediation to a branch and tag.

Noah Patel: So what’s the main argument *against* branch? Mostly backport conflicts and long-term drift. But drift is controlled by policy: only Sev1/Sev2/security, minimal diffs, perf gates.

Sofia Ivanova: Conflicts: we’ll get some, but scheduler has good test coverage now + we’re adding targeted smoke tests on release branches. That should reduce regressions even when conflicts happen.

Sahana Iyer: If we do branch, we need strict rules for merging *from* LTS back to main (or not). I suggest: never merge LTS into main, only cherry-pick from main to LTS. Otherwise we create weird history.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. LTS is downstream only.

Sean Gallagher: Another nuance: perf gate stability. If tags-only, perf gates would have to run on main head for each patch, and you’ll get more variance (different kernels/heuristics). Branch helps keep perf noise down and makes regressions easier to attribute.

Mateo Alvarez: Sounds like consensus: dedicated LTS branch is the right call, with explicit constraints:
- only one active LTS line
- downstream-only cherry-picks
- pinned build/toolchain for reproducibility
- clearly documented dependency uplift policy (security-driven)

Talia Benson: Action items I’m hearing:
1) Connor to update ADR text to explicitly reject tags-only and call out why (baseline stability + build reproducibility + customer pinning).
2) Sahana to add a short section on build determinism and why branch matters.
3) Quentin to review the pinning semantics wording for Private/Helm.

Connor O'Brien: Yup. I’ll push ADR edits today and re-request sign-off. If anyone feels strongly we should keep tags-only as a fallback, speak now.

Devon Price: No objection. Maybe include one line: tags still exist for each patch (`1.18.1-lts`), but they’re cut from the LTS branch.

Connor O'Brien: Good call.

Rafael Mendes: +1 and add “waiver process” reference for perf gates (SRE approval) so people don’t bypass it under pressure.

Connor O'Brien: Noted.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: Let’s land it. We can revisit if branch maintenance cost explodes, but right now branch is the only way to make the promise real.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: 

Dr. Aisha Rahman: :thumbsup: Please make sure ADR mentions security OOB patches can be cut directly on LTS branch with tightened approval.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Thanks all.

Sean Gallagher: Another nuance: perf gate stability. If tags-only, perf gates would have to run on main head for each patch, and you’ll get more variance (different kernels/heuristics). Branch helps keep perf noise down and makes regressions easier to attribute.

Mateo Alvarez: Sounds like consensus: dedicated LTS branch is the right call, with explicit constraints:
- only one active LTS line
- downstream-only cherry-picks
- pinned build/toolchain for reproducibility
- clearly documented dependency uplift policy (security-driven)

Talia Benson: Action items I’m hearing:
1) Connor to update ADR text to explicitly reject tags-only and call out why (baseline stability + build reproducibility + customer pinning).
2) Sahana to add a short section on build determinism and why branch matters.
3) Quentin to review the pinning semantics wording for Private/Helm.

Connor O'Brien: Yup. I’ll push ADR edits today and re-request sign-off. If anyone feels strongly we should keep tags-only as a fallback, speak now.

Devon Price: No objection. Maybe include one line: tags still exist for each patch (`1.18.1-lts`), but they’re cut from the LTS branch.

Connor O'Brien: Good call.

Rafael Mendes: +1 and add “waiver process” reference for perf gates (SRE approval) so people don’t bypass it under pressure.

Connor O'Brien: Noted.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: Let’s land it. We can revisit if branch maintenance cost explodes, but right now branch is the only way to make the promise real.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: 

Dr. Aisha Rahman: :thumbsup: Please make sure ADR mentions security OOB patches can be cut directly on LTS branch with tightened approval.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1741015402
preview:
Noah Patel: We need to lock the backport policy risk rubric for the LTS line this week. Can we align here on: (1) what qualifies (Sev1/Sev2/security), (2) who has to approve, (3) what testing is mandatory before we merge into runtime-*-lts?

Mateo Alvarez: +1. Let’s keep it boring. Default should be “no backport” unless it meets clear criteria.

Connor O'Brien: Draft rubric proposal (trying to match what Release Eng can operationalize):
- Eligible classes:
  A) Security: CVE or Redwood Security advisory, or credible vuln w/ exploitability (even pre-CVE). Always eligible.
  B) Sev1: crash/data corruption/hard outage in production (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) OR “cannot serve” for a material customer segment.
  C) Sev2: customer-impacting correctness issue OR latency/throughput regression exceeding thresholds and confirmed not workload-specific.
- Non-eligible by default:
  - New features, behavior changes without strong customer need
  - Refactors / “cleanup”
  - Perf optimizations unless they fix a Sev2 regression (or are needed to mitigate a Sev1)

Connor O'Brien: Risk scoring for each candidate (to decide gates + approvals):
- Low: small, isolated fix; no kernel/scheduler hot paths; easy to test; no config changes.
- Med: touches hot path but localized; adds/updates tests; perf impact uncertain but measurable.
- High: kernel changes, scheduler/policy changes, autotune/defaults changes, memory management (KV cache), threading, or anything that can shift perf characteristics across workloads.

Hiro Tanaka: I like the “default no” framing.
On kernel side: any change to kernel selection / autotune / fused ops should be auto High risk. Even a tiny diff can shift perf or correctness (esp across H100 vs A10). If we backport, we need at least:
- microbench on the affected kernel path
- perf-canary full suite on A100 + H100 (or at minimum one datacenter SKU + one edge SKU)
- explicit note in release notes “kernel hot path changed”

Sofia Ivanova: +1 for scheduler/policy: anything touching queueing, admission, backpressure, load-shedding, or batching heuristics should be High. Also: if a fix changes fairness/latency tradeoffs, it needs explicit sign-off and a known-issue/mitigation note.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE perspective: for LTS we should treat perf regressions as reliability. Eligibility for Sev2 perf backports should be tied to a threshold.
Proposal: if p95 latency regresses >3% OR throughput drops >5% on canonical workloads vs the LTS baseline, that’s Sev2 eligible. But we need a waiver process because benches are noisy.

Allison Grant: On thresholds: I’m ok with that as a starting point, but call out “must reproduce on dedicated runners” + “2/3 runs” to reduce false positives. Also we should require the perf report artifact attached to the backport PR.

Sahana Iyer: Testing/gates: can we be explicit so engineers don’t guess?
For any LTS backport PR:
- unit + integration suite on release branch CI
- deterministic build check (same inputs => same artifact hash) (we have this partly done)
- smoke test deploy in staging env with pinned model set
For Med/High risk:
- scheduler regression suite (release branch)
- perf-canary gate summary attached (and reviewed)
For High risk:
- 2 GPUs minimum (A100 + H100) OR documented exception with rationale

Mateo Alvarez: Agree.
On approvals, I want clear owners so no one is surprised during patch week.
My take:
- Security: Security sponsor (Aisha/Hanae) + Release Eng + component owner (kernel/scheduler/runtime)
- Sev1: Runtime TL (Noah or delegate) + Release Eng + component owner + SRE (Sean/Rafael) for rollout risk
- Sev2: Component owner + Release Eng; SRE required if perf related; Runtime TL optional unless High risk

Noah Patel: That’s close. I’d like one more constraint: no backport unless the fix is already in main and has baked for at least 48h (unless security emergency). Keeps us from “inventing” on the LTS branch.

Connor O'Brien: +1. We can encode as a checklist item: “Fix merged to main + commit SHA referenced” and “>=48h bake” (exception only for security).

Hiro Tanaka: Also: “minimal diff” requirement. If the PR is bigger than it needs to be, we bounce it back to mainline cleanup.
Maybe a hard rule: if it changes more than X files or includes unrelated formatting, it’s not backportable.

Sofia Ivanova: Not sure we need a hard number (people will game it), but we can say: “cherry-pick cleanly preferred; conflict resolution must be surgical; any rewrite is High risk”.

Sean Gallagher: Mandatory rollback guidance for High risk backports. Even if it’s “rollback to previous patch” we should document steps + expected data plane behavior.

Allison Grant: +1 and for perf-related changes, require “before/after” numbers in the PR description (p50/p95 + throughput) even if it’s just canary output.

Sahana Iyer: How do we capture this in GitHub workflow? Labels + templates?

Connor O'Brien: Plan:
- Labels: `backport-candidate`, `lts-approved`, `lts-risk:low|med|high`, `lts-type:security|sev1|sev2`, `lts-perf-impact`.
- Backport PR template sections:
  - Eligibility (security/sev1/sev2) + incident/ticket link
  - Risk score + rationale
  - Test evidence (CI links)
  - Perf evidence (perf-canary link + summary)
  - Approvals checklist

Mateo Alvarez: Make sure “lts-approved” is only applied by Release Eng after approvals are actually collected.

Noah Patel: One more: Sev2 correctness (not perf) — what’s the bar? I’d say must be user-visible wrong output (not “we prefer different wording”). Think: tool calling schema breaks, structured output invalid JSON, tokenization mismatch, etc.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also: kernel numerical correctness bugs qualify even if no crash.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler correctness: starvation / deadlock / pathological queueing qualifies.

Connor O'Brien: Summarizing decision (tell me if anything is off):
1) Eligible for LTS backport:
- Security: CVE/advisory/credible vuln (exception to bake requirement allowed)
- Sev1: crash, data corruption, cannot serve, major incident
- Sev2: customer-impacting correctness; perf regression beyond threshold (p95 >3% or throughput >5% on canon suite, reproduced)
2) Not eligible by default: new features, refactors, optimizations not tied to Sev1/Sev2 regression
3) Preconditions: merged to main + references commit SHA; >=48h bake unless security emergency
4) Risk rubric:
- High = kernels/autotune/defaults, scheduler/admission/backpressure/batching heuristics, KV/memory/threading, config default changes
- Med/Low as discussed based on hot path + blast radius
5) Mandatory testing:
- Always: unit+integration on release branch CI, deterministic build check, staging smoke
- Med/High: scheduler regression suite + perf-canary gate artifact reviewed
- High: perf-canary on 2 GPU SKUs (A100+H100 preferred) or exception w/ documented waiver + SRE sign-off
6) Approvals:
- Security: Security sponsor + Release Eng + component owner
- Sev1: Runtime TL + Release Eng + component owner + SRE
- Sev2 correctness: component owner + Release Eng (Runtime TL if High risk)
- Sev2 perf: component owner + Release Eng + SRE

Mateo Alvarez: Looks good. Add: “waivers are rare” + waiver must be written in PR w/ explicit risk acceptance.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: And waivers should be time-boxed (e.g., must rerun perf on next available runner).

Sahana Iyer: I’ll update the CI checklist doc and PR template to reflect this. Connor, can you own labels + who can apply what?

Connor O'Brien: Yep. I’ll take action items:
- [ ] create labels + permissions
- [ ] backport PR template
- [ ] short doc page that Support/CS can point to when customers ask “why didn’t you backport X?”

Noah Patel: Great. Once the doc exists, I’ll pin it in #eng-runtime and we’ll refer back when exceptions come up.

Hiro Tanaka: Please include a line: “Kernel changes in LTS are exceptional” so we don’t normalize it.

Connor O'Brien: Will do.

title: #eng-runtime: 1740862203
preview:
Omar El-Sayed: seeing something weird in the new cache hit-rate counters on the perf canary. under continuous batching, `kv_cache_hit_total` looks too high (like > requests) and the miss-reason breakdown doesn’t add up. anyone else seeing it?

Samir Qureshi: yeah I saw that in staging last night. looked like hits got attributed to the “wrong” request when multiple requests share a single prefill batch.

Julian Moore: +1. the scheduler emits a single prefill op for N reqs (continuous batch), then we split decode steps per req. if instrumentation is attached to the prefill op, it’s gonna smear across reqs.

Omar El-Sayed: exactly. I dug a bit and the counter increment is currently happening at `PrefillDone` with whatever `active_request_ctx` happens to be “first” in the batch.

Omar El-Sayed: repro: send 2 prompts, A repeated (should be hit), B unique (miss). in a batched prefill, both get a hit.

Omar El-Sayed: logs from a local run (instrumentation debug build):
```text
I0303 18:12:41.102 runtime/cache_metrics.rs:211] prefill_complete batch_id=cb_7 reqs=[r_201,r_202] prefix_reuse_tokens=384
I0303 18:12:41.102 runtime/cache_metrics.rs:226] emit kv_cache_hit_total+1 route=/v1/chat/completions req_id=r_201 model=llama-3.1-70b
I0303 18:12:41.102 runtime/cache_metrics.rs:226] emit kv_cache_hit_total+1 route=/v1/chat/completions req_id=r_201 model=llama-3.1-70b
# ^ second increment should have been miss for r_202 (no reuse)
```

Samir Qureshi: :facepalm: yep. we’re using `batch_ctx.primary_req` there.

Noah Patel: what’s the intended semantic per ADR? hit/miss at request-level, not batch-level. if batch contains mixed reuse, we need partial hit semantics per request.

Omar El-Sayed: ADR says: per request, `hit` iff reused_prefill_tokens > 0 (and reuse attributed to that request). for partial hits, we track reuse histogram separately, but hit-rate counter should still reflect per-request.

Julian Moore: we have the per-request mapping already in scheduler: each req has `prefill_reused_tokens` and `prefill_total_tokens` after the batch finishes. we just don’t plumb it down to metrics.

Sofia Ivanova: instrumentation should happen when we finalize request accounting, not at batch prefill completion. otherwise any batch-level aggregation will break. can we move emission to `RequestAccounting::finalize(req)`?

Omar El-Sayed: that’s my current plan.

Omar El-Sayed: quick sketch (pseudo):
```rust
for req in batch.reqs {
  let reused = req.cache.reused_prefill_tokens;
  let prefill = req.cache.prefill_tokens;
  if reused > 0 { kv_cache_hit_total.inc(labels(req)); }
  else { kv_cache_miss_total.inc(labels(req, reason=req.cache.miss_reason)); }
  kv_cache_reuse_tokens.observe(reused, labels(req));
  kv_cache_prefill_tokens.observe(prefill, labels(req));
}
```

Samir Qureshi: +1. we need to be careful not to double count when a request gets rescheduled across batches (e.g. partial prefill). but for continuous batch, each req should have one finalized prefill accounting.

Noah Patel: also check streaming retry path doesn’t re-finalize the same request (we just fixed double counting there in another PR).

Omar El-Sayed: yeah, I’ll guard with `if req.metrics_emitted.swap(true) { return; }` like we did for stream retries.

Marcus Reed: can you post perf numbers once you move it? request-finalize runs on hot path too, but should be minimal.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. it’s just counters/histograms with sampling on. no extra allocations.

Julian Moore: can you include a negative test for mixed batch? something like: A repeated prompt twice + B unique, force them into same prefill batch.

Samir Qureshi: how do we force them into the same batch deterministically in tests?

Julian Moore: integration test can set `continuous_batching.max_delay_ms=50` and send the two requests within the window. we already have a test harness for “batch of 2”.

Omar El-Sayed: ok I’ll add an integration test variant.

Omar El-Sayed: opening PR now with the fix.

buildkite-bot: Omar El-Sayed opened PR https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18433 "Fix cache hit attribution under continuous batching" (status: running)

Sofia Ivanova: please also update comment/docs in cache_metrics.rs. someone will otherwise “optimize” it back to batch-level emission.

Omar El-Sayed: will do.

Marcus Reed: once green, I’ll run it through perf-canary with continuous batching enabled. expecting hit-rate to drop (correctly) for mixed workloads.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks for catching. this is exactly the kind of bug that makes dashboards look great :)

Samir Qureshi: reviewer note: ensure miss_reason stays stable. if reused==0 but reason is “unknown”, we should still emit miss_total with `reason=unknown`.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. I’m preserving miss_reason from request ctx (`capacity`, `ttl_expired`, `policy_disabled`, `hash_mismatch`, `version_mismatch`, else `unknown`).

Noah Patel: also confirm the per-request labels (route/model/version/deployment_mode) come from the request, not batch. batch may have mixed routes in theory.

Omar El-Sayed: good call. labels pulled from req ctx.

buildkite-bot: PR https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18433 build #1 passed (unit, lint)

Omar El-Sayed: pushed integration test `cache_metrics_mixed_batch_attribution` and it fails on main, passes on PR.

Omar El-Sayed: snippet from test assertion:
```text
expected: hits=1 misses=1
main:     hits=2 misses=0
pr:       hits=1 misses=1
```

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: approve from scheduling side.

Marcus Reed: ran perf-canary (continuous batching on, sampling default). no measurable p95 regression (<0.2%). hit-rate graphs now match expectation (mixed workload no longer all-hit).

Noah Patel: approved. please add a short note to release notes / known limitations: pre-18433 dashboards likely overreported hits for batched prefills.

Omar El-Sayed: added note in PR description + will ping Allison/observability to annotate dashboard.

Mateo Alvarez: ship it. also let’s make sure the dashboard alert rules don’t start firing when this rolls out (hit-rate will “drop” but it’s a correction).

Omar El-Sayed: agreed. I’ll post in #eng-sre once merged so they can mute/adjust alerts during rollout.

Samir Qureshi: nice catch. thanks all.

Sofia Ivanova: please also update comment/docs in cache_metrics.rs. someone will otherwise “optimize” it back to batch-level emission.

Omar El-Sayed: will do.

Marcus Reed: once green, I’ll run it through perf-canary with continuous batching enabled. expecting hit-rate to drop (correctly) for mixed workloads.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks for catching. this is exactly the kind of bug that makes dashboards look great :)

Samir Qureshi: reviewer note: ensure miss_reason stays stable. if reused==0 but reason is “unknown”, we should still emit miss_total with `reason=unknown`.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. I’m preserving miss_reason from request ctx (`capacity`, `ttl_expired`, `policy_disabled`, `hash_mismatch`, `version_mismatch`, else `unknown`).

Noah Patel: also confirm the per-request labels (route/model/version/deployment_mode) come from the request, not batch. batch may have mixed routes in theory.

Omar El-Sayed: good call. labels pulled from req ctx.

buildkite-bot: PR https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18433 build #1 passed (unit, lint)

Omar El-Sayed: pushed integration test `cache_metrics_mixed_batch_attribution` and it fails on main, passes on PR.

Omar El-Sayed: snippet from test assertion:
```text
expected: hits=1 misses=1
main:     hits=2 misses=0
pr:       hits=1 misses=1
```

Sofia Ivanova: :thumbsup: approve from scheduling side.

Marcus Reed: ran perf-canary (continuous batching on, sampling default). no measurable p95 regression (<0.2%). hit-rate graphs now match expectation (mixed workload no longer all-hit).

Noah Patel: approved. please add a short note to release notes / known limitations: pre-18433 dashboards likely overreported hits for batched prefills.

Omar El-Sayed: added note in PR description + will ping Allison/observability to annotate dashboard.

Mateo Alvarez: ship it. also let’s make sure the dashboard alert rules don’t start firing when this rolls out (hit-rate will “drop” but it’s a correction).

Omar El-Sayed: agreed. I’ll post in #eng-sre once merged so they can mute/adjust alerts during rollout.

Samir Qureshi: nice catch. thanks all.

title: #eng-runtime: 1740651107
preview:
Samir Qureshi: Quick gut check on the proposed `wasted_prefill` metric in the PR. What exactly are we calling “wasted” here?

Samir Qureshi: If it’s “prefill tokens that didn’t contribute to subsequent decode due to misses/evictions” that seems super decoding-strategy dependent (spec decode / chunked prefill / streaming cancels etc). Worried we’ll ship a metric nobody trusts.

github-bot: PR opened: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/18424 (add-wasted-prefill-metric)

Omar El-Sayed: Yeah +1 to tightening the definition. The intent (at least from the runtime side) was: prefill work we did that never produced any user-visible output, i.e. request dies before we decode even 1 token.

Omar El-Sayed: Not “didn’t get reused by a later request” (that’s a different thing, and hard to attribute).

Noah Patel: Agree those are two different concepts.

Noah Patel: “wasted because cache churn” is really “recomputed prefill” (miss due to eviction / ttl / policy disabled) — that should be covered by miss_reason + missed_tokens, not by a new metric name.

Samir Qureshi: So wasted_prefill := request-level abort before decode starts?

Omar El-Sayed: More or less. Proposed definition I have in my notes:
- we emit `prefill_tokens_total` always
- we emit `wasted_prefill_tokens_total` only when `output_tokens == 0` AND termination_reason in {client_cancel, deadline_exceeded, internal_error}

Omar El-Sayed: That makes it comparable across decoding strategies because it’s not about acceptance/rejection, it’s simply “did we ever get to output.”

Sofia Ivanova: Important nuance with scheduler: we can finish prefill, enqueue decode, then request gets dropped (client disconnect) before first decode step. That’s still “no output”. Works w/ Omar’s definition.

Sofia Ivanova: But pls be explicit it’s *after prefill completes*. Otherwise partial-prefill aborts are messy (we may not know the exact token count depending on kernel path).

Julian Moore: +1. In continuous batching we can start prefill, then the batch gets reshaped, and if the request is cancelled mid-prefill we may only have partial counts.

Julian Moore: I’d rather define it in terms of already-existing counters: `prefill_tokens_total` (increment as we go) and then “wasted” computed at end if `decode_steps == 0`.

Marcus Reed: From perf side, I want something that doesn’t require extra per-token branching.

Marcus Reed: If we already have `prefill_tokens_total` and `completion_tokens_total`, then wasted can be:

Marcus Reed: ```
if completion_tokens == 0 && prefill_tokens > 0 && request_final_status != "success":
  wasted_prefill_tokens += prefill_tokens
```

Marcus Reed: That’s 1 extra counter increment in the end-of-request path.

Samir Qureshi: What about “success” responses that are 0 tokens (e.g., stop sequences / tool call immediately)?

Noah Patel: Those are not “wasted” IMO. If user asked for tool call and model returns tool invocation w/ zero generation tokens (or extremely short), prefill still “contributed” to the decision.

Noah Patel: Also some customers use `max_tokens=0` for embeddings-ish patterns (lol). We shouldn’t flag that as wasted.

Omar El-Sayed: Yep. That’s why I like filtering on non-success terminal statuses.

Omar El-Sayed: Another question: speculative decoding.

Omar El-Sayed: Spec decode can “decode” draft tokens that get rejected, but that’s decode-side compute; doesn’t change prefill semantics. We just care whether we produced *any* committed tokens / response payload.

Sofia Ivanova: For chat streaming: do we treat “we streamed 1 token then client disconnects” as not wasted? It’s not 0 output.

Omar El-Sayed: Correct, not wasted in that metric. It might still be “unproductive” from a cost POV, but that’s a different metric.

Julian Moore: +1 keep it narrow or it becomes a grab bag.

Samir Qureshi: Ok. Then the phrase “due to misses/evictions” in the sprint doc is misleading.

Noah Patel: I’d rewrite that. Misses/evictions should be covered by:
- `prefix_cache_miss_reason{reason=evicted|ttl_expired|policy_disabled|hash_mismatch|version_mismatch}`
- and something like `prefix_cache_missed_tokens_total` (or histogram)

Noah Patel: That tells you how much prefill you had to redo because cache didn’t help.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. If we ship “wasted prefill” and it’s actually “cache churn,” PMs + SRE will interpret it wrong.

Mateo Alvarez: Let’s keep wasted_prefill = “request aborted before producing output”. Then separately we talk about “recompute due to eviction” etc.

Mateo Alvarez: Also please document expected baseline. Streaming workloads will have non-trivial client_cancel; don’t page on it.

Marcus Reed: Another gotcha: retries. We had that double-counting bug on stream retries last week.

Marcus Reed: If a request gets retried internally and the customer sees one response, do we count wasted for the failed attempt?

Omar El-Sayed: Good call. For now:
- count wasted per *attempt* with `attempt_outcome!=success` AND `completion_tokens==0`
- BUT ensure the retry path sets attempt_outcome properly so we don’t double increment in the same attempt.

Omar El-Sayed: We already have the “attempt_id” in tracing; we are not putting that in metrics labels, but internally the request object should only flush metrics once.

Samir Qureshi: That’s fine. As long as metric isn’t a proxy for “customer saw errors”.

Omar El-Sayed: Exactly. It’s a compute-efficiency signal.

Sofia Ivanova: Please also decide whether we want this as counter vs histogram.

Sofia Ivanova: Counter is fine but histogram might help (are we wasting 50 tokens vs 5k tokens).

Omar El-Sayed: I was thinking histogram for tokens (bucketed) + a counter for events.

Marcus Reed: Histogram is okay if buckets are fixed + low cardinality. Don’t want per-route surprise.

Omar El-Sayed: Yup. Use the same token buckets we’re using elsewhere (prompt length buckets). No extra labels beyond model/region/deployment mode + allowed route tag.

Julian Moore: So summary definition text we can paste into the PR:

Julian Moore: “Wasted prefill tokens are prefill-stage tokens computed for requests that terminate without producing any completion tokens (completion_tokens==0) and with a non-success termination reason (client_cancel, timeout, internal_error). This metric is intended to capture prefill compute that produces no user-visible output. It is not a cache reuse metric; cache churn is captured via miss_reason and missed_tokens.”

Omar El-Sayed: Perfect. I’ll update the PR description + metric comment and sync with Nadia on naming.

Samir Qureshi: Works for me. I’ll add a unit test case: cancel after prefill => increments wasted; cancel after 1 streamed token => does not.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: ship it with that wording. Also add a one-liner in the internal docs so SRE doesn’t interpret spikes as “cache busted.”

Omar El-Sayed: Will do. Action items:
- [Omar] Update PR + metric docstring w/ definition + non-goals
- [Samir] Add tests for cancel/no-output vs cancel/after-output
- [Marcus] sanity check overhead w/ histogram enabled in canary

title: #eng-runtime: 1740217732
preview:
Hiro Tanaka: heads up on the mixed FP8/INT8 path. we need stronger kernel selection guardrails before we let more profiles ship.

Hiro Tanaka: current issue: profile sets weights=int8 (per-channel) but activations fp8. on H100 this *usually* picks the right fused GEMM, but on L40S we’re sometimes falling into a “fallback” kernel that technically runs but blows up decode latency and occasionally OOMs at long ctx.

Noah Patel: +1. the selector is treating fp8 act as “supports flash-attn v3” but the int8 weight path doesn’t always have the same max seqlen coverage.

Grace Kim: can we just hard-cap context length for that combo? like `max_supported_seq_len` per kernel family and force policy fallback to baseline if request ctx > cap.

Omar El-Sayed: careful: cap needs to consider *prefill+decode* and KV cache growth. we saw: prefill at 12k ok, then decode pushes memory over on batch merges.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler angle: continuous batching merges requests w/ different remaining ctx. if we only check request.seq_len at admission time, we can still exceed cap when merged (effective max across batch).

Hiro Tanaka: exactly. guardrail should be in runtime kernel picker at execution time (per batch), not just policy.

Jordan Lee: determinism requirement too: if we sometimes pick mixed path and sometimes fallback based on transient batch composition, customers will see nondeterministic performance. do we want “hard rule”: if any request in batch exceeds cap => entire batch uses baseline?

Noah Patel: that’s the simplest. and it’s deterministic *given the batch* but the batch itself depends on timing.

Sofia Ivanova: right, but we can make it deterministic at request-level by *pinning* execution mode at admission.

Sofia Ivanova: e.g. once request admitted, set `execution_quant_mode = MIXED_FP8_INT8` only if request.max_ctx <= cap. then batching only groups compatible modes together.

Omar El-Sayed: that would avoid the KV cache blowups too, because we won’t merge mixed-mode with baseline-mode.

Grace Kim: ok but what’s the cap? per GPU and per kernel implementation.

Hiro Tanaka: for now, propose conservative caps:
- H100: mixed FP8/INT8 supported up to 16k context (prefill), decode ok
- L40S: mixed FP8/INT8 supported up to 8k context; above that force baseline (fp16/bf16) or pure int8 path if available

Jonas Weber: from perf suite: L40S mixed path p95 decode regresses badly >8k anyway. at 12k it’s ~1.7x baseline decode time due to non-fused epilogue path. so cap at 8k seems fine.

Noah Patel: what about A100? we still have some dedicated clusters.

Hiro Tanaka: A100 doesn’t have fp8 tensor cores (at least not in our stack), so mixed fp8/int8 is “not supported” and should never be selected. today it’s blocked by `gpu_capability >= 90` but we should make that explicit.

Jordan Lee: can we encode this in profile metadata so policy engine blocks incompatible enables? (profile declares supported_gpu_skus + max_context)

Hiro Tanaka: yes, but i still want runtime check because profiles can be wrong / future kernels change.

Grace Kim: +1 defense in depth.

Mateo Alvarez: joining late. what’s the concrete change request?

Hiro Tanaka: 1) add guardrail table in runtime kernel selector for quant mode combos
2) expose `max_supported_seq_len` (and maybe `max_prefill_seq_len` vs `max_total_seq_len`) per combo+gpu_sku_class
3) admission-time pinning so batching doesn’t mix modes / exceed caps

Mateo Alvarez: do it. also add observability: count how often we fallback due to seq_len cap (separate from general incompatibility).

perf-canary-bot: perf-canary run #4821 flagged regression: scenario `mixed_fp8_int8_decode_longctx` L40S p95 +62% vs baseline. threshold 20%. link: https://perf-canary.redwood/run/4821

Jonas Weber: yep that’s the same. in that scenario, requests are 10k-12k ctx. if we cap at 8k it should stop selecting mixed.

Noah Patel: question: do we cap based on `prompt_tokens` only or `prompt_tokens + max_new_tokens`?

Omar El-Sayed: KV cache depends on total (prompt + generated). we should cap based on `effective_context = prompt + max_new_tokens`.

Sofia Ivanova: but `max_new_tokens` is user provided and often huge. for streaming, it’s a ceiling. if we use the ceiling, we’ll disable mixed for a ton of traffic.

Hiro Tanaka: maybe two caps:
- “safe cap” on prompt (prefill) for kernel availability
- “memory cap” on total for KV cache

Hiro Tanaka: for v1 guardrail, i’d do: if `prompt_tokens > cap_prefill` => baseline.

Omar El-Sayed: but our observed OOM was decode-phase KV. prompt was fine.

Noah Patel: we can compute an upper bound memory estimate from `prompt_tokens` + min(`max_new_tokens`, `server_decode_cap`). we already cap decode at server side for some tiers.

Mateo Alvarez: keep it simple but correct. if it’s too conservative, that’s acceptable for v1. correctness > savings.

Jordan Lee: policy side: should return a compatibility warning when user enables profile and has routes configured for >8k context.

Grace Kim: console can show “this profile supports up to 8k on L40S; requests above will run baseline”.

Sofia Ivanova: runtime admission pinning still needs to know the cap per deployment’s gpu class. in hosted, route can span multiple gpu types across regions.

Noah Patel: right. for hosted multi-region, policy resolves per region anyway. runtime only sees resolved region+pool. so admission pinning can use local pool gpu class.

Hiro Tanaka: implementation sketch:

Hiro Tanaka: 
```cpp
// pseudo
QuantMode resolve_quant_mode(Request r) {
  if (!feature_flags.quant_profiles_enabled) return BASELINE;
  auto mode = r.route_resolved_quant_mode; // from policy
  if (mode == MIXED_FP8_INT8) {
    auto caps = caps_table[gpu_class][mode];
    if (r.prompt_tokens > caps.max_prompt_tokens) return BASELINE;
    if (estimate_kv(r) > caps.max_kv_bytes) return BASELINE;
  }
  return mode;
}

// scheduler groups by resolved_quant_mode
```

Grace Kim: + add a specific reason enum for fallback
`fallback_reason=SEQ_LEN_CAP` / `fallback_reason=KV_ESTIMATE`.

Mateo Alvarez: pls also include `kernel_family_selected` tag for traces. we’re debugging these too often blind.

Jonas Weber: and perf-canary scenario should assert fallback rate ~100% for long ctx when mixed is enabled on L40S.

Jordan Lee: determinism note: if we use kv estimate based on *batch size* it becomes nondeterministic. kv estimate should be per-request only.

Omar El-Sayed: agreed. per-request kv estimate (worst case) is fine. don’t use current batch shape.

Sofia Ivanova: one more: prompt_tokens sometimes unknown until tokenization. we have it at request admission though.

Noah Patel: yep, after tokenize. for embeddings it’s different but mixed path only for generation.

Hiro Tanaka: action items:
- Hiro: PR to add caps table + fallback reasons + trace tags
- Sofia/Noah: admission pinning + batching separation by quant mode
- Jonas: update perf-canary scenarios/thresholds + add assertion on fallback reason

Mateo Alvarez: sounds good. ping me for review on selector changes.

Grace Kim: i’ll drop the proposed caps in the thread once i pull latest microbench.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: thanks all

Hiro Tanaka: implementation sketch:

Hiro Tanaka: 
```cpp
// pseudo
QuantMode resolve_quant_mode(Request r) {
  if (!feature_flags.quant_profiles_enabled) return BASELINE;
  auto mode = r.route_resolved_quant_mode; // from policy
  if (mode == MIXED_FP8_INT8) {
    auto caps = caps_table[gpu_class][mode];
    if (r.prompt_tokens > caps.max_prompt_tokens) return BASELINE;
    if (estimate_kv(r) > caps.max_kv_bytes) return BASELINE;
  }
  return mode;
}

// scheduler groups by resolved_quant_mode
```

Grace Kim: + add a specific reason enum for fallback
`fallback_reason=SEQ_LEN_CAP` / `fallback_reason=KV_ESTIMATE`.

Mateo Alvarez: pls also include `kernel_family_selected` tag for traces. we’re debugging these too often blind.

Jonas Weber: and perf-canary scenario should assert fallback rate ~100% for long ctx when mixed is enabled on L40S.

Jordan Lee: determinism note: if we use kv estimate based on *batch size* it becomes nondeterministic. kv estimate should be per-request only.

Omar El-Sayed: agreed. per-request kv estimate (worst case) is fine. don’t use current batch shape.

Sofia Ivanova: one more: prompt_tokens sometimes unknown until tokenization. we have it at request admission though.

Noah Patel: yep, after tokenize. for embeddings it’s different but mixed path only for generation.

Hiro Tanaka: action items:
- Hiro: PR to add caps table + fallback reasons + trace tags
- Sofia/Noah: admission pinning + batching separation by quant mode
- Jonas: update perf-canary scenarios/thresholds + add assertion on fallback reason

Mateo Alvarez: sounds good. ping me for review on selector changes.

Grace Kim: i’ll drop the proposed caps in the thread once i pull latest microbench.

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: thanks all

title: #eng-runtime: 1740144420
preview:
Hiro Tanaka: seeing a decode p95 regression when enabling the new INT8 profile on llama3.1-70b-instruct variant x1. baseline p95 decode ~38ms/token, INT8 profile jumps to ~52ms/token on A100-80GB. encode looks fine. anyone else seeing this?

Noah Patel: which profile id? and what runtime build?

Hiro Tanaka: profile ref `qpl:llama3.1-70b-x1:int8_w8a16:2025-02-19.3` (aka v3). runtime `runtime@b7f4c2a` from yesterday. route is pinned, no other changes.

Sofia Ivanova: is it only when continuous batching kicks in? (i.e. queue depth > 1)

Hiro Tanaka: happens even at qd=1. perf suite run `decode_only` shows it.

Omar El-Sayed: any chance we’re invalidating KV cache layout due to different pack/unpack? INT8 weights shouldn’t touch KV but we do some alignment changes for fused kernels.

Hiro Tanaka: grabbed nsight systems trace. looks like we’re not taking the fused attention kernel on decode; falling back to a slower path.

Noah Patel: can you paste the kernel names / top hotspots?

Hiro Tanaka: yep. from nsys summary (decode step, batch=1, seqlen=4096, new_tokens=64):
```
Time(%)  Total Time (ns)  Instances  Avg (ns)    Name
46.8     2.13e+09         64         3.33e+07    void at::native::scaled_dot_product_attention...
21.4     9.74e+08         128        7.61e+06    void cutlass::Kernel<...> gemm_f16...
12.2     5.55e+08         64         8.67e+06    void kv_cache::gather_scatter<<<...>>>
7.9      3.61e+08         64         5.64e+06    void layernorm<<<...>>>
```
Baseline run shows attention as `flash_fwd_splitkv` at ~22% instead of 46%.

Grace Kim: that suggests kernel selection is flipping. do we have a guard that disables flash attention when int8 weights enabled? (i vaguely remember a conservative check in pr-18479)

Hiro Tanaka: I checked logs with `RUNTIME_LOG_LEVEL=debug`, seeing:
`kernel_select: flash_attn=DISABLED reason=unsupported_combo(w8a16,int8_matmul)`
That seems… wrong? We shouldn’t tie attention kernel to weight GEMM mode.

Noah Patel: yeah, attention is independent. the guard might be too broad: “if quantized then disable flash” type thing.

Sofia Ivanova: is this only for v3 profile? what about v2?

Hiro Tanaka: v2 profile (`...:2025-02-12.1`) is fine. v3 is the one that regresses.

Jonas Weber: perf-canary run from last night flagged same thing on A100, didn’t repro on H100. (reacted :eyes:)

Omar El-Sayed: H100 path uses different attention backend; might hide it.

Grace Kim: v3 changed anything besides weights? e.g. activation dtype or smoothquant params? if it flipped act to fp16 vs bf16 it could trip a capability check.

Hiro Tanaka: profile says w8a16, activations fp16 (same as v2), but it also sets `preferred_attention_backend=auto` and `max_context=8192`. v2 had `max_context=4096`.

Noah Patel: ah. the “max_context” might be driving a conservative attention selection. maybe we disable flash if max_context > 4096 for that model family.

Sofia Ivanova: but your trace is seqlen=4096. even if max_context=8192, we should still allow flash at runtime when actual seqlen <= supported.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. selection should be based on actual request constraints, not profile max. unless the profile forces a different KV layout that breaks flash.

Omar El-Sayed: KV layout: for >4k we allocate a different paged cache config (larger page size) if `max_context` is higher. That might cause the flash kernel to be disabled because it can’t handle that page size.

Hiro Tanaka: interesting. I saw `kv_cache.page_size=256` in the v3 run, baseline/v2 is `page_size=128`.

Omar El-Sayed: there we go. our flash attention decode kernel only supports page_size=128 right now (and maybe 64). For 256 we fallback to sdpa.

Grace Kim: can we clamp page_size based on actual seqlen? or change heuristic: don’t bump page_size just because profile advertises max_context.

Noah Patel: we should also surface this as an explicit warning: “flash disabled due to kv_page_size=256” not “unsupported_combo(w8a16,int8_matmul)”. that error message is misleading.

Hiro Tanaka: agree. quick experiment: forced `KV_PAGE_SIZE=128` env var with v3 profile, decode p95 goes back to ~39ms/token.

Selene Huang: any risk changing page size affects correctness? (i.e. address calculations)

Omar El-Sayed: shouldn’t. page size is perf/memory tradeoff. correctness is same, just different indexing. But we need to ensure we don’t OOM for 8k contexts if we keep 128.

Jonas Weber: for A100 80GB, 70B @ 8k is already tight; bumping page size was meant to reduce metadata overhead. maybe the right fix is “page_size=128 if seqlen<=4096 else 256” (or pick dynamically at allocation).

Sofia Ivanova: + make it deterministic across replicas (same request -> same selection). but that’s fine if it’s purely a function of request seqlen + model.

Mateo Alvarez: action items:
1) fix kernel_select log reason (don’t blame int8)
2) adjust KV cache page sizing heuristic to be request-aware so we don’t disable flash for 4k runs
3) add perf-canary coverage specifically for “profile max_context > request seqlen” case

Hiro Tanaka: I can open a quick PR for #1/#2 unless Omar wants it.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll take #2 (KV sizing). Hiro can do #1 (log reason + guard). Let’s coordinate to avoid conflicts.

Noah Patel: can someone paste the relevant config code path? I think it’s in `kv_cache/config.rs`.

Omar El-Sayed: it’s in `KvCacheConfig::from_profile()` currently:
```
if profile.max_context >= 8192 {
  page_size = 256;
}
```
We should move it to `KvCacheConfig::from_request(model, seqlen, profile)`.

Grace Kim: also update compatibility matrix docs? customers might ask why “INT8 made it slower” when it’s actually KV paging.

Hiro Tanaka: ok, I’ll add a metric tag too: `attention_backend=flash|sdpa` and `kv_page_size` in runtime span logs (if not already). helps with future triage.

Jonas Weber: please do. perf suite can then assert “flash still enabled” on the 4k decode benchmark.

Mateo Alvarez: keep me in the loop, this is close to beta. we should consider temporarily blocking v3 publication for A100 until fix lands (or mark as caveat).

Hiro Tanaka: ack. I’ll ping Arjun/Jonas separately about gating v3 for A100 with a caveat in the profile metadata.

Sofia Ivanova: thanks. also: can we add a regression test that enabling a profile with max_context=8192 doesn’t change decode kernel path for seqlen=4096? feels like exactly what we missed.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. I’ll add a small unit/integration test around KV config selection.

Hiro Tanaka: cool. posting numbers for record:
- baseline: 38.4ms/token p95 decode
- v3 default: 52.1ms/token p95 decode (flash disabled)
- v3 + KV_PAGE_SIZE=128: 39.2ms/token p95 decode

Mateo Alvarez: (reacted :thumbsup:)

title: #eng-ml: 1740068833
preview:
Arjun Desai: early deltas from the profile library runs are in :bar_chart: (tl;dr good savings, one red flag)

Arjun Desai: model: `rwi/llama3-70b-instruct` (hosted A100-80G + H100-80G)
profile candidate: `qpl_llama3_70b_int8_w8a8_v0.4` (smoothquant-ish; calibration: `calset-2025-02-10-mix1`)

Arjun Desai: aggregate vs baseline (FP16) on our standard prompt sets (decode len ~256, context p50 ~1.5k):
- cost/token: -17.8% (A100), -14.9% (H100)
- p95 latency: +2.3% (A100), +1.1% (H100)
- throughput: +9–12% depending on batch mix

Arjun Desai: BUT: we’re seeing a quality regression on one prompt set: `tools-json-strict-v3`.
- pass@1 strict JSON validity drops ~3.6pp (baseline 96.4% -> 92.8%)
- failure mode: more trailing commas / missing closing brace, mostly on multi-tool outputs

Jonas Weber: thx. is that outside Selene’s thresholds for that suite?

Selene Huang: yeah, for `tools-json-strict-v3` our guardrail is max -1.0pp on JSON validity (and no new failure mode clusters). -3.6pp is a hard block if it holds up.

Sana Farid: do we know n + CI? want to sanity check significance.

Arjun Desai: n=2,000 prompts sampled (same seed as last week). bootstrap CI for delta is [-4.4pp, -2.7pp]. so… not noise.

Karthik Iyer: is regression isolated to int8 profile? do we see it in fp8 mix?

Arjun Desai: tested `qpl_llama3_70b_fp8_w_fp8_a_fp16_v0.2` quickly (smaller run, n=600) and JSON validity delta was -0.4pp (within noise). savings are smaller though (~-8% cost/token).

Jonas Weber: do we have per-length breakdown? tool calls often have longer contexts.

Arjun Desai: yep, it’s worse at context >4k.
- <=4k: -2.1pp
- >4k: -6.8pp
Also seeing more “partial tool args” where it ends early.

Mateo Alvarez: (runtime side) any chance this is not “quality” but a truncation / max_tokens interaction? e.g. different tokenization length under quant or earlier EOS?

Arjun Desai: good q. we controlled max_tokens and verified output token counts. it’s not systematically shorter; it’s more malformed JSON.

Olivia Grant: is this the suite that uses the strict validator + whitespace normalization? could be formatting drift.

Arjun Desai: validator is strict JSON parse after whitespace normalization only. commas/braces are real errors.

Selene Huang: this is exactly the sort of suite we need to be conservative on. if customers enable quant on tool routes and JSON breaks, it’s a bad beta story.

Karthik Iyer: hypotheses?

Arjun Desai: 2 likely causes:
1) calibration set under-represents tool-calling / JSON-heavy outputs (more natural language)
2) w8a8 is pushing logit noise on punctuation tokens (comma/brace) esp in long context

Jonas Weber: we should probably mark v0.4 as “do not publish” until we fix. do we have an alternative int8 variant? maybe w8a16?

Arjun Desai: we tried `w8a16` earlier; savings drop to ~-10% but quality was fine on tools suite (n=800, delta -0.7pp). not fully rerun yet.

Sana Farid: if we’re going to compare variants, we should run paired eval w/ same prompt sample and do McNemar on the binary pass/fail, not just bootstrap. can help decide quickly.

Selene Huang: +1. also cluster failures—if it’s the same 50 prompts failing, that’s less scary than broad drift.

Arjun Desai: failures are broad. not just a handful.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: ok action-wise: don’t publish `v0.4` to the library. treat as blocked. can we spin a `v0.5` with tool-heavy calibration? and in parallel rerun `w8a16` as a fallback candidate.

Arjun Desai: yep. I can:
- build `calset-2025-02-20-toolsmix` (pull from `tools-json-strict-v3` + some customer-ish tool traces)
- recalibrate int8 and rerun full suite tonight

Jonas Weber: pls also run perf suite on A100/H100 for the w8a16 candidate so we have something shippable if v0.5 slips.

Mateo Alvarez: if we end up recommending w8a16 for tool routes, we should make sure runtime selection + compatibility matrix can express “tooling-safe” caveat. otherwise customers will pick the biggest savings one.

Selene Huang: we can add a caveat field: “Not recommended for tool/structured output routes” for now, but I’d prefer we just not publish until it passes.

Olivia Grant: for beta gating: should we add `tools-json-strict-v3` as a REQUIRED suite for any profile marked “general purpose”? currently it’s required for chat models but not embeddings obviously.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: yes. make it required for any instruct/chat model profile, especially anything intended for route-level enablement. Arjun/Selene pls sync and update the eval gating doc.

Sana Farid: also note this in the weekly results sheet w/ a clear “BLOCKED” label so no one accidentally flips “recommended”.

Arjun Desai: will do. summary:
- `qpl_llama3_70b_int8_w8a8_v0.4` blocked due to -3.6pp JSON validity on `tools-json-strict-v3` (worse >4k ctx)
- rerun w/ tool-heavy calibration as `v0.5`
- rerun `w8a16` as potential beta-safe option

Jonas Weber: thx. pls drop links to artifacts when you have them.

Arjun Desai: will post results + failure examples tomorrow AM.

Selene Huang: also please include a few raw failing outputs in the thread (redacted). helps when we explain to PM why we blocked.

Arjun Desai: yup.

Mateo Alvarez: (cc runtime) once you have candidate v0.5, we can do a quick perf-canary run to confirm no weird decode latency spikes.

Arjun Desai: great, I’ll ping you when the new artifacts land.

title: #eng-runtime: 1739824101
preview:
Omar El-Sayed: Need to settle the hit/miss definitions today so Samir can finish counters + Nadia can lock schema. Main confusion: KV cache hit vs prefix cache hit, and what “partial reuse” means esp multi-turn chat.

Samir Qureshi: +1. Implementation wise I can emit (a) tokens reused (skipped compute) and (b) tokens eligible. But people keep asking for “request hit rate” which gets weird w partial.

Noah Patel: Let’s be super explicit about numerator/denominator and *stage*. KV cache is about reuse across turns within a session (or same route) at decode-time, but prefix cache is prefill-time reuse of prompt prefix. If we mix the two it’ll be garbage.

Sofia Ivanova: Also continuous batching complicates attribution. Prefill for multiple reqs in one batch, we need per-request counts. We can’t have “hit” mean “batch-level cache was warm”.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposal:
- Prefix cache metrics computed at prefill stage.
- KV cache metrics computed during decode stage.
But question: for prefill, is “hit” measured as prompt tokens reused (prefix reuse depth) / prompt tokens requested? Or only eligible tokens (excluding system prompt? tool schema? etc.)

Nadia Rahman: From schema/cardinality side: want stable semantic: “eligible_prefill_tokens” is whatever runtime attempted to prefill (after canonicalization / truncation). Don’t try to carve out “system vs user” in metrics.

Allison Grant: For dashboards, SRE wants to see “why did GPU cost spike”: prefill compute went up because prefix reuse went down OR decode got more expensive because KV reuse went down. So split is good.

Samir Qureshi: KV cache doesn’t really “reuse tokens” in decode; it reuses KV tensors for prior context tokens so each new token decode avoids recomputing attention over previous tokens. So the natural unit is “context tokens served from cache” per decode step.

Noah Patel: Right: in decode step t, context length = L. If KV cache has entries for L tokens, it’s a hit. If not, we pay a prefill-like recompute / rebuild KV.

Omar El-Sayed: This is where partial comes in: you can have KV for first K tokens but missing tail due to eviction/version mismatch. Do we count partial as hit?

Sofia Ivanova: We should. Otherwise every eviction makes hit-rate binary and noisy. But we need two metrics: “full hit” vs “reuse fraction”.

Marcus Reed: Perf view: I care about “wasted prefill” too: tokens we prefilled that didn’t contribute to subsequent decode reuse (due to miss/evict). Partial reuse should correlate with wasted prefill.

Allison Grant: For alerting, a single scalar is still useful. But it has to be stable. We can alert on drop in “reused_prefill_tokens / total_prefill_tokens” and separately on “kv_reuse_fraction”.

Nadia Rahman: Naming from standards doc: I’d like token-based ratios, not request-based. Request-based is workload-shape dependent and hard to compare across prompt buckets.

Samir Qureshi: So for prefix cache:
- total_prefill_tokens = prompt tokens we would have computed in prefill
- prefix_reused_tokens = tokens loaded from prefix cache (i.e., skip compute)
- prefix_reuse_ratio = prefix_reused_tokens / total_prefill_tokens
And optional histogram on reuse depth.

Noah Patel: Careful: “total_prefill_tokens” includes tokens that are *not cacheable*? Like if caching disabled for route.

Omar El-Sayed: Good point. Denominator should be “cache_eligible_prefill_tokens”. Then if caching disabled, we don’t tank hit-rate, we emit misses by reason.

Nadia Rahman: Yes. Emit “eligible” and then “miss_reason” on the misses. That’s more actionable.

Allison Grant: +1 please. Otherwise dashboard says 0% hit-rate and oncall spends 2h only to find someone flipped policy off.

Samir Qureshi: OK so prefix cache:
- eligible_prefill_tokens
- reused_prefill_tokens
- missed_prefill_tokens = eligible - reused
and miss_reason breakdown: policy_disabled, ttl_expired, capacity, hash_mismatch, version_mismatch (do we need all?)

Omar El-Sayed: On miss taxonomy: keep it small/stable. I’m fine with those 5.

Sofia Ivanova: Where does “evicted” land? That’s capacity, right? We can have eviction metrics separately.

Noah Patel: For KV cache, define:
- kv_context_tokens_needed: how many context tokens decode expects to have cached (post any truncation)
- kv_context_tokens_present: tokens actually present in KV cache
- kv_reuse_ratio = present / needed
Plus a counter for “kv_miss_event” when present < needed, with miss_reason.

Samir Qureshi: Implementation detail: “needed” can change within a request because of sliding window / max_seq? but our runtime uses fixed context window per request. I can compute per decode step, but that’s heavy.

Marcus Reed: Please don’t do per-step metrics in prod, overhead will be awful. Aggregate per request: evaluate at first decode step only.

Omar El-Sayed: Agree: define KV reuse at boundary between prefill->decode: before first decode token, how many KV entries are already there vs needed for context. That’s the moment that matters for latency spikes.

Noah Patel: This also handles multi-turn chat: turn N starts with some existing cache. We measure reuse at turn start.

Allison Grant: How do we treat streaming where client cancels mid-generation? Does that affect denominators?

Samir Qureshi: For prefix cache, prefill already happened, so it’s counted. For KV reuse, we measure before first decode, so cancellation after doesn’t matter. That’s fine.

Sofia Ivanova: Retries though… gateway can retry streaming on transient. We can double count unless we de-dupe by request_id.

Omar El-Sayed: We are *not* logging raw request_id as a label. But runtime can guard by “only emit once per logical request” using internal attempt counter.

Nadia Rahman: +1. No high-card labels. If you need debugging, annotate a trace span not metric label.

Allison Grant: Exactly. Metrics: low-card. Traces: per-request.

Marcus Reed: So what is “hit-rate” in English in ADR? People will ask.

Noah Patel: I’d write:
- Prefix cache hit-rate: fraction of cache-eligible prefill tokens served from prefix cache.
- KV cache hit-rate (reuse effectiveness): fraction of context tokens already present in KV cache at the start of decoding for a request/turn.
And define “partial hit” explicitly as ratio in [0,1].

Samir Qureshi: Do we still need a boolean “full_hit” metric?

Allison Grant: For dashboards, nice to have “full hit %” as a separate panel. But don’t call it “hit-rate”.

Nadia Rahman: If we do it, make it a counter: full_hit_requests_total and eligible_requests_total, ratio derived in query. But careful with workload mix changes.

Omar El-Sayed: Let’s keep request-level as optional. Primary is token-based. Request-based can be added later if SRE insists.

Sofia Ivanova: Re: multi-turn attribution: if a chat has 10 turns, each turn emits its own prefill metrics and kv reuse metrics. That’s fine.

Noah Patel: One nuance: prefix cache could be “warm” because last turn shares a lot of prefix. But if prompts include timestamps / random IDs, hash_mismatch will dominate. That’s the actionable bit.

Allison Grant: That’s why miss_reason needs hash_mismatch. We’ll point customers at canonicalization.

Marcus Reed: Also “version_mismatch”: model rollout invalidates cached tensors. We need this to catch SUP-3912 style regressions.

Nadia Rahman: OK then.
Action items:
- Omar: update ADR text w token-based definitions + partial semantics + stage boundaries.
- Samir: implement eligible/reused counters and KV reuse ratio as per-turn at first decode.
- Sofia: sanity check in continuous batching path for per-request attribution.
- Allison: dashboard definitions: separate panels for prefix vs KV, avoid single blended “cache hit-rate”.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 to this direction. Keep it simple and stable. Token-based primary metric, request-based optional. Also please add a small “how to interpret” blurb in the internal readme so people stop equating KV and prefix.

Omar El-Sayed: Cool. I’ll post ADR draft in #architecture for final review by EOD. :thumbsup:

Samir Qureshi: I’ll adjust the enum to those 5 miss reasons and wire the denominator as eligible tokens. Will ping if I hit ambiguity around ttl_expired vs capacity.

Allison Grant: When you have the metric names, drop them here so I can start panel queries.

Nadia Rahman: Please use the naming from standards: `cache_prefix_*` and `cache_kv_*` prefixes (and no “hit_rate” suffix for the raw counters).

Omar El-Sayed: Ack. Will align names with Nadia’s doc once I paste the final list.

Sofia Ivanova: Also flag: scheduler can cause locality changes; not a miss reason but shows up as hit drop. Might want a tag for region/pool (low-card) so we can see it.

Nadia Rahman: Region is allowed. Pool is tricky (cardinality). Let’s stick to deployment_mode + region + model + route_id allowlist.

Allison Grant: Works for SRE. Pool is too granular anyway.

Omar El-Sayed: Closing loop: definitions above are the ones we’ll implement unless objections in the next hour.

title: #architecture: 1739450020
preview:
Talia Benson: Kicking off a thread to coordinate the architecture review for route-level quantization enablement + fallback behavior. Need sign-off this week to keep the schema/API/routing work unblocked.

Talia Benson: ADR draft is here (WIP): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-0137-Route-level-quantization-profile-enablement

Talia Benson: Proposed review slot: Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. Attendees: Platform (Priya/Ethan/Mina/Ravi/Jordan), Runtime (Mateo/Noah), Console (Dylan/Chloe), Security/RBAC (Naomi/Marcus), PM (Marissa). If you can’t make it, pls comment on the ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. Main thing from Product side: must be explicitly opt-in per route for v1, no global “tenant default enables everything.” Also needs “easy rollback” story that Support can execute.

Priya Natarajan: Thanks Talia. Read through ADR quickly. Overall direction seems right. I want to make sure we’re crisp on:
- what exactly is stored in route config (profile_id vs profile_ref vs alias)
- where compatibility is enforced (policy engine vs runtime)
- deterministic resolution (same request config => same profile)

Ethan Park: I can make Thu.
Question: ADR says “route references a profile ref string” and examples show both `recommended` and a versioned id. We should avoid anything that can change under customer feet.

Mina Haddad: +1. If we allow `recommended` alias, then “recommended” moving to a new version changes behavior without an explicit config write. That’s basically hidden rollout.

Ravi Menon: Agree. My suggestion: v1 should only allow explicit versioned `quant_profile_id` (immutable). If we need an alias later, treat it as a separate field with “pinning” semantics.

Jordan Lee: Determinism hat on: any alias that’s resolved at request time based on “latest” breaks determinism unless we snapshot it into resolved policy at write time.

Noah Patel: Runtime side: we can handle either, but if policy engine resolves to an explicit immutable id before the request hits the runtime, it’s cleaner. Runtime should never call “what’s the newest profile” mid-request.

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Runtime should consume resolved result: {enabled, profile_id, reason_if_fallback}. Everything else belongs in control plane.

Dylan Brooks: Console UX question: customers will ask “just use recommended.” But we can still show “recommended” in UI and when they click enable, we write the pinned versioned ID. That gives UX simplicity without the hidden change risk.

Chloe Martin: That’s workable for backend too. Console can call a “preview” endpoint that returns which profile id would be pinned + compatibility warnings.

Naomi Feldman: For audit/compliance: we need the audit event to include the exact pinned profile id and diff. “Enabled recommended” is not sufficient for change control.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also need clarity on who can enable/disable this. Ideally a separate permission (route config write but with elevated capability). At minimum, record actor + route + old/new profile.

Talia Benson: Sounds like we’re converging on: UI may say “recommended” but route config stores immutable `quant_profile_id` (or `default`). I’ll add that as a decision point in ADR.

Priya Natarajan: On compatibility enforcement: ADR currently says policy engine checks model/hardware/seq length and falls back to baseline. Runtime also has guardrails.
We should decide: which layer is authoritative? (I’d like policy engine to be authoritative; runtime should still validate to protect itself.)

Jordan Lee: Agree. Policy eval should produce a single resolved decision, including fallback, to avoid oscillation.
Also: if runtime independently decides to fallback, we must avoid a feedback loop where policy keeps trying and runtime keeps rejecting.

Ravi Menon: +1 on “no oscillation.” I propose we include a `resolution_outcome` in the resolved policy: `APPLIED | FALLBACK_INCOMPATIBLE | DISABLED | KILL_SWITCH`.
Runtime logs it but shouldn’t re-resolve.

Noah Patel: Runtime still has to protect against mismatched artifacts / corrupted profile / unsupported kernel path. In those cases we should fail closed to baseline and emit metrics.
But we should NOT attempt to re-query policy. Just do local fallback.

Mateo Alvarez: Exactly. Runtime local fallback is last resort; also needs a counter so SRE can alert when it happens.

Connor O'Brien: From release/oncall POV: we need a clear kill switch behavior in the ADR:
- global kill switch disables application everywhere (even if route is enabled)
- should be fast propagating (minutes)
- should still keep system healthy (fallback baseline)

Mina Haddad: Routing view: if kill switch is on, route selection should still pick the same model variant; only precision/quant mode changes.
Do not redirect to different region/tier unless we’re already in a failure mode.

Ethan Park: Another detail: caching.
- route config service caching resolved settings is good
- but we must ensure config updates invalidate cache quickly
- and resolved decision is included in request context so runtime doesn’t do extra calls

Chloe Martin: +1. Our control plane endpoint can return “resolved quant settings” with a TTL + etag. Router can cache on route_id + model_variant + region.

Priya Natarajan: Fallback semantics: ADR says “fallback to baseline precision.” Need to define baseline precisely (e.g., model’s default serving mode). Also define what happens if baseline also not available (separate normal routing fallback).

Ravi Menon: Suggest: quantization fallback is orthogonal. If quant profile incompatible/unavailable -> serve with route’s normal model variant at baseline precision. If the model itself is unavailable -> normal routing fallback logic (existing).

Jordan Lee: We should explicitly state order of operations in ADR:
1) route -> model variant selection (existing)
2) resolve quant setting (enabled? which profile?)
3) compatibility check using selected model variant + hardware class + max_context
4) if incompatible -> set resolution outcome to fallback baseline

Noah Patel: +1. Also sequence length constraints: profiles might be validated only up to N ctx. If request ctx > N, we should fallback rather than attempting to run and risking correctness/perf.

Mateo Alvarez: That should be in compatibility matrix (profile declares `max_context_tokens` or similar). Runtime has the true request ctx at execution time; if request exceeds, runtime must fallback.

Marissa Cole: Product requirement: when fallback happens (incompatibility), customer should see it in Console + be able to alert on it. Otherwise they’ll think they’re saving money but they aren’t.

Dylan Brooks: Console can show “enabled but falling back X%” at route level. Need consistent signal name + reason.

Naomi Feldman: On audit: if kill switch overrides, that should also be a logged event with actor (SRE) and reason (incident ticket link maybe).

Connor O'Brien: We can do that. Also would like a “runbook” link in ADR so we’re aligned. I’ll drop the existing rollback doc once it’s merged.

Talia Benson: Action items before review:
- Priya/Jordan/Ravi: propose final decision on alias vs pinned profile id (sounds pinned)
- Noah/Mateo: propose runtime last-resort fallback behavior + metrics
- Naomi/Marcus: confirm RBAC/audit requirements (who can toggle, what gets logged)
- Dylan/Chloe: confirm Console flow: preview -> write pinned id -> show warnings

Priya Natarajan: I’ll add comments in ADR today.

Jordan Lee: I’ll comment with determinism language + avoid alias resolution at request time.

Noah Patel: I’ll add a section to ADR for runtime responsibilities vs policy responsibilities, and the “no re-query” rule.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add security notes. One ask: ensure profile artifacts are integrity checked (signing / checksum) and failures trigger fallback.

Talia Benson: Great. See you Thu unless conflicts. :thumbsup:

- should be fast propagating (minutes)
- should still keep system healthy (fallback baseline)

Mina Haddad: Routing view: if kill switch is on, route selection should still pick the same model variant; only precision/quant mode changes.
Do not redirect to different region/tier unless we’re already in a failure mode.

Ethan Park: Another detail: caching.
- route config service caching resolved settings is good
- but we must ensure config updates invalidate cache quickly
- and resolved decision is included in request context so runtime doesn’t do extra calls

Chloe Martin: +1. Our control plane endpoint can return “resolved quant settings” with a TTL + etag. Router can cache on route_id + model_variant + region.

Priya Natarajan: Fallback semantics: ADR says “fallback to baseline precision.” Need to define baseline precisely (e.g., model’s default serving mode). Also define what happens if baseline also not available (separate normal routing fallback).

Ravi Menon: Suggest: quantization fallback is orthogonal. If quant profile incompatible/unavailable -> serve with route’s normal model variant at baseline precision. If the model itself is unavailable -> normal routing fallback logic (existing).

Jordan Lee: We should explicitly state order of operations in ADR:
1) route -> model variant selection (existing)
2) resolve quant setting (enabled? which profile?)
3) compatibility check using selected model variant + hardware class + max_context
4) if incompatible -> set resolution outcome to fallback baseline

Noah Patel: +1. Also sequence length constraints: profiles might be validated only up to N ctx. If request ctx > N, we should fallback rather than attempting to run and risking correctness/perf.

Mateo Alvarez: That should be in compatibility matrix (profile declares `max_context_tokens` or similar). Runtime has the true request ctx at execution time; if request exceeds, runtime must fallback.

Marissa Cole: Product requirement: when fallback happens (incompatibility), customer should see it in Console + be able to alert on it. Otherwise they’ll think they’re saving money but they aren’t.

Dylan Brooks: Console can show “enabled but falling back X%” at route level. Need consistent signal name + reason.

Naomi Feldman: On audit: if kill switch overrides, that should also be a logged event with actor (SRE) and reason (incident ticket link maybe).

Connor O'Brien: We can do that. Also would like a “runbook” link in ADR so we’re aligned. I’ll drop the existing rollback doc once it’s merged.
